[D02G 1/022] while simultaneously drawing the yarn -- The D02G 1/022 subclass covers processes that produce crimped or curled fibres, filaments, yarns or threads and at the same time draw the yarn to impart latent properties, combining texturing and drawing in a single operation.
[D02G 1/0226] multiple false-twisting -- The D02G 1/0226 subclass covers multiple false-twisting, a process in which a yarn, filament or thread is subjected to successive false twists rather than a single twist to create a crimped or curled structure with latent characteristics. This technique imparts texture, bulk or elasticity without permanently twisting the material.
[D02G 1/0233] with real twist being imparted to the yarn before or after false-twisting -- This CPC subclass D02G 1/0233 pertains to textile processes that give a real twist to yarns, either before or after a false-twisting operation, thereby producing permanently twisted or crimped fibers, filaments, threads, or yarns.
[D02G 1/024] with provision for imparting irregular effects to the yarn -- The CPC subclass D02G 1/024 covers processes for producing crimped or curled fibres, filaments, yarns or threads that include a provision to impart irregular effects to the yarn, such as varying texture or uneven crimp, within the broader textile and yarn-crimping domain.
[D02G 1/0246] at least some of the filaments being simultaneously broken or cut, e.g. by stretching or abrading -- The CPC entry D02G 1/0246 falls under textiles-yarns-crimping and covers processes that produce crimped or curled fibres, filaments, yarns or threads in which at least some filaments are simultaneously broken or cut, for example by stretching, abrading or similar mechanical actions. This method imparts latent characteristics to the material while partially severing the filaments.
[D02G 1/0253] while bonding at least some of the filaments or fibres together -- This subclass covers methods for producing crimped or curled fibres, filaments, yarns or threads in which at least part of the individual elements are bonded together during the crimping process, creating a composite structure with latent properties such as improved elasticity, bulk or resilience. It falls under the textiles hierarchy for yarn crimping.
[D02G 1/026] in the presence of a crimp finish -- The CPC code D02G 1/026 refers to methods of producing crimped or curled fibres, filaments, yarns or threads in which a crimp finish is applied, imparting latent characteristics to the material.
[D02G 1/0266] false-twisting machines -- The entry D02G 1/0266 covers false-twisting machines, equipment that imparts a temporary twist to fibres, filaments, yarns or threads to create a crimped or curled shape and latent characteristics. These machines are used in textile processing to improve texture, elasticity and handling before the twist is removed.
[D02G 1/0273] threading up and starting the false-twisting machine -- This CPC subclass covers the process of threading up and starting a false-twisting machine, a step used to produce crimped or curled fibres, filaments, yarns or threads. It falls under textiles, yarns, and the crimping or curling of fibres, describing the initial set-up of false-twisting equipment.
[D02G 1/028] by twisting or false-twisting at least two filaments, yarns or threads, fixing the twist and separating the filaments, yarns or threads -- It covers a process where at least two filaments, yarns or threads are twisted or false-twisted together, the twist is fixed (for example by heat or chemical treatment), and then the individual elements are separated, producing crimped or curled fibers with latent characteristics.
[D02G 1/0286] characterised by the use of certain filaments, fibres or yarns -- D02G 1/0286 covers processes that produce crimped or curled fibres, filaments, yarns or threads with latent characteristics, where the outcome is defined by the particular type of filament, fibre or yarn used. It is situated in the textile hierarchy under yarns and crimping or curling of fibres.
[D02G 1/0293] composed, at least in part, of natural fibres
[D02G 1/04] Devices for imparting false twist -- A device that imparts a false twist to fibres, filaments, yarns or threads to create a crimped or curled texture, thereby giving the material latent properties such as bulk, elasticity or improved handling, is covered by this entry.
[D02G 1/06] Spindles -- Spindles are rotary devices that twist or otherwise manipulate fibres, filaments, yarns or threads to impart a permanent or latent crimp or curl, giving the material bulk, elasticity or other desired characteristics for later processing. They form part of the D02G 1/06 subclass covering methods of producing crimped or curled textile components.
[D02G 1/08] Rollers -- Rollers are cylindrical tools that contact fibres, filaments, yarns or threads to impart a crimped or curled shape, often by pressure, heat or mechanical deformation. They may be solid, grooved, textured or heated, and are employed in processes that create latent crimp, improve handle, elasticity or bulk of the material. This equipment is classified under the crimping or curling of fibres within the textile hierarchy.
[D02G 1/082] with the periphery of at least one disc -- This subgroup pertains to processes that impart crimp or curl to fibers, filaments, yarns or threads by employing at least one disc-shaped element on the periphery of the material during treatment. The disc may contact or surround the strand to induce the desired latent crimp characteristics. It falls under D02G 1/08 (crimping or curling) within textile technology.
[D02G 1/085] between crossed belts -- D02G 1/085 covers a method of imparting crimp or curl to fibres, filaments, yarns or threads by passing them between two intersecting moving belts. The crossed-belt arrangement bends the material to create latent curvature that can be developed later. This technique is used in textile processing to improve bulk, elasticity and handling of the final product.
[D02G 1/087] between the flanks of rotating discs -- The D02G 1/087 entry covers a method for crimping or curling fibres, filaments, yarns or threads by processing them between the flanks of rotating discs, thereby imparting latent characteristics such as bulk and elasticity. This technique is used in textile manufacturing to produce textured yarns with improved handling and performance.
[D02G 1/10] using knife edges, e.g. heated knife edges, for edge crimping -- The code D02G 1/10 refers to a method of imparting a crimp or curl to fibers, filaments, yarns or threads by passing them over or between knife edges, which may be heated to facilitate deformation. This technique creates latent characteristics in the material for later processing.
[D02G 1/12] using stuffer boxes -- D02G 1/12 covers the use of stuffer boxes in the process of imparting crimp or curl to fibers, filaments, yarns or threads to give them latent properties. Stuffer boxes are devices that temporarily hold the material while it is mechanically worked to create a textured or twisted structure, facilitating later processing or finishing.
[D02G 1/122] introducing the filaments in the stuffer box by means of a fluid jet -- This subclass describes a method of feeding filaments into a stuffer box by means of a fluid jet, a step used to impart crimping or curling before further processing. It falls under the textile yarns category dealing with the production of crimped or curled fibres, filaments, yarns or threads.
[D02G 1/125] including means for monitoring or controlling yarn processing -- D02G 1/125 pertains to methods for crimping or curling fibres, filaments, yarns or threads that incorporate means for monitoring or controlling the yarn-processing operation, such as tension or temperature sensors, to achieve desired latent properties.
[D02G 1/127] including drawing or stretching on the same machine -- The entry covers methods that combine crimping or curling of fibres, filaments, yarns or threads with drawing or stretching on the same machine, allowing simultaneous tension adjustment and latent-property development during production. It describes a single-machine process that imparts both the crimped structure and the desired stretch to the material.
[D02G 1/14] using grooved rollers or gear-wheel-type members -- This classification covers textile processes that crimp or curl fibres, filaments, yarns or threads by employing grooved rollers or gear-wheel-type members, thereby imparting latent characteristics such as elasticity or bulk to the material. It falls under the hierarchy TEXTILES → YARNS → CRIMPING OR CURLING FIBRES, FILAMENTS, THREADS, OR YARNS.
[D02G 1/16] using jets or streams of turbulent gases, e.g. air, steam -- The D02G 1/16 subclass relates to producing crimped or curled fibres, filaments, yarns or threads by directing jets or turbulent streams of gases-such as air or steam-onto the material, thereby imparting latent characteristics that affect its later performance.
[D02G 1/161] yarn crimping air jets -- The D02G 1/161 classification covers air-jet techniques for imparting crimp or curl to yarns, threads, fibres or filaments, creating latent properties such as bulk, elasticity or improved handling. It is used in textile processing to produce textured yarns that enhance fabric performance.
[D02G 1/162] with provision for imparting irregular effects to the yarn -- D02G 1/162 covers methods for producing crimped or curled fibres, filaments, yarns or threads that include a provision to create irregular or uneven effects in the yarn, such as varying the degree of crimp or introducing random distortions to give the yarn latent characteristics.
[D02G 1/164] in the presence of a liquid, e.g. a crimp finish -- D02G 1/164 covers processes for crimping or curling fibres, filaments, yarns or threads by treating them with a liquid, such as a crimp finish, which imparts latent crimp characteristics. The method typically involves applying the liquid to the material before drying or setting, enabling the fibre to retain the desired curl after subsequent processing.
[D02G 1/165] characterised by the use of certain filaments or yarns -- D02G 1/165 covers methods for producing crimped or curled fibres, filaments, yarns or threads that impart latent properties, specifically when the process is defined by the use of particular types of filaments or yarns. It falls under the textiles hierarchy of yarns and crimping or curling, and pertains to techniques that rely on selected filament or yarn characteristics to achieve the desired crimped effect.
[D02G 1/167] including means for monitoring or controlling yarn processing -- D02G 1/167 covers processes for producing crimped or curled fibres, filaments, yarns or threads that incorporate means for monitoring or controlling the yarn-processing operation, such as sensors, feedback systems or automated adjustments. The entry falls under the hierarchy Textiles → Yarns → Crimping or Curling Fibres, Filaments, Threads, or Yarns, and focuses on imparting latent characteristics to the yarn during production.
[D02G 1/168] including drawing or stretching on the same machine -- This classification covers processes that produce crimped or curled fibres, filaments, yarns or threads by applying drawing or stretching operations on the same machine that also forms the crimp, thereby imparting latent properties to the material. It falls under D02G 1/168 in the CPC hierarchy.
[D02G 1/18] by combining fibres, filaments, or yarns, having different shrinkage characteristics -- This subclass covers methods for producing crimped or curled fibres, filaments, threads or yarns by blending together materials that shrink at different rates. The differing shrinkage causes one component to contract more than the other, creating latent crimp or curl in the resulting product.
[D02G 1/20] Combinations of two or more of the above-mentioned operations or devices -- This subclass covers methods or apparatus that jointly perform two or more of the operations for crimping or curling fibers, filaments, yarns or threads, or the devices used for those operations, to impart latent properties. It relates to textile processing where multiple crimping techniques are combined in a single step.
[D02G 1/205] After-treatments for fixing crimp or curl -- D02G 1/205 concerns post-processing steps that stabilise a previously introduced crimp or curl in fibres, filaments, yarns or threads. Techniques include heat-setting, chemical treatments or mechanical actions that lock the deformation so the shape is retained during subsequent use.
[D02G 3/00] Yarns or threads, e.g. fancy yarns -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Yarns or threads characterised by the material or by the materials from which they are made. These aspects are covered by D02G3/02 .  Yarns or threads characterised by constructional features. These aspects are covered by D02G3/22 .  Yarns or threads characterised by the purpose for which they are designed. These aspects are covered by D02G3/44 .  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Shoe laces -> A43C1/00 - Dental floss -> A61C15/04 - Warp knitted yarns -> D04B21/202 - Braided cords, lines or tows -> D04C1/12 - Ropes and strands for ropes -> D07B
[D02G 3/02] Yarns or threads characterised by the material or by the materials from which they are made -- The CPC subclass D02G 3/02 covers yarns or threads whose distinguishing feature is the material or combination of materials used in their construction, such as fibers of specific polymers, metals, composites or blends. It includes fancy yarns where the composition, rather than shape or colour, provides the defining characteristic.
[D02G 3/025] Yarns or threads formed from hair  horse-hair threads  D02G3/14
[D02G 3/04] Blended or other yarns or threads containing components made from different materials -- This CPC subclass D02G 3/04 covers blended or other yarns or threads that incorporate components made from different materials, such as fancy or mixed-material yarns. It falls under the textile hierarchy for crimping or curling fibres, focusing on yarns or threads that combine distinct fibers or filaments.
[D02G 3/042] all components being made from natural material -- The D02G 3/042 subclass designates yarns or threads whose all constituent parts are sourced from natural materials such as cotton, wool, linen or silk, including any natural fibers, binders or finishes. [D02G 3/045] all components being made from artificial or synthetic material -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Blended or other yarns or threads containing components including aramid fibres and similar high performance fibres.
[D02G 3/047] including aramid fibres -- D02G 3/047 covers yarns or threads that are processed by crimping or curling, specifically those incorporating aramid fibres. It falls under the textiles hierarchy for yarns and describes techniques for imparting permanent texture to fancy or specialty yarns.
[D02G 3/06] Threads formed from strip material other than paper -- The code D02G 3/06 classifies threads or yarns that are produced by shaping strip material other than paper into a thread form. It falls under textile yarns involving crimping or curling of fibres, filaments, or threads, and includes specialty or fancy yarns made from such non-paper strips.
[D02G 3/08] Paper yarns or threads -- Paper yarns or threads are textile fibers produced primarily from paper pulp, used similarly to conventional yarns for weaving, knitting, braiding or decorative purposes. The classification covers manufacturing methods, properties and applications of these paper-based strands within the broader field of yarn crimping, curling and specialty fibers.
[D02G 3/10] Yarns or threads formed from collagenous materials, e.g. catgut -- D02G 3/10 refers to yarns or threads that are produced from collagen-based substances such as catgut. These yarns fall under the textile hierarchy of yarns that are crimped or curled, and are used for specialty applications including medical sutures and traditional textile products.
[D02G 3/12] Threads containing metallic filaments or strips -- D02G 3/12 designates yarns or threads that incorporate metallic filaments or thin metal strips. The code applies to products where metal elements are blended with textile fibres to give decorative shine, increased strength, or electrical conductivity, such as fancy metallic yarns used in apparel, upholstery or technical fabrics.
[D02G 3/14] Horse-hair threads -- D02G 3/14 covers horse-hair threads, a specialty yarn classified under crimping or curling fibres, filaments, threads or yarns within the textiles hierarchy. These threads are used as fancy yarns for decorative or functional textile applications.
[D02G 3/16] Yarns or threads made from mineral substances -- D02G 3/16 covers yarns or threads whose primary constituent is a mineral substance rather than organic fibers. These yarns may be formed from glass, metal, ceramic or other inorganic fibers and are used in technical textiles, reinforcement or decorative applications.
[D02G 3/18] from glass or the like -- Yarns or threads produced from glass, glass-based fibers or comparable inorganic materials, typically used in technical textiles, reinforcement applications or decorative fabrics where the properties of glass, such as high tensile strength and heat resistance, are required. This entry classifies such glass-derived yarns within the broader group of crimped or curled fibres and threads.
[D02G 3/182] the glass being present only in part of the structure -- This subclass covers yarns or threads in which glass material is incorporated only in a portion of the structure, such as glass-covered, partially glass-core or glass-spliced yarns used for reinforcement, specialty or decorative purposes. It falls under the broader categories of textiles, yarns and the crimping or curling of fibres.
[D02G 3/185] in the core -- D02G 3/185 covers textile processes that create a core within yarns or threads, typically involving crimping or curling of fibres to form a structured interior. The classification includes methods for producing fancy or specialty yarns where the core provides mechanical or aesthetic properties. It falls under the hierarchy of textiles, yarns, and crimping or curling fibres.
[D02G 3/187] in the sheath -- A process for crimping or curling fibres, filaments, threads or yarns in which the treatment is carried out while the material is enclosed in a protective sheath or covering, such as applying heat, tension or chemicals to the yarn inside the sheath to produce fancy or textured effects.
[D02G 3/20] from asbestos -- The subclass D02G 3/20 covers yarns or threads that are made using asbestos as the primary material. It includes any crimped, curled or otherwise processed asbestos fibres incorporated into fancy or specialty yarns, typically for heat-resistant or insulating textile applications.
[D02G 3/22] Yarns or threads characterised by constructional features -- D02G 3/22 covers yarns or threads that are distinguished by their constructional features, such as the way fibres are twisted, plied or combined, including fancy yarns designed for specific texture or appearance. It falls under the broader categories of textiles, yarns and crimping or curling of fibres.
[D02G 3/24] Bulked yarns or threads, e.g. formed from staple fibre components with different relaxation characteristics -- Bulked yarns or threads are produced by combining staple-fiber components that relax at different rates, creating a thicker, textured structure. This technique enhances bulk and elasticity, often used for decorative or performance-oriented textiles.
[D02G 3/26] with characteristics dependent on the amount or direction of twist -- D02G 3/26 relates to yarns or threads, especially fancy yarns, whose physical or visual properties are governed by the amount or direction of twist applied during manufacture. The classification covers techniques that use twist to create crimp, curl, texture, elasticity or other characteristic effects in the yarn.
[D02G 3/28] Doubled, plied, or cabled threads -- **Definition:** This place covers:  US-A-2004/0065072  US-A-2005/0009430  US-A-3 690 056  **Glossary:** - Doubled yarns: yarns consisting of two or more strands treated as one in the weaving process. The strands are not twisted together. The doubled yarn must not be confused with ply yarns, which are stronger because they are twisted. - Plied or folded yarns: yarns comprising two or more strands or yarns twisted together. They are termed two-ply, three-ply, and so on, according to the number used in their construction. Ply yarns are preferred where durability is all-important. - Cabled or cord yarns: yarns comprising two or more plied yarns twisted together. In general they are constructed by twisting the plied yarns around each other successively in the opposite direction of the preceding twist, i.e. S/Z/S or Z/S/Z. As a result, the opposite twist directions cause the plies to grip each other and maintain adherence. These yarns are hard, rough and strong.
[D02G 3/281] using one drawing frame for two slivers and twisting of the slivers to a single yarn, i.e. spin-twisting  guides having associated actuators with mutual action  D01H13/1658 -- **Definition:** This place covers:  EP-A-0 038 143
[D02G 3/283] using hollow spindles through which one yarn is running -- **Definition:** This place covers:  EP-A-0 593 919
[D02G 3/285] one yarn running over the feeding spool of another yarn -- **Definition:** This place covers:  US-A-6 098 392
[D02G 3/286] with alternatively "S" and "Z" direction of twist, e.g. Self-twist process -- **Definition:** This place covers:  US-A-4 246 750  **Glossary:** - Self-twist yarn: yarn that comprises two or more strands that have areas of twist in what is known as the Z-direction and areas of twist in what is known as the S-direction. The twisted areas in the strands are each separated by non-twisted areas. Torque is trapped in the twisted strand and is otherwise known as residual twist. When two or more twisted strands are brought together, the torque or residual twist causes the strands to twist around each other naturally, without interference, to form a self twisted yarn.
[D02G 3/288] with same direction of twist, but with an amount of twist varying along the thread -- **Definition:** This place covers:  US-A-2 961 010
[D02G 3/30] Crêped or other highly-twisted yarns or threads -- **Glossary:** - Crêped yarns or hard twist: yarns with a number of twist per unit of length higher than ordinary yarn.
[D02G 3/32] Elastic yarns or threads -- D02G 3/32 pertains to elastic yarns or threads that incorporate stretch-providing elements such as elastomeric fibers or a crimped construction, enabling the material to recover after deformation. These yarns are employed in products that require flexibility and resilience, including sports apparel, hosiery, and technical textiles.
[D02G 3/322] using hollow spindles  details of spindles, e.g. bearings  D01H7/86 -- **Definition:** This place covers:  WO-A-2010/064647
[D02G 3/324] using a drawing frame -- **Definition:** This place covers:  EP-A-0 548 474
[D02G 3/326] the elastic properties due to the construction rather than to the use of elastic material -- A yarn or thread whose stretchiness is derived from its construction, such as special twists, crimps, or loops, rather than from any elastic polymer incorporated in the material. The classification covers textile yarns designed to exhibit elastic behaviour through their geometry or processing rather than through elastic additives.
[D02G 3/328] containing elastane -- A subclass of textile yarns that incorporates elastane fibres, providing stretch and recovery properties to the yarn or thread. It falls under crimping or curling fibres, covering fancy or specialty yarns designed for applications requiring elasticity.
[D02G 3/34] Yarns or threads having slubs, knops, spirals, loops, tufts, or other irregular or decorative effects, i.e. effect yarns -- **Definition:** This place covers:  US-A-4 304 092  FR-A-2 315 554  US-A-3 945 189  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Filamentary material with threaded or stringed pearls or beads -> D04D1/04
[D02G 3/343] using hollow spindles  details of spindles, e.g. bearings  D01H7/86 -- The CPC subclass D02G 3/343 covers methods for crimping or curling yarns and threads that employ hollow spindles, including the design and components of such spindles such as bearings. It is applied to the production of decorative or fancy yarns where the spindle's internal cavity and supporting elements affect the fiber's shape and texture.
[D02G 3/346] with coloured effects, i.e. by differential dyeing process -- The subclass D02G 3/346 relates to yarns or threads that display coloured effects created by a differential dyeing process, such as fancy or variegated yarns where different portions are dyed in distinct hues. It is part of the hierarchy covering textiles, yarns, and the crimping or curling of fibres, filaments, threads, or yarns.
[D02G 3/36] Cored or coated yarns or threads -- **Definition:** This place covers:  WO-A-01/98572  DE-U-202006008644  US-A-4 056 924  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Cored yarns or threads with an elastic core and/or sheath -> D02G3/32
[D02G 3/362] using hollow spindles  details of spindles, e.g. bearings  D01H7/86
[D02G 3/365] around which a reel supporting feeding spool rotates -- **Definition:** This place covers:  CH-A-607 246
[D02G 3/367] using a drawing frame -- The D02G 3/367 subclass covers the production of yarns or threads-especially decorative or fancy yarns-by using a drawing frame to align, stretch and thin the fibres, filaments or yarns. This method is part of the crimping or curling techniques applied to textiles to achieve uniformity and desired mechanical properties.
[D02G 3/38] Threads in which fibres, filaments, or yarns are wound with other yarns or filaments, ; -- **Definition:** This place covers:  WO-A-2009037631  EP-A-1 964 952  WO-A-2009/131149
[D02G 3/385] using hollow spindles, e.g. making coverspun yarns -- The subclass D02G 3/385 covers processes for forming yarns or threads that employ hollow spindles, such as the production of coverspun yarns where a core filament is wrapped with additional fibers. It belongs to the textile hierarchy for crimping or curling fibres, threads and yarns.
[D02G 3/40] Yarns in which fibres are united by adhesives -- A subclass of textile yarns that are formed by bonding individual fibres together with adhesive substances rather than by twisting or spinning. It covers yarns such as fancy or decorative threads where glue, resin, or polymer bind the fibres, including the processes for applying the adhesive and the resulting bonded yarn structures.
[D02G 3/402] the adhesive being one component of the yarn, i.e. thermoplastic yarn -- D02G 3/402 relates to yarns or threads that incorporate an adhesive as an integral component of the yarn, commonly a thermoplastic polymer, to bind fibres together and impart crimping or curling in fancy yarns. This classification is used for textile products where the adhesive is applied during yarn formation rather than as a post-treatment.
[D02G 3/404] Yarns or threads coated with polymeric solutions -- The subclass D02G 3/404 covers yarns or threads that have been coated with polymeric solutions, such as adhesives, binders, or protective films, applied to modify surface characteristics, enhance durability, or impart functional properties. It falls within the hierarchy textiles-yarns-crimping or curling fibres, focusing on coated or finished yarns.
[D02G 3/406] where the polymeric solution is removable at a later stage, e.g. by washing -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Yarns or threads coated with polymeric solutions removable at a later stage. The polymeric solution can also be in the form of a yarn, e.g. twisted or blended with other components of the yarn.
[D02G 3/408] Flocked yarns -- Flocked yarns are yarns or threads that have a layer of short fibres, called flock, applied to their surface to create a soft, velvety or plush texture. The flock is typically adhered by heat, adhesive or electrostatic processes, giving the yarn decorative appearance and a tactile feel useful in apparel, home textiles and novelty fabrics.
[D02G 3/42] Chenille threads -- **Definition:** This place covers:  EP-A-0 620 185  DE 3 445 138  **Glossary:** - Chenille yarn: is manufactured by placing short lengths of yarn, called the "pile", between two core yarns and then twisting the yarn together. The edges of these piles then stand at right angles from the yarn's core, giving the chenille both its softness and its characteristic look. Chenille will look different in one direction compared to another, as the fibres catch the light differently.
[D02G 3/44] Yarns or threads characterised by the purpose for which they are designed -- The code D02G 3/44 classifies yarns or threads that are specifically engineered for a particular end-use rather than merely their material or construction. It includes decorative fancy yarns, fire-resistant or conductive threads, medical sutures and other specialty fibers whose purpose defines the product.
[D02G 3/441] Yarns or threads with antistatic, conductive or radiation-shielding properties  electroconductive, anti-static or radiation-shielding filaments  D01F1/09 ,  D01F1/106 ; anti-static fabrics  D03D15/533 ; conducting, insulating or anti-static layers for floor coverings  D06N7/0042 -- D02G 3/441 covers yarns or threads that are designed to be electro-conductive, antistatic or to shield against radiation, including filaments with these functions and related textile products such as anti-static fabrics or conductive layers for floor coverings. It falls under the hierarchy of TEXTILES → YARNS → CRIMPING OR CURLING FIBRES, FILAMENTS, THREADS, OR YARNS.
[D02G 3/442] Cut or abrasion resistant yarns or threads -- The CPC subclass D02G 3/442 relates to yarns or threads that are engineered to resist cutting or abrasion. Such yarns may be reinforced, coated or tightly twisted to provide durability in applications like protective clothing, upholstery, or other industrial textiles.
[D02G 3/443] Heat-resistant, fireproof or flame-retardant yarns or threads  fire- or flame-proof filaments  D01F1/07 ; woven fabrics using heat-resistant or fireproof threads  D03D15/513 -- D02G 3/443 covers yarns or threads that are heat-resistant, fire-proof or flame-retardant, including specially treated filaments and fancy yarns designed for fire-safety applications. It also references related categories for fire-proof filaments (D01F 1/07) and woven fabrics made from such heat-resistant threads (D03D 15/513). This entry falls under the textile hierarchy of yarns, specifically crimping or curling fibres, yarns and threads.
[D02G 3/444] Yarns or threads for use in sports applications  strings for tennis rackets  A63B51/02 -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Yarns or threads for sport applications, including fishing lines and tennis racket strings made up of yarns.
[D02G 3/445] Yarns or threads for use in floor fabrics  floor fabrics  A47G27/00 ; floor coverings on textile basis  D06N7/0005 -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Yarns or threads for use in floor fabrics, e.g. carpets, floor mats, artificial lawn.
[D02G 3/446] Yarns or threads for use in automotive applications -- This CPC subclass D02G 3/446 covers yarns or threads specially formulated for automotive purposes, including materials used in interior upholstery, reinforcement of composites, sealing tapes, and safety components such as airbags. The classification includes any fiber or filament product intended to meet the performance, durability and environmental requirements of vehicle applications.
[D02G 3/447] Yarns or threads for specific use in general industrial applications, e.g. as filters or reinforcement -- D02G 3/447 covers yarns and threads that are manufactured for specialised industrial purposes such as filtration media, reinforcement in composites, or other functional roles. The classification includes any synthetic or natural fibers that have been treated or shaped to provide the properties required for these applications.
[D02G 3/448] Yarns or threads for use in medical applications  D02G3/449  takes precedence; surgical suture materials  A61L17/00 -- D02G 3/448 covers yarns or threads designed for medical use, such as specialty or fancy yarns employed in health-care products. The subclass is subordinate to D02G 3/449, which specifically deals with surgical suture materials, and therefore the latter takes precedence.
[D02G 3/449] Yarns or threads with antibacterial properties  filaments containing agents inhibiting growth of microorganisms  D01F1/103 -- D02G 3/449 covers yarns or threads that are treated or made to possess antibacterial activity, including filaments incorporating substances that inhibit microbial growth. It falls under crimping or curling fibres, yarns and fancy yarns designed to resist bacteria.
[D02G 3/46] Sewing-cottons or the like -- It covers sewing-cottons and similar yarns intended for stitching, including fine or decorative yarns used in garment construction, and is classified under D02G 3/46 within the textile hierarchy of yarns, crimping or curling fibres, and textiles.
[D02G 3/48] Tyre cords -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Textile tyre cords and hybrid tyre cords.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Metal tyre cords -> D07B1/0606
[D02H] WARPING, BEAMING OR LEASING -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Apparatuses and methods for warping, beaming and leasing of warp yarns in preparation of the weaving process.  **Glossary:** - Warping: comprises winding parts of the warp yarns, normally from bobbins, on a warping beam, whereby sequential parts are wound next to each other until the full weaving width is covered. The warping beam is then normally in a next step of warp preparation used for beaming - Beaming: comprises winding the full width of the warp yarns in a single winding operation on the weaving beam (i.e. the beam which is to be placed on the loom). The warp yarns can be wound from a creel or a warping beam. - Leasing: comprises inserting lease cords between the warp yarns to separate groups of warp yarns. - Fibre: a relatively-short, elongated member of natural or artificial material - Filament:: an endless or quasi-endless, elongated member of natural (e.g. silk) or artificial material - Yarn: a unitary assembly of fibres, usually produced by spinning - Thread:: an assembly of yarns or filaments, usually produced by twisting
[D02H 1/00] Creels, i.e. apparatus for supplying a multiplicity of individual threads -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Structures with yarn packages for supplying a multiplicity of individual threads in particular to warping machines or looms.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group (EP-A1-0 732 433):
[D02H 11/00] Methods or apparatus not provided for in the preceding groups, e.g. for cleaning the warp -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods or apparatus not provided for in the groups D02H1/00 - D02H9/00 , e.g. for cleaning the warp.
[D02H 13/00] Details of machines of the preceding groups -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Details of machines of the groups D02H1/00 - D02H11/00 .
[D02H 13/02] Stop motions -- The D02H 13/02 "Stop motions" subclass covers devices and mechanisms that halt or control the movement of yarns in warping, beaming or leasing equipment, such as brakes, stops, or other actuation systems that pause yarn feed to regulate tension and positioning. It is part of the CPC hierarchy under TEXTILES → YARNS → WARPING, BEAMING OR LEASING → Details of machines of the preceding groups.
[D02H 13/04] responsive to breakage, slackness, or excessive tension of threads, with detectors for individual threads or small groups of threads -- D02H 13/04 covers machines and control systems for yarn processing that automatically detect and react to the breakage, slackness, or excessive tension of individual threads or small groups of threads, typically using sensors or detectors to maintain consistent tension during warping, beaming or leasing operations.
[D02H 13/06] mechanical -- D02H 13/06 pertains to the mechanical features of machines employed in warping, beaming or leasing of yarns, including their construction, components and functional details. It falls under the textile yarns hierarchy and addresses the technical aspects of equipment used to prepare yarns for weaving or knitting.
[D02H 13/08] electrical -- D02H 13/08 relates to the electrical aspects of yarn warping, beaming or leasing machines, including motor drives, power supplies, control circuitry and sensors that operate the equipment. It is applied to inventions concerning the wiring, electrical installation and control systems used in these textile processing machines.
[D02H 13/10] responsive to thread-measuring devices, e.g. stopping the machine when required length of warp has been wound -- The D02H 13/10 subclass covers machines that incorporate thread-measuring devices to control warp processing, such as automatically stopping the loom when a preset length of warp has been wound. It pertains to detailed features of warp-forming equipment that respond to measured yarn length, ensuring precise termination of the winding operation.
[D02H 13/12] Variable-speed driving mechanisms -- D02H 13/12 covers the design and operation of variable-speed driving mechanisms for machines that warp or beam yarns, such as warp beams and leasing devices. It includes any adjustable motor, gear or electronic control system that enables the speed of the yarn feed to be varied during operation to match production requirements. This subclass falls under the broader categories of textiles, yarns, and warping or beaming equipment.
[D02H 13/14] controlled automatically by tension in the warp -- This subclass covers machines for warping or beaming yarn that are automatically regulated by measuring and adjusting warp tension. It includes control systems that keep tension constant to ensure uniform yarn feed during textile fabric preparation.
[D02H 13/16] Reeds, combs, or other devices for determining the spacing of threads -- D02H 13/16 covers reeds, combs and similar devices used to set or measure the spacing of yarns on warp beams, ensuring correct thread pitch during warping, beaming or leasing operations in textile production.
[D02H 13/18] with adjustable spacing -- This subclass covers machines for warping, beaming or leasing yarns that incorporate a mechanism to adjust the spacing between yarns, enabling variable pitch for different fabric structures. The feature is used to control yarn placement and density during preparation.
[D02H 13/20] with fixed spacing -- D02H 13/20 refers to machines used in the warping, beaming or leasing of yarns that provide a fixed spacing between the individual yarns. Such equipment keeps the distance between yarns constant during preparation of warp beams, facilitating uniform fabric production.
[D02H 13/22] Tensioning devices -- D02H 13/22 covers tensioning devices employed in the warping, beaming or leasing stages of textile yarn processing. These mechanisms control the pull on warp threads to maintain uniform tension during preparation of fabrics. The class falls under textiles-yarns-warping, beaming or leasing, detailing machines for these operations.
[D02H 13/24] for individual threads -- The CPC code D02H 13/24 belongs to the textiles-yarns hierarchy under warping, beaming or leasing and concerns machines that handle individual threads. It classifies equipment that processes single yarn strands during warping or beaming, detailing mechanisms for feeding, guiding, tensioning or otherwise treating each thread separately. This subclass is used for patents on specialized thread-handling devices.
[D02H 13/26] for threads in warp form -- D02H 13/26 is a CPC subclass under TEXTILES → YARNS → WARPING, BEAMING OR LEASING that relates to machines dealing specifically with threads in warp form, describing apparatus for arranging, feeding, or preparing warp yarns for subsequent weaving operations.
[D02H 13/28] Warp beams -- This subclass covers machines and apparatus that produce, support or handle warp beams-the devices that store and feed warp yarns onto weaving looms. It includes the design, construction and operating features of the beams themselves, as distinct from the broader warping, beaming or leasing equipment that precedes them in the hierarchy.
[D02H 13/30] with flanges -- D02H 13/30 covers the design and construction details of textile warping, beaming or leasing equipment that incorporate flanges, such as the shape, size, attachment method and positioning of these flange components on shafts or frames. The code is used to classify patents describing how flanged elements improve stability, alignment or handling of yarn during the warping process.
[D02H 13/32] adjustable -- D02H 13/32 covers machines used in the warping, beaming or leasing of yarns that incorporate adjustable elements, such as variable-speed drives, tension-control devices or interchangeable guides, enabling the operator to modify warp parameters during operation. The classification refines the broader group on details of such machines.
[D02H 13/34] sectional -- D02H 13/34 classifies textile warping or beaming equipment that is constructed in separate, interchangeable sections, allowing the machine to be assembled, extended, or reconfigured from modular units. The subgroup covers design details of such sectional machines and their supporting structures.
[D02H 13/36] Means for attaching warp to beam -- The subclass D02H 13/36 covers apparatus for fixing warp yarns to a warping beam in textile production. It includes clamps, pins, hydraulic or pneumatic holders and other mechanisms that securely attach the warp to the beam during warping, beaming or leasing operations. These devices ensure proper alignment and tension of the warp before it is woven.
[D02H 13/38] Storage racks for beams -- D02H 13/38 pertains to storage racks for warp or weft beams in textile-manufacturing equipment. It covers the design and arrangement of shelving, frames or other support structures that hold and organize beams during warping, beaming or leasing operations.
[D02H 3/00] Warping machines -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Apparatuses and methods for winding warps normally from a creel on a beam (from where it is, as a next step, normally wound on the warp beam of the loom).
[D02H 3/02] Sectional warpers -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Sectional warpers which normally feature a large drum and feeding warp threads perpendicular to the drum axis from a bobbin creel.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group (EP-A2-1 510 605):
[D02H 3/04] Sample warpers -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Warpers for warping relatively short lengths of warp threads which are used for weaving a sample fabric. These warpers are normally sectional warpers with sideways winding from a bobbin creel.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group (DE-A1-102006023496):
[D02H 5/00] Beaming machines -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Apparatuses and methods for beaming whereby a full width of warp yarns is wound in a single winding operation on the weaving beam (i.e. the beam to be placed in the loom). The warp yarns can be unwound from a creel (i.e. direct beaming) or from a warping beam.
[D02H 5/02] combined with apparatus for sizing or other treatment of warps
[D02H 7/00] Combined warping and beaming machines -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Machines wherein the functions of warping and beaming are united, e.g. by using sectional beaming to create a beam which can be directly used in a weaving machine.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Direct beaming wherein the whole width of warp yarns is beamed at once -> D02H5/00
[D02H 9/00] Leasing -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Apparatuses and methods for inserting lease cords between the warp yarns to separate groups of warp yarns.
[D02H 9/02] Lease rods or bands -- The CPC subclass D02H 9/02 relates to the leasing of rods or bands employed in the warping or beaming of yarns in textile production. It covers methods and apparatus for temporarily attaching, using, and releasing these components as part of the yarn-handling process.
[D02J] FINISHING OR DRESSING OF FILAMENTS, YARNS, THREADS, CORDS, ROPES OR THE LIKE -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Changing the physical characteristics or properties of filaments, yarns, threads, cords, ropes or the like  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Producing crimped or curled fibres, filaments, yarns or threads, giving them latent characteristics -> D02G1/00 - Treatment with liquids, gases or vapours -> D06B - Finishing other than by liquid treatment, of yarns in warp or sheet form -> D06C - Chemical bleaching, dry-cleaning or washing fibres, threads, yarns, fabrics -> D06L - Dyeing or printing textiles -> D06P - Decorating textiles -> D06Q - Treatment during rope-making, apparatus for treating ropes auxiliary to rope-making -> D07B - Details of, or auxiliary devices incorporated in rope-making machines for softening, lubricating or impregnating ropes or component strands thereof -> D07B7/12  **Glossary:** - Bulking: making the filaments and yarns more voluminous by changing the structure - Slub: a thick lumpy knot along the length of the yarn - Drawing: stretching and elongating the yarns or filaments - Draft: the amount to which the yarn or filament is drawn - Fibre: a relatively-short, elongated member of natural or artificial material - Filament: an endless or quasi-endless, elongated member of natural (e.g. silk) or artificial material - Yarn: a unitary assembly of fibres, usually produced by spinning - Thread: an assembly of yarns or filaments, usually produced by twisting
[D02J 1/00] Modifying the structure or properties resulting from a particular structure -- The CPC subclass D02J 1/00 deals with methods that alter the structure or properties of a filament, yarn, thread, cord or rope by modifying its inherent architecture. It includes processes that change mechanical, chemical or physical characteristics through treatments that affect the original configuration.
[D02J 1/02] Bulking, e.g. looping -- This CPC subclass D02J 1/02 lies within textiles-yarns-finishing of filaments, yarns, threads, cords or ropes and describes processes that increase the bulk of these items, such as looping, thereby modifying their structure or resulting properties.
[D02J 1/04] Compacting -- Compacting in the context of textile finishing refers to processes that reduce the bulk or thickness of yarns, fabrics, or similar structures to improve dimensional stability, handleability, or appearance. It typically involves mechanical or thermal treatment that alters the material's internal structure.
[D02J 1/06] Imparting irregularity, e.g. slubbing or other non-uniform features, e.g. high- and low-shrinkage or strengthened and weakened sections -- The D02J 1/06 class covers methods of deliberately creating irregularities in filaments, yarns, threads, cords or ropes, such as slubbing, varying shrinkage or alternating reinforced and weakened zones. These techniques modify the structure or properties of the material to produce non-uniform performance or aesthetic effects.
[D02J 1/08] Interlacing constituent filaments without breakage thereof, e.g. by use of turbulent air streams -- The code D02J 1/08 covers methods for interlacing filament components of yarns, threads or cords without breaking them, typically by applying turbulent air streams to blend the filaments while preserving their continuity. This technique is used in textile finishing to modify the structure or properties of the yarn.
[D02J 11/00] Combinations, not covered by any one of the preceding groups, of processes provided for in such groups -- This CPC subclass D02J 11/00 covers combined finishing or dressing operations applied to yarns, threads, cords, ropes and similar filament products where the combination does not fall within any specific preceding subgroup. It includes hybrid or sequential processes that merge two or more of the listed treatments into a single operation.
[D02J 1/12] Modifying stretch/bulk properties of textured yarns or the like by after-treatment -- D02J 1/12 is a CPC subclass under textiles-yarns-finishing that deals with the treatment of filament or textured yarns. It covers after-treatment processes that modify the stretch or bulk properties of textured yarns using mechanical, thermal, chemical or other methods.
[D02J 1/14] Rendering uniform or evening non-uniform features -- The subclass includes processes that make yarns, filaments or cords uniformly regular by removing or smoothing out variations in diameter, texture, twist or surface appearance; methods such as drawing, calendering, surface treatment or other mechanical or chemical actions that render non-uniform features even.
[D02J 1/16] Rubbing or similar working, e.g. to redistribute or remove fibres -- The CPC sub-group D02J 1/16 covers mechanical operations such as rubbing, brushing or similar working applied to yarns, threads, cords or rope to alter the fiber surface, redistribute fibres or remove loose or excess fibers, thereby modifying the structure or properties of the finished textile.
[D02J 1/18] Separating or spreading -- D02J 1/18 covers methods for separating or spreading yarns, filaments, threads, cords or ropes during finishing operations. Techniques include opening, loosening, fanning or arranging the material to modify its structure or properties before further treatment.
[D02J 1/20] Stressing or stress-relieving, e.g. by vibration or subjection to electrostatic stress or electric discharge -- D02J 1/20 covers processes that apply or remove stress in yarns, filaments, threads, cords or ropes to alter their structure or properties, using techniques such as mechanical vibration, electrostatic forces or electric discharge. These methods are used to improve strength, elasticity or other performance characteristics.
[D02J 1/22] Stretching or tensioning, shrinking or relaxing, e.g. by use of overfeed and underfeed apparatus, or preventing stretch -- The CPC subclass D02J 1/22 relates to processes and devices that stretch or tension, shrink or relax, for example by using over-feed or under-feed apparatus, to alter the structure or properties of textiles such as yarns, threads, cords or ropes. It encompasses methods that modify the material's dimensions or mechanical characteristics after the filament or yarn has been formed.
[D02J 1/221] Preliminary treatments -- This CPC subclass D02J 1/221 covers preliminary treatments applied to yarns, filaments, threads, cords or ropes to modify the structure or properties resulting from a particular structure before further processing, including cleaning, desizing, and other chemical or mechanical actions that alter surface or internal characteristics.
[D02J 1/222] Stretching in a gaseous atmosphere or in a fluid bed -- D02J 1/222 covers the process of stretching yarns, threads, cords, ropes or similar filaments within a gaseous atmosphere or a fluid-bed environment to alter their structure or properties. It falls under the textile hierarchy of yarns, specifically the finishing or dressing of filaments aimed at modifying the resulting material characteristics.
[D02J 1/223] Stretching in a liquid bath -- This subclass deals with processes that stretch yarns, filaments, threads, cords or ropes while they are immersed in a liquid bath, such as water, solvents or chemical solutions. The liquid environment assists deformation, often under tension or heat, to align polymer chains and modify dimensions, thereby improving tensile strength or other properties of the material.
[D02J 1/224] Selection or control of the temperature during stretching -- D02J 1/224 concerns methods for selecting or controlling the temperature during the stretching of filaments, yarns, threads, cords or ropes, a step that modifies their structure or resulting properties. It is part of the CPC group dealing with the finishing or dressing of such textile elements.
[D02J 1/225] Mechanical characteristics of stretching apparatus -- This subgroup covers the design and performance parameters of machinery used to stretch, draw or otherwise elongate yarns, including tension, speed, draft ratio and other mechanical characteristics that influence the resulting filament structure and properties. It is part of the CPC class for finishing or dressing of filaments, yarns, threads, cords, ropes or the like.
[D02J 1/226] Surface characteristics of guiding or stretching organs -- This CPC subclass covers treatments that alter the surface properties of devices used to guide or stretch filaments, yarns, threads, cords, ropes or similar textile elements, such as rollers, guides, stretching plates or other analogous organs. It includes coatings, texturing, lubricating or other modifications that affect friction, grip, wear resistance or other functional characteristics of these guiding or stretching components.
[D02J 1/227] Control of the stretching tension; Localisation of the stretching neck; Draw-pins -- This CPC subclass deals with methods and apparatus for controlling the tension applied while stretching yarns or filamentary materials, including techniques for locating the narrow neck region where deformation occurs and devices such as draw-pins that hold or guide the material during drawing.
[D02J 1/228] Stretching in two or more steps, with or without intermediate steps -- The D02J 1/228 class covers methods of stretching filaments, yarns, threads, cords or ropes in two or more stages, optionally inserting intermediate treatments, to alter the mechanical or dimensional properties of the material. It is a sub-category of finishing or dressing processes aimed at modifying structure or properties resulting from a particular structure.
[D02J 1/229] Relaxing  in combination with crimping  D02G1/20
[D02J 13/00] Heating or cooling the yarn, thread, cord, rope, or the like, not specific to any one of the processes provided for in this subclass
[D02J 13/001] in a tube or vessel -- D02J 13/001 covers the thermal treatment of yarns, threads, cords, ropes or similar filaments when the heating or cooling is carried out inside a tube or other vessel, rather than by the specific processes defined in related subclasses. This subclass falls under the textiles hierarchy for finishing or dressing of such materials.
[D02J 13/003] by contact with at least one stationary surface, e.g. a plate -- The subclass D02J 13/003 covers processes for heating or cooling yarns, threads, cords, ropes or similar filaments by bringing them into contact with at least one stationary surface such as a heated or cooled plate. The method transfers temperature to the material without moving the contacting surface, enabling finishing or property modification.
[D02J 13/005] by contact with at least one rotating roll -- The CPC subclass D02J 13/005 covers finishing processes for yarns, threads, cords or ropes in which the material is heated or cooled by direct contact with at least one rotating roll. It falls under textiles → yarns → finishing or dressing of filaments, yarns, threads, cords, ropes or the like, specifically for thermal treatment not covered by other more specific subclasses.
[D02J 13/006] in a fluid bed  stretching in a fluid bed  D02J1/222
[D02J 13/008] with elimination of fumes -- The subclass D02J 13/008 covers heating or cooling treatments of yarns, threads, cords, ropes or similar textile products in which the process incorporates means to eliminate or suppress fumes generated during the operation, such as ventilation, exhaust systems or other fume-removal techniques.
[D02J 3/00] Modifying the surface -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Apparatuses and methods for treatments of finishing of filaments, yarns, threads, cords, ropes or the like by modifying the surface, e.g. by mechanical action through abrading or indenting the surface.
[D02J 3/02] by abrading, scraping, scuffing, cutting, or nicking -- **Glossary:** - Scuffing: an abrasive wear - Nicking: is an abrasive wear by small cuts
[D02J 3/04] by brushing -- The CPC subclass D02J 3/04 covers processes for modifying the surface of yarns, threads, cords, ropes or similar textile products by brushing. This technique removes loose fibers, smooths the material or creates a uniform texture as part of finishing or dressing operations. It falls under the broader category of textile finishing for filaments and yarns.
[D02J 3/06] by polishing, e.g. glazing, glossing -- D02J 3/06 refers to surface-modifying processes that polish textile filaments, yarns, threads, cords or ropes to produce a glazed or glossy finish, typically by applying a polishing treatment that enhances visual appearance and smoothness.
[D02J 3/08] by compressing, e.g. by calendering or ironing -- D02J 3/08 covers methods that alter the surface of yarns, threads, cords or similar textile elements by applying compressive forces such as calendering or ironing to smooth, flatten or otherwise modify their exterior. It falls under the CPC hierarchy TEXTILES - YARNS - FINISHING OR DRESSING OF FILAMENTS, YARNS, THREADS, CORDS, ROPES OR THE LIKE - Modifying the surface.
[D02J 3/10] by indenting -- D02J 3/10 covers techniques for modifying the surface of yarns, filaments, cords or ropes by creating indentations, such as punching, embossing or de-forming, to alter texture, appearance or functional properties.
[D02J 3/12] by removing projecting ends of fibres -- D02J 3/12 concerns textile surface modification techniques that improve the feel or appearance by cutting away the projecting ends of fibres, such as singeing, shearing or other trimming operations that remove fuzz, hairs or loose fibre tips from yarns, threads, cords or ropes.
[D02J 3/14] by shearing -- The CPC subclass D02J 3/14 covers surface modification of yarns, filaments, threads, cords or ropes using shearing techniques, such as cutting, trimming or shaving the outer layer to change texture, remove protrusions or create a smoother finish. This process falls under the broader category of finishing or dressing of textile filaments and is used to improve functional or aesthetic properties of the material.
[D02J 3/16] by singeing -- The CPC subclass D02J 3/16 covers a surface-treating operation in which yarns or similar textile products are passed through a flame or heated gas to burn off protruding fibers, a process known as singeing. This treatment smooths the filament surface, reduces hairiness, and improves the yarn's appearance and subsequent processing characteristics. It is a specific method of modifying the surface of textiles within the broader category of finishing or dressing.
[D02J 3/18] Treating with particulate, semi-solid, or solid substances, e.g. wax -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Waxing filamentary material as additional measures during package formation -> B65H71/00 - Waxing yarns in twisting or spinning machines -> D01H13/308
[D02J 7/00] Cleaning, e.g. removing dust, loose fibres, charred ends -- The CPC subclass D02J 7/00 pertains to cleaning processes applied to textiles such as yarns, threads, cords or ropes, specifically methods for removing dust, loose fibers, charred ends or similar contaminants.
[D03] WEAVING -- The CPC class D03 pertains to weaving within the broader textiles field. It includes inventions concerning the preparation, interlacing, and joining of warp and weft yarns, as well as the design, operation, and improvement of looms and related equipment for producing woven fabrics.
[D03C] SHEDDING MECHANISMS -- **Definition:** This place covers: all mechanisms and methods involved in the formation of a weaving shed.  **Glossary:** In this place, the following terms or expressions are used with the meaning indicated: The term "shedding" relates to an operation in which warp yarns are being moved apart so that the weft yarn can be inserted (i.e. part of the warp yarns are brought in an upper position and others are brought in a lower position).
[D03C 1/00] Dobbies -- **Definition:** This place covers:  shedding mechanisms with a selectable position of the heald frame.
[D03C 1/005] Electronic dobbies -- **Definition:** This place covers:  dobbies with an electronic selection mechanism.
[D03C 1/02] Single-lift dobbies, i.e. dobbies in which the same draw-knife or equivalent operates for every pick -- **Definition:** This place covers:  dobbies in which the same draw-knife or equivalent operates for every pick. Normally these comprise a mechanically operated selection mechanisms.
[D03C 1/04] Open-shed single-lift dobbies -- An open-shed single-lift dobby is a shedding mechanism used in weaving that lifts a selected set of warp threads in one movement to create an open shed, allowing the weft to pass through with minimal resistance. This type of dobby is classified under textile weaving shedding mechanisms for its ability to produce a wide, unobstructed shed with a single lift action.
[D03C 1/06] Double-lift dobbies, i.e. dobbies in which separate draw-knives or equivalent operate on alternate picks -- **Definition:** This place covers:  dobbies in which separate draw-knives or equivalent operate on alternative picks. Normally these comprise a mechanically operated selection mechanism.
[D03C 1/08] reversible, i.e. correct sheds open automatically when the loom is driven backwards -- This subclass covers shedding mechanisms, particularly dobby-controlled, that automatically re-establish the proper shed when the loom is driven backwards, allowing the correct shed to open without manual adjustment.
[D03C 1/10] Centre-shed dobbies, i.e. shed formed by lifting some threads and lowering the others from central shed-closed position -- **Definition:** This place covers:  dobbies whereby the shedding movement always starts from the middle position (i.e. the position where the warp yarns are flat). Normally these comprise a mechanically operated selection mechanism.
[D03C 11/00] Selvedge shedding mechanisms not forming part of main shedding mechanism -- Selvedge shedding mechanisms not forming part of main shedding mechanism (CPC D03C 11/00) covers auxiliary devices placed at the fabric edges that lift or lower warp yarns to create a shedding action only on the selvedge, separate from the primary shedding system used across the main body of the cloth. These mechanisms are used to control edge formation, prevent fraying, or produce decorative edge effects without influencing the overall weave pattern.
[D03C 1/12] Dobbies employing toothed gearing instead of draw-knives -- This subclass covers dobby looms that use toothed gearing instead of draw-knives to control warp shedding. The gearing replaces the knife's function, allowing precise selection of warp threads for each shed.
[D03C 1/14] Features common to dobbies of different types -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Stop motions acting on a defective operation of shedding mechanisms -> D03D51/46
[D03C 1/142] Leveling mechanisms -- **Definition:** This place covers:  mechanisms to bring all warps to the same level.
[D03C 1/144] linking to the heald frame -- **Definition:** This place covers:  connections between a dobby and heald frame.
[D03C 1/146] Independent drive motor -- **Definition:** This place covers:  shedding mechanisms driven by a separate motor, independent from the main motor.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Shedding mechanisms whereby each heald frame is driven by a separate drive motor -> D03C13/025
[D03C 1/148] Eccentrics -- **Definition:** This place covers:  dobbies comprising eccentrics to drive the shafts.
[D03C 1/16] Arrangements of dobby in relation to loom -- The subclass D03C 1/16 concerns how a dobby mechanism is arranged within a loom, including its placement relative to harnesses, cams, and other shedding components. It covers the spatial configuration and integration of dobby units that control the selective raising of warp threads, influencing shed formation and pattern flexibility.
[D03C 1/18] Knives -- Knives for shedding mechanisms in weaving machines, specifically those used with dobby looms to cut or shape warp or weft threads during the shedding process. The class includes rotary or reciprocating knives that separate yarns, create slits or remove excess material to control shed formation.
[D03C 1/20] Hooks -- Hooks in the context of weaving shedding mechanisms refer to the small mechanical elements used in dobby devices to engage, hold, or release warp threads during the shedding process, enabling precise selection of yarns for each shed. This sub-class covers the design, arrangement and operation of such hooks that form part of the dobby's control system.
[D03C 1/22] Needles -- **Definition:** This place covers:  mechanisms with needles to read information from the pattern cards or chains.
[D03C 1/24] Cylinders -- **Definition:** This place covers:  mechanisms in which cylinders or pattern cards or chains driven by cylinders cooperate with the selection hooks of the dobby.
[D03C 1/26] Facilitating engagement of lifting-hooks with draw-knives -- The D03C 1/26 classification covers devices that aid the connection between lifting hooks and draw-knives in dobby shedding mechanisms of weaving looms. It concerns arrangements that improve the reliability or speed of hook-knife engagement during yarn shedding. This falls under textiles, weaving, shedding mechanisms, and specifically dobby systems.
[D03C 1/28] by creating and then eliminating back-lash between hooks and knives -- This subgroup of D03C 1 defines a shedding-mechanism technique in which a temporary backlash is deliberately created between the loom's hooks and knives and then removed, improving the timing and smoothness of the shedding action while reducing noise and wear. It applies to looms that use a controlled back-lash to achieve precise hook-knife engagement before eliminating it for normal operation.
[D03C 1/30] wherein all the hooks are disengaged from the knives, and selected hooks then re-engaged -- The code D03C 1/30 covers shedding mechanisms for looms where initially all the warp hooks are released from their cutting knives and thereafter only specific hooks are re-latched to control shed formation. This method is used in dobby looms to selectively raise or lower warp threads for pattern weaving.
[D03C 13/00] Shedding mechanisms not otherwise provided for -- **Definition:** This place covers:  shedding mechanisms not otherwise provided for.
[D03C 13/02] with independent drive motors  D03C1/146  and  D03C3/205  take precedence -- **Definition:** This place covers:  shedding mechanisms other than dobbies and jacquards, which are driven by a separate motor, independent from the main motor.
[D03C 13/025] with independent frame drives -- **Definition:** This place covers:  shedding mechanisms wherein each frame is driven by one or more separate independent motors.
[D03C 1/32] wherein all the hooks are engaged with the knives, and unselected hooks then disengaged -- The D03C 1/32 subclass covers shedding mechanisms for dobby looms in which every hook is initially engaged with a corresponding knife and any hooks that are not required for the selected weave are subsequently released, allowing precise control of yarn lifting and lowering during the weaving process.
[D03C 1/34] Arrangements wherein warp threads pass one another at different heights to avoid congestion -- The subclass D03C 1/34 defines weaving configurations in which warp threads are positioned at different vertical levels so that they pass each other without contact, thereby preventing congestion in the shed and improving shedding efficiency. This arrangement is typically employed in dobby-controlled looms.
[D03C 1/36] Card- or chain-saving arrangements, e.g. cross-border dobbies -- This subclass covers devices that secure warp or weft in a loom using cards or chains, especially cross-border dobbies that hold the fabric edges while it is shed. Such arrangements enable stable shedding and improve fabric quality by preventing yarn displacement.
[D03C 15/00] Pattern cards or chains -- **Definition:** This place covers:  jacquard or dobby cards or jacquard chains which store pattern information.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Storing pattern information in electronic form -> D03C19/005
[D03C 15/02] Chains of metal, wood, or similar material with projecting pattern-indicating elements -- Chains made of metal, wood or similar material that carry projecting elements indicating a weave pattern are used as pattern-cards or chains in shedding mechanisms. They translate the pattern design into the sequence of raised and lowered warp threads during weaving.
[D03C 15/04] Cards or paper perforated to indicate pattern -- The D03C 15/04 subclass covers perforated cards or paper sheets that are employed in weaving sheds to dictate the lift pattern of warp threads. These pattern cards, marked with holes or cuts, control the sequence of thread raising and lowering, enabling the loom to produce the intended weave design.
[D03C 15/06] Apparatus for setting pegs in, or removing pegs from, pattern cards -- Apparatus for setting pegs in, or removing pegs from, pattern cards are devices that manipulate the individual pegs on a pattern card used in weaving shedding mechanisms. The equipment typically includes tools or mechanisms that insert, extract, or reposition pegs to program the lift pattern of warp threads.
[D03C 15/08] Apparatus for interconnecting, e.g. by lacing, or disconnecting pattern cards -- The D03C 15/08 class covers devices that join or release pattern cards or chains in shedding mechanisms of weaving looms, typically by lacing or similar means. Such apparatus enables the rapid reconfiguration of the card or chain arrangement to alter shed patterns during textile production.
[D03C 17/00] Card-punching apparatus -- **Definition:** This place covers: apparatuses for punching jacquard or dobby cards or jacquard chains for storing the pattern information.  **Glossary:** In this place, the following terms or expressions are used with the meaning indicated: Jacquard or dobby cards are punched cards containing pattern information and are used for controlling a jacquard meachanism or dobby. Jacquard chains are series of jacquard cards.
[D03C 17/02] manually-controlled, i.e. holes to be punched determined by the operator -- D03C 17/02 covers manually-controlled card-punching devices used in shedding mechanisms for weaving, where the operator decides the exact hole pattern to be punched.
[D03C 17/04] power-operated -- D03C 17/04 covers power-operated card-punching machines that drive the shedding mechanism in weaving looms. These devices use a motor or other power source to punch or move perforated cards that determine which warp threads are lifted, replacing manual operation.
[D03C 17/06] automatic, i.e. design scanned optically or otherwise to control punching apparatus or to control loom jacquard directly -- The D03C 17/06 subclass covers automatic control of card-punching devices by means of scanned designs, such as optical or digital pattern recognition, enabling direct programming of the punch machine or direct linkage to a loom's jacquard system for textile weaving.
[D03C 19/00] Methods or devices concerned with designing or making patterns, not provided for in other groups of this subclass -- **Definition:** This place covers:  miscellaneous methods or devices concerned with designing or making patterns, in particular those methods or devices not covered by D03C15/00 and D03C17/00 .
[D03C 19/005] Electronic -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Storage of pattern information in electronic form.
[D03C 2700/00] Shedding mechanisms -- D03C 2700/00 covers shedding mechanisms, the devices used in weaving machines to separate warp threads so the weft can be inserted. It includes shuttle, rapier, air-jet, water-jet, projectile and other systems that create the rhythmic opening and closing of the shed.
[D03C 2700/01] Shedding mechanisms using heald frames -- The CPC subclass D03C 2700/01 covers shedding mechanisms that use heald frames to raise and lower warp yarns during weaving. Heald frames consist of parallel rods or wires each carrying a heald that can be moved to create the shed, allowing selective yarn selection for pattern formation.
[D03C 2700/0105] Cam-driven heald frame movement -- The code D03C 2700/0105 covers cam-driven movement of the heald frame in weaving machines. It pertains to shedding mechanisms that use a cam to raise or lower the healds, thereby creating the opening and closing of yarn sheds required for warp yarn selection during fabric production.
[D03C 2700/0111] Devices for levelling the heald frames -- It covers apparatus that adjust or equalize the tension of heald frames in a loom to ensure uniform shedding. Such devices may include cams, springs, pneumatic or hydraulic systems that raise, lower or tilt frames during operation. They are used to improve warp shedding accuracy in weaving.
[D03C 2700/0116] Arrangements in which warp threads pass one another at different heights -- It covers weaving arrangements where warp threads cross each other at different vertical levels, allowing the yarns to pass without contact and creating interlaced structures that enable varied patterning and texture in the fabric.
[D03C 2700/0122] Devices for adjusting the geometric and time parameters of shed opening -- Devices that modify the width, shape or timing of the shed opening during weaving, such as adjustable tappets, cams, dobby bars, pneumatic or electronic controls. They enable precise regulation of thread lift, improving pattern accuracy and fabric quality. These mechanisms fall under shedding mechanisms in the textile weaving subclass.
[D03C 2700/0127] Program-controlled heald frame movement -- D03C 2700/0127 designates shedding mechanisms in which the movement of the heald frame is governed by a programmed control system, such as electronic or computer-based commands, allowing precise timing and variable motion patterns during weaving. This class covers devices that adjust the heald frame position according to stored instructions rather than manual or purely mechanical actuation.
[D03C 2700/0133] with mechanical transmission -- This CPC subgroup D03C 2700/0133 falls under textiles-weaving-shedding mechanisms and pertains to shedding devices that employ a mechanical transmission system to raise and lower warp threads. It includes cams, gears, levers or other mechanically linked components that coordinate the shedding sequence.
[D03C 2700/0138] using hooks or lifters -- The CPC subclass D03C 2700/0138 covers shedding mechanisms for weaving that separate warp and weft by employing hooks or lifters. These devices use hooked elements or lifting arms to raise selected warp threads, creating the shed through mechanical action. The method is applied in looms to control yarn movement during fabric formation.
[D03C 2700/0144] Single-lift mechanisms -- Single-lift mechanisms are shedding devices in weaving that raise each warp thread one at a time using a single lifting element such as a cam, rod or pneumatic cylinder. This approach produces simple, low-profile motion suitable for certain shuttleless looms and enables precise control of shedding patterns.
[D03C 2700/015] of closed-shed type -- Closed-shed type shedding mechanisms are weaving systems in which the warp threads are arranged to alternate without opening a full shed, often using fixed harnesses or mechanical devices that keep the shed closed while the weft is inserted. This design simplifies loom operation and is employed for producing specific fabric structures where continuous shedding is required.
[D03C 2700/0155] Double-lift mechanisms -- The CPC subclass D03C 2700/0155 covers double-lift mechanisms used in shedding devices for weaving machines. It falls under TEXTILES → WEAVING → SHEDDING MECHANISMS and defines arrangements where two lifting elements work together to separate warp threads during each pick. This classification is used by the EPO and USPTO to index patents on such dual-lift shedding systems.
[D03C 2700/0161] of negatively-driven type -- D03C 2700/0161 addresses shedding mechanisms of the negatively-driven type used in weaving. In this arrangement the movement that separates warp threads is generated by a negative drive such as a spring or gravity rather than a positive motor. The subclass falls under textiles to weaving to shedding mechanisms.
[D03C 2700/0166] of positively-driven type -- The code D03C 2700/0166 designates shedding mechanisms for weaving that are of the positively-driven type, where each warp thread is actively moved by a drive system rather than by passive or negative actions. This classification covers looms that use mechanical, hydraulic or electronic drives to raise or lower individual yarns to create the shed.
[D03C 2700/0172] Constructional details -- D03C 2700/0172 addresses the constructional details of shedding mechanisms used in textile weaving, focusing on the design and arrangement of components that separate warp yarns during each beat. It includes specifications for rods, cams, levers, and related structural features that enable the required shedding action.
[D03C 2700/0177] Devices for levelling the heald frames -- The code D03C 2700/0177 pertains to devices that level the heald frames in weaving looms, adjusting their vertical position to keep the shedding action uniform and prevent uneven warp tension. These mechanisms belong to the shedding-mechanisms subgroup of textile weaving classifications.
[D03C 2700/0183] Devices for adjusting the geometric and time parameters of shed opening -- Devices for adjusting the geometric and time parameters of shed opening include mechanisms that fine-tune the width, angle and timing of the shed in weaving machines. Examples are adjustable cams, driven levers, pneumatic or hydraulic actuators and electronic controls that modify the opening shape or phase for each pick, thereby improving fabric quality and machine efficiency.
[D03C 2700/0188] Heald frame return devices -- Heald frame return devices are mechanisms on looms that reposition the heald frames after they have been lifted to form a shed, ensuring rapid and precise resetting for the next weaving cycle. They may employ springs, cams or pneumatic systems to achieve reliable return motion in shedding mechanisms.
[D03C 2700/0194] Frame-operating devices for ribbon looms -- Frame-operating devices for ribbon looms are mechanisms that move the loom's frame to create the shedding motion needed for weaving narrow, tape-like fabrics. They control the timing and displacement of the warp and weft layers, allowing precise formation of interlacing patterns in ribbon-type looms. This technology falls under shedding mechanisms within the broader textile weaving classification.
[D03C 2700/14] Heald frame lifting devices -- Heald frame lifting devices are mechanisms in weaving looms that raise and lower the heald frames, which hold the heald wires that separate warp threads during shedding. They provide precise control of yarn selection for each shed, enhancing pattern accuracy and loom speed.
