[D03C 3/00] Jacquards -- **Definition:** This place covers:  shedding mechanisms with healds or other yarn engaging devices, which can be individually selected according to a predetermined weaving pattern.
[D03C 3/02] Single-lift jacquards -- **Definition:** This place covers:  selection mechanisms comprising a single selectable hook for each heald.
[D03C 3/04] of open-shed type -- The classification D03C 3/04 covers open-shed shedding mechanisms used in jacquard looms. It relates to weaving systems where the shed is formed by an open arrangement of warp-lifting devices, allowing individual warp threads to be raised or lowered without a continuously closed shed. This type of mechanism is employed to produce complex patterned fabrics.
[D03C 3/06] Double-lift jacquards -- **Definition:** This place covers:  selection mechanisms comprising two selectable hooks for each heald.
[D03C 3/08] of open-shed type -- D03C 3/08 pertains to open-shed type jacquard shedding mechanisms used in weaving. It describes systems that create a shed by selectively lifting individual warp threads rather than forming a continuously closed shed, enabling the production of fabrics with intricate, variable patterns.
[D03C 3/10] Centre-shed jacquards -- **Definition:** This place covers:  jacquards wherein the shedding movement always starts from the middle position (i.e. where the warp yarns are flat).
[D03C 3/12] Multiple-shed jacquards, i.e. jacquards which move warp threads to several different heights, e.g. for weaving pile fabrics -- The D03C 3/12 subclass covers jacquard shedding devices that raise warp threads to several distinct heights, enabling the production of woven pile fabrics with varied pile levels. These multiple-shed jacquards allow complex patterns by moving selected warps to different positions during each shed.
[D03C 3/14] Jacquards not employing lifting-hooks, e.g. employing knotted cords in conjunction with keyhole slots -- D03C 3/14 covers jacquard shedding mechanisms that do not use lifting hooks, instead employing knotted cords or similar elements engaged in keyhole slots to select warp threads. The arrangement provides individual control of yarns without the conventional hook system, allowing alternative methods of pattern formation in weaving.
[D03C 3/16] Verdol or other jacquards having intermediate power-operated needles between reading needles and lifting-hooks -- This subclass covers Verdol and similar jacquard looms that use intermediate power-operated needles placed between the reading needles and the lifting hooks to control warp shedding. The power-driven needles provide finer, mechanised control of pattern formation than manual lifting mechanisms.
[D03C 3/18] Selvedge jacquards -- The D03C 3/18 subclass covers selvedge jacquard mechanisms used in weaving to produce patterned fabrics along the fabric edge, or selvedge. It relates to shedding devices that control individual warp threads for intricate designs while simultaneously forming a finished selvedge, combining the functions of a jacquard and a selvedge former.
[D03C 3/20] Electrically-operated jacquards -- D03C 3/20 covers electrically-operated jacquards, shedding mechanisms that use electronic control to raise or lower selected warp threads in weaving machines, enabling the production of complex textile patterns.
[D03C 3/205] Independently actuated lifting cords -- **Definition:** This place covers:  jacquards wherein each lifting cord or each group of lifting cords being driven by a separate actuator.
[D03C 3/22] Fluid-operated jacquards -- **Definition:** This place covers:  pneumatic or hydraulic jacquard mechanisms.
[D03C 3/24] Features common to jacquards of different types -- D03C 3/24 covers the structural and functional elements that are shared by various types of jacquard shedding devices used in woven fabrics. It includes common control mechanisms, needle arrangements and programming features that enable individual warp-thread selection across different jacquard systems.
[D03C 3/26] General arrangements of jacquards, or disposition in relation to loom -- D03C 3/26 covers the overall layout and positioning of jacquard mechanisms within weaving equipment, addressing how the jacquard unit is arranged on or integrated with the loom. It includes general configurations that affect the control of individual warp threads for pattern creation.
[D03C 3/28] Pick-finding arrangements -- D03C 3/28 Pick-finding arrangements relates to devices and methods used in Jacquet-type shedding mechanisms to determine the position of selected warp threads during a pick. The arrangements include sensors, optical or mechanical systems that locate and identify the active picks for precise control of pattern formation in woven fabrics.
[D03C 3/30] Arrangements wherein hooks are positively operated to close shed -- The code D03C 3/30 classifies weaving mechanisms that use positively actuated hooks to close the shed, a technique common in jacquard and similar shedding systems where each hook is deliberately engaged to select the required warp threads.
[D03C 3/32] Jacquard driving mechanisms -- This subclass comprises the mechanisms that power a Jacquard loom's selection system, including cams, levers, electronic drives, pneumatic or hydraulic actuators, and other devices that move the hooks, pins or needles to raise or lower individual warp threads during shedding.
[D03C 3/34] Cylinder operating mechanisms -- **Definition:** This place covers:  mechanisms in which cylinders or pattern cards or chains driven by cylinders selectively engage with jacquard hooks.
[D03C 3/36] Griffe operating mechanisms -- **Definition:** This place covers:  knife mechanisms, i.e. mechanisms comprising knives oscillating in opposite directions and which are selectively engagable with the jacquard hooks.
[D03C 3/38] Comber boards -- Comber boards are flat or slotted boards that guide, align and separate fibers during the combing stage of yarn preparation. In shedding mechanisms of Jacquard looms they help control the opening of warp threads and ensure uniform yarn feed.
[D03C 3/40] Constructions of lifting-cords -- D03C 3/40 covers the design and arrangement of cords used to raise or lower warp threads in jacquard weaving machines, including their attachment, tensioning and integration with shedding mechanisms. It addresses various cord materials and configurations that enable selective warp lifting for pattern formation.
[D03C 3/42] Arrangements of lifting-cords -- D03C 3/42 covers the specific layout and routing of lifting cords used to raise warp threads in Jacquard weaving machines. It includes designs that coordinate cord positioning to achieve precise shedding patterns for complex fabrics. The classification applies to mechanisms that control individual warp selection through cord arrangements in textile looms.
[D03C 3/44] Lingoes -- **Glossary:** In this place, the following terms or expressions are used with the meaning indicated: In this group, the term "lingoes" indicates means to tension the lifting cords, normally by elastic force or by their own weight.
[D03C 5/00] Cam or other direct-acting shedding mechanisms, i.e. operating heald frames without intervening power-supplying devices -- **Definition:** This place covers:  mechanisms whereby the movement of the shedding frame is not programmable. There is no selection of the frame, the frame movement is always the same. A typical example is a shedding mechanism where the shaft movement is determined by a cam and the fixed movement determined by said cam is followed for each weft insertion.
[D03C 5/005] Leveling mechanisms -- **Definition:** This place covers:  cam mechanisms or other direct-acting shedding mechanism to bring all warps to the same level.
[D03C 5/02] operated by rotating cams -- The CPC subclass D03C 5/02 relates to shedding mechanisms for looms that are driven directly by rotating cams, operating the heald frames without any intervening power-supplying devices. It falls under the hierarchy textiles → weaving → shedding mechanisms and concerns cam or other direct-acting shedding systems.
[D03C 5/04] Construction or shape of cams -- This subclass covers the design and geometric features of cams that drive the heald frames in direct-acting shedding mechanisms for weaving. It includes the shape of cam lobes, surface contours, and dimensional configurations that determine cam lift and timing.
[D03C 5/06] operated by cam devices other than rotating cams -- D03C 5/06 covers shedding mechanisms for weaving in which heald frames are driven directly by cam devices that are not rotating, such as reciprocating or oscillating cams, allowing warp threads to be lifted and lowered without intermediate power-transmission elements. This falls under the textile shedding-mechanism category.
[D03C 7/00] Leno or similar shedding mechanisms -- **Definition:** This place covers:  mechanisms and methods to make warp yarns cross between the weft insertions.
[D03C 7/005] Combined leno and patterned shedding motion -- **Definition:** This place covers:  shedding motions with both a leno shedding motion and a second "main" shedding motion (e.g. jacquard or dobby) for the warp threads.
[D03C 7/02] Gauze healds -- **Definition:** This place covers:  in particular mechanisms with a doup and heddle.
[D03C 7/04] Mechanisms having discs oscillating about a weftwise axis and having apertures for warp threads -- **Definition:** This place covers:  mechanisms having discs oscillating about a weftwise axis and having apertures for warp threads. These discs oscillate between each or a limited number of picks to avoid twist.
[D03C 7/06] Mechanisms having eyed needles for moving warp threads from side to side of other warp threads -- **Definition:** This place covers:  in particular mechanisms with a comb-like structure.
[D03C 7/08] Devices for twisting warp threads repeatedly in the same direction -- **Definition:** This place covers:  devices for twisting warp threads repeatedly in the same direction. These normally have a bobbin on the disc.
[D03C 9/00] Healds -- **Definition:** This place covers: healds and frames for holding the healds.  **Glossary:** In this place, the following terms or expressions are used with the meaning indicated: Healds are small strips of material, wire or cord with an eyelet through which a warp yarn is threaded. The healds are moved whereby the warp yarns follow this movement. A shed is formed by moving the warp yarns to different positions.
[D03C 9/02] Healds -- Healds are adjustable rods or wires on a loom that hold and raise selected warp threads to form the shed during weaving, enabling precise pattern formation and thread control. They are a key element of shedding mechanisms, allowing individual or grouped yarns to be lifted or lowered in coordination with the reed and harnesses. This component falls under the textile weaving subclass D03C 9/02 in the Cooperative Patent Classification.
[D03C 9/022] anti-buckling -- **Definition:** This place covers:  heddles with a stiffening element in its construction, e.g. longitudinal bend.
[D03C 9/024] Eyelets -- **Definition:** This place covers:  designs of eyelets.
[D03C 9/026] Material used -- The classification covers the material composition of healds, the individual wires or rods that lift and lower warp threads in a shedding mechanism of a loom. It includes metal, alloy, polymer or composite materials used to make the healds and related aspects such as surface treatment.
[D03C 9/04] Metal healds -- Metal healds are rigid longitudinal bars, typically made of steel or aluminum, that are mounted in a loom's harness to control the lifting of warp yarns during shedding. They replace wooden or plastic healds to improve strength, durability, and precision in weaving operations, especially for high-speed or heavyweight fabrics.
[D03C 9/06] Heald frames -- The CPC code D03C 9/06 covers heald frames, the structural components that hold the healds (or reed bars) in a loom and control their movement during the shedding process in weaving. It falls within the hierarchy of Textiles → Weaving → Shedding Mechanisms → Healds, and specifies design, construction, and functional aspects of these frames.
[D03C 9/0608] Construction of frame parts -- The CPC subclass D03C 9/0608 covers inventions relating to the construction of frame parts for healds in shedding mechanisms of weaving machines. It includes the design, arrangement, and joining of metal or composite frames that support the heald wires, focusing on structural aspects and methods of assembly.
[D03C 9/0616] Horizontal upper or lower rods -- D03C 9/0616 covers horizontal rods placed either above or below the heald frames in a shedding mechanism of a weaving loom. These rods support or guide the healds and assist in the upward or downward movement of warp threads during shedding.
[D03C 9/0625] Composition or used material -- The code D03C 9/0625 classifies inventions concerning the composition or material used in healds, the shedding devices that raise and lower warp threads in a weaving loom. It falls under the textile weaving subclass that addresses shedding mechanisms.
[D03C 9/0633] Heald bars or their connection to other frame parts -- D03C 9/0633 covers the design and attachment of heald bars within weaving shedding mechanisms, focusing on how the bars are linked to frame components such as harnesses or looms. It pertains to textile equipment that controls warp thread movement during weaving.
[D03C 9/0641] Distance guides for keeping distance to the next frame -- A distance guide for keeping distance to the next frame is a device attached to a heald that maintains a set spacing between successive harness frames in a loom. By ensuring the frames remain correctly aligned, it helps prevent irregular shedding and improves the uniformity of the woven fabric. This component is classified under CPC D03C 9/0641, relating to shedding mechanisms in weaving.
[D03C 9/065] Side stays -- Side stays are auxiliary members mounted on the sides of healds in weaving shedding mechanisms to keep the healds properly aligned, spaced and stable during operation. They are usually slender rods or plates that extend from the heald body to a frame or guide, reducing lateral movement and vibration.
[D03C 9/0658] Intermediate supports or their connection to other frame parts -- The class D03C 9/0658 covers intermediate supports used in weaving looms to hold or position healds and the methods for connecting these supports to other frame components. It includes brackets, bars, and link mechanisms that stabilize the shedding mechanism while allowing adjustability and alignment of the healds.
[D03C 9/0666] Connection of frame parts  D03C9/0633  and  D03C9/0658  take precedence -- D03C 9/0666 covers the method and structure for joining frame components of a weaving shed's healds, where the connections defined in sub-groups D03C9/0633 and D03C9/0658 are given priority. It deals with the mechanical linkage of the heald frame parts to ensure proper alignment and operation of shedding mechanisms in textile looms.
[D03C 9/0675] Corner connections between horizontal rods and side stays -- The CPC subclass D03C 9/0675 addresses the way horizontal rods (healds) are linked to side stays at the corners of a loom's shed, covering various joining methods such as brackets, bolts, clamps or welding that secure the heald frame to the side rails. This falls under TEXTILES → WEAVING → SHEDDING MECHANISMS → Healds.
[D03C 9/0683] Arrangements or means for the linking to the drive system -- The classification covers devices that connect heald frames to the loom's power source, including shafts, belts, gears, couplings and other linkages that transmit motion to raise or lower the healds for shed formation. It encompasses both rigid and flexible drive arrangements that synchronize heald movement with the overall loom drive system.
[D03C 9/0691] Arrangements of means for damping or noise reduction -- The code D03C 9/0691 refers to specific configurations of healds, the loom components that control warp thread release, that incorporate features to damp vibrations or lower operational noise. It covers design arrangements such as added mass, resilient mounts, or acoustic-absorbing elements intended to reduce the sound generated during the shedding process in weaving machines.
[D03D] WOVEN FABRICS -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Woven fabrics or weaving processes resulting in particular woven fabrics. These aspects are covered by D03D1/00 - D03D27/00 .  Specific types of weaving machines or processes to operate such weaving machines. These aspects are covered by D03D29/00 - D03D47/00 .  Details or features common to different types of weaving machines. These aspects are covered by D03D49/00 and D03D51/00 .  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Devices for warp yarn preparation (i.e. creeling, warping, beaming or leasing) -> D02H - Shedding mechanisms -> D03C - Auxiliary apparatuses combined with or associated with looms -> D03J1/00 - Post treatments of fabrics -> D06B , D06C , D06M
[D03D 1/00] Woven fabrics -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Woven fabrics or weaving methods where the structure or method makes the fabric specifically suitable for a certain application (e.g. a warp and weft yarn density can make a fabric specifically suitable for use as an airbag).
[D03D 1/0005] Woven fabrics for safety belts -- The CPC entry D03D 1/0005 covers woven fabrics specifically designed for use in safety belts. It includes the structure, material composition and manufacturing methods of woven textiles that provide the strength, durability and energy-absorbing characteristics required for personal-protective or restraint applications. This sub-group falls under the broader categories of textiles, weaving and woven fabrics.
[D03D 1/0011] Woven fabrics for labels -- The subclass D03D 1/0011 in the Cooperative Patent Classification designates woven fabrics that are produced for use as labels. It covers fabrics formed by interlaced yarns that carry information, branding or care instructions and can be attached to products or packaging. This entry sits under textiles → weaving → woven fabrics.
[D03D 1/0017] Woven household fabrics -- D03D 1/0017 designates woven household fabrics, covering textile products such as table linens, curtains, bedspreads and other woven items intended for domestic use. These fabrics are made by interlacing yarns in a regular pattern and are classified under the CPC hierarchy textiles - weaving - woven fabrics.
[D03D 1/0023] Mobs or wipes -- The CPC subclass D03D 1/0023 covers woven fabrics that are supplied as mobs or wipes, meaning continuous sheets or pads designed for cleaning, medical or industrial applications. It includes the production, treatment and use of these woven wipes.
[D03D 1/0029] Doormats -- The CPC subclass D03D 1/0029 designates doormats, textile floor coverings placed at entrances to remove dirt from shoes. These items are typically woven or non-woven fabrics made of coir, rubber, synthetic fibers or similar materials and are classified under textiles → weaving → woven fabrics.
[D03D 1/0035] Protective fabrics -- Protective fabrics are woven textiles engineered to resist hazards such as heat, flame, abrasion, chemicals or ballistic impact, often through special fibers, coatings or laminates. They are employed in occupational safety, firefighting, military gear and industrial applications.
[D03D 1/0041] Cut or abrasion resistant -- D03D 1/0041 pertains to woven fabrics that are treated, coated or constructed to provide cut or abrasion resistance, making them suitable for protective clothing, industrial textiles and safety gear. It includes methods, materials and finishes that enhance durability against mechanical wear and falls under the CPC hierarchy of textiles-weaving-woven fabrics.
[D03D 1/0043] for elongated members, i.e. sleeves -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Protective sleeves for elongated members, e.g. for insulating conductive wires or for covering reinforcement wires in clothing. The sleeve can be woven as a tubular fabric or as a flat fabric which is subsequently rolled-up into a tubular shape.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group:
[D03D 1/0047] Camouflage fabrics -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Camouflage fabrics e.g. for visual or thermal camouflage, e.g. with heat reflective properties.
[D03D 1/0052] Antiballistic fabrics -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Antiballistic woven fabrics or woven layers.
[D03D 1/0058] Electromagnetic radiation resistant -- D03D 1/0058 covers woven fabrics that are designed to withstand or block electromagnetic radiation. It includes textiles incorporating conductive or metallic fibers, coatings, or additives that provide shielding against radio, microwave or other electromagnetic frequencies. Such fabrics are used in protective clothing, enclosures and technical applications where radiation resistance is required.
[D03D 1/0064] Noise dampening -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Fabrics designed to dampen noise.
[D03D 1/007] UV radiation protecting -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Fabrics normally used to provide protection from sun or UV-radiation, i.e. sunscreens.
[D03D 1/0076] Photovoltaic fabrics -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Woven fibre structures for solar cells.
[D03D 1/0082] Fabrics for printed circuit boards -- D03D 1/0082 classifies woven textile fabrics employed as substrates for printed circuit boards, where the material provides mechanical support and dielectric insulation for circuit patterning. The entry falls under the hierarchy of TEXTILES → WEAVING → WOVEN FABRICS and addresses fabrics specifically designed for electronic assembly.
[D03D 1/0088] Fabrics having an electronic function -- This CPC subclass D03D 1/0088 covers woven fabrics that incorporate electronic components or properties, such as conductive threads, sensors, or heating elements, enabling functions like signal transmission, data collection, or thermal regulation. It includes fabrics designed for smart clothing, medical monitoring, or protective gear where the textile itself performs an electronic role.
[D03D 1/0094] Belts  D03D1/0005  takes precedence -- **Definition:** This place covers:  All kinds of woven belts not covered by other groups.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Tapes or ribbons -> D03D3/005
[D03D 1/02] Inflatable articles -- The D03D 1/02 subclass concerns woven textile items that can be inflated, ranging from air-filled cushions and mattresses to inflatable safety devices and toys. It covers the design, construction and finishing of such woven fabrics that form an airtight chamber for temporary inflation.
[D03D 1/04] Sack- or bag-like articles -- D03D 1/04 relates to sack- or bag-like articles made from woven fabrics, covering the structure, features and manufacturing of items such as sacks, bags, pouches and similar containers. The subclass falls within the weaving group of woven fabrics in the textiles section of the CPC.
[D03D 1/06] Curtain heading tapes -- The CPC subclass D03D 1/06 pertains to woven fabric strips designed as heading tapes for curtains, i.e., narrow woven tapes that are sewn or attached to the upper edge of a curtain to enable hanging, drawing or pleating. These tapes are typically made of textile fibers and may include reinforcement or decorative elements.
[D03D 1/08] Ladder tapes -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Two tapes that are connected by threads or connecting layers that are designed to hold slats of fabric, wood, plastic or metal that adjust by rotating from an open position to a closed position by allowing slats to overlap. The scope of this group also covers full-width fabrics having tapes or fabrics connected by connecting layers whereby the connecting layers themselves constitute the slats that adjust by rotating from an open position to a closed position by allowing the slats to overlap.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Details of ladder tapes or ladder chains for venetian blinds -> E06B9/382
[D03D 11/00] Double or multi-ply fabrics not otherwise provided for -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Fabrics with multiple woven layers connected by warp or weft yarns, the layers being all of the same size, i.e. the dimension of the layers does not vary in the z-direction (i.e. the direction perpendicular to the warp and weft yarns).  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Multi-ply fabrics in which the length or width direction is limited to a predetermined dimension in order to create a rectangular or other three dimensional shape -> D03D25/005 - Layered products whereby the layers are not connected by warp or weft yarns, i.e. layers connected by lamination -> B32B5/26
[D03D 11/02] Fabrics formed with pockets, tubes, loops, folds, tucks or flaps -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Fabrics consisting of a single tube -> D03D3/02
[D03D 13/00] Woven fabrics characterised by the special disposition of the warp or weft threads, e.g. with curved weft threads, with discontinuous warp threads, with diagonal warp or weft -- This subclass covers woven textiles in which the arrangement of the warp or weft is intentionally altered, such as curving the weft, using discontinuous warp strands, or arranging the threads diagonally. Such configurations modify the fabric's mechanical or aesthetic properties and are disclosed in patents concerning specialized weaving techniques.
[D03D 13/002] With diagonal warps or wefts -- D03D 13/002 classifies woven fabrics whose warp or weft threads are set diagonally, producing a slanted pattern across the material. The diagonal arrangement distinguishes these textiles from conventional orthogonal weaves and is often employed to achieve specific aesthetic or functional properties.
[D03D 13/004] with weave pattern being non-standard or providing special effects -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Fabrics characterised by their weave pattern.  Fabrics having a non-standard weave pattern (standard weave patterns are e.g. plain weave, twill, basket, sateen etc).  Fabrics having a standard weave pattern but new effects are created due to the pattern.
[D03D 13/006] With additional leno yarn -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Fabric patterns with a leno binding yarn additional to the weft and warp yarns, e.g. for fixing yarns in reinforcement fabrics.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Leno yarns in selvedges -> D03D5/00 - Gauze or leno woven fabrics (i.e. fabrics consisting mainly of leno yarns in the warp) -> D03D19/00
[D03D 13/008] characterised by weave density or surface weight -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Fabrics characterised by the yarn fineness, the weave density or surface weight.
[D03D 15/00] Woven fabrics characterised by the material, structure or properties of the fibres, filaments, yarns, threads or other warp or weft elements used -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Woven fabrics obtained by interlacing of warp and weft elements.  Documents classified here are directed to the weaving and have general relevance for weaving. If the application relates to properties of the yarns per se and a woven fabric is mentioned only by way of example, the corresponding place should be identified.
[D03D 15/20] characterised by the material of the fibres or filaments constituting the yarns or threads -- D03D 15/20 covers woven fabrics whose distinguishing feature is the material composition of the fibers or filaments that form the yarns or threads. It applies to fabrics where the type of fiber, such as cotton, polyester, nylon or blends, defines the fabric's properties.
[D03D 15/208] cellulose-based -- The subclass D03D 15/208 includes woven fabrics whose yarns, threads or other warp or weft elements are made from cellulose-based fibers such as cotton, viscose, lyocell, modal or other regenerated cellulose. It covers these fabrics regardless of the specific weave structure, finish or additional material components.
[D03D 15/217] natural from plants, e.g. cotton -- **Glossary:** - Plant: Vegetable that usually grows in earth and may have a stem, leaves, roots or flowers, or may produce seeds.
[D03D 15/225] artificial, e.g. viscose -- D03D 15/225 relates to woven fabrics whose warp or weft elements are artificial fibres such as viscose, a regenerated cellulose material. It classifies textiles defined by the use of these synthetic fibres in the fabric structure.
[D03D 15/233] protein-based, e.g. wool or silk -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Fabrics with warp and/or weft yarns or threads comprising protein-based fibres or filaments, e.g wool fibres.
[D03D 15/235] Cashmere or silk -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Fabrics with warp and/or weft yarns or threads comprising cashmere or silk fibres.
[D03D 15/242] inorganic, e.g. basalt -- This subgroup covers woven fabrics whose warp or weft elements are made from inorganic materials, such as basalt fibers. It includes textiles where the fibre composition, structure or properties derive from minerals rather than organic polymers.
[D03D 15/247] Mineral -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Fabrics with warp or weft yarns or threads comprising a mineral like rock or basalt.
[D03D 15/25] Metal -- Woven fabrics in which the warp, weft or other yarn elements are made of metal or metal-based filaments, such as metal wire cloth, metallic mesh or fabric reinforced with metal threads, are classified under CPC D03D 15/25. This subclass covers textiles whose defining feature is the use of metal as the primary material component.
[D03D 15/258] Noble metal -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Fabrics with warp or weft yarns or threads comprising "noble metal", e.g. gold, silver, platinum or iridium.
[D03D 15/267] Glass -- Glass denotes woven fabrics whose warp, weft or other yarn elements are made from glass fibres, either as continuous filaments or spun yarns. These fabrics are used primarily for reinforcement, insulation or other technical purposes where the properties of glass fibres-high strength, temperature resistance and electrical insulation-are required.
[D03D 15/275] Carbon fibres -- D03D 15/275 covers woven fabrics whose warp or weft elements are made from carbon fibres, including fabrics where carbon fibres are the primary material or are blended with other fibres to provide specific mechanical, thermal or electrical properties.
[D03D 15/283] synthetic polymer-based, e.g. polyamide or polyester fibres -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Cellulose-based artificial fibres -> D03D15/225
[D03D 15/292] Conjugate, i.e. bi- or multicomponent, fibres or filaments -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Fabrics with warp or weft yarns or threads comprising conjugate fibres or filaments, i.e. bi- or multicomponent fibres or filaments, e.g. core-sheath and island-in-sea.  Illustrative examples of subject matter classified in this place:  The single fibre or filament is built up of two or more components.  **Glossary:** - conjugate: conjugate indicates fibres or filaments having two or more different components within the individual fibre or filament, e.g. a polyethylene sheath component and a polyester core component
[D03D 15/30] characterised by the structure of the fibres or filaments -- The D03D 15/30 subclass covers woven fabrics whose defining feature is the structure of the constituent fibres or filaments, such as their cross-sectional shape, hollow cores, twists or other morphological characteristics that determine the fabric's nature.
[D03D 15/33] Ultrafine fibres, e.g. microfibres or nanofibres -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Fabrics with warp or weft yarns or threads with extremely fine staple fibres or filaments, typically less than 1 dtex.
[D03D 15/37] with specific cross-section or surface shape -- D03D 15/37 covers woven fabrics whose warp or weft elements are defined by a particular cross-section or surface shape, such as flattened, hollow, ribbed or otherwise profiled yarns. The classification is used when the distinctive shape of the fibres, filaments, yarns or threads contributes to the fabric's properties or appearance.
[D03D 15/40] characterised by the structure of the yarns or threads -- The CPC group D03D 15/40 pertains to woven fabrics whose defining feature is the specific structure or arrangement of the yarns or threads that make up the warp and weft. It sits within the hierarchy textiles-weaving-woven fabrics-woven fabrics characterised by material, structure or properties of the constituent fibres, filaments, yarns or threads.
[D03D 15/41] with specific twist -- D03D 15/41 relates to woven fabrics whose warp, weft or other yarn components are manufactured with a defined twist, influencing the fabric's texture, strength, elasticity or other functional properties. The classification captures any textile product where the specific twist of the yarns is a distinguishing feature.
[D03D 15/43] with differing diameters -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Fabrics comprising separate yarns or threads of different diameters or linear densities or with different diameters within individual yarns or threads.
[D03D 15/44] with specific cross-section or surface shape -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Fabrics with warp or weft yarns or threads defined by a particular cross-sectional shape, e.g. flat, oval or asymmetrical.
[D03D 15/46] Flat yarns, e.g. tapes or films -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Fabrics with warp and/or weft yarns or threads which are flat or tape-like or films.
[D03D 15/47] multicomponent, e.g. blended yarns or threads -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Fabrics with warp or weft yarns or threads comprising yarns or threads of different materials, e.g. cotton and polyester.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Multicomponent fibres or filaments -> D03D15/292  **Glossary:** - Multicomponent: Yarns or threads having two or more different types of fibres or filaments that are combined together into a yarn or thread structure, indicates yarns or threads built up of two or more components which are joined, e.g. core spun yarn or plied yarn.
[D03D 15/49] textured -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Fabrics with warp or weft yarns or threads comprising textured or fancy yarns.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Chenille -> D03D27/18
[D03D 15/497] Knitted threads -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Fabrics with warp and/or weft yarns or threads of knitted strips.
[D03D 15/50] characterised by the properties of the yarns or threads -- D03D 15/50 is a Cooperative Patent Classification for woven fabrics where the defining feature is the properties of the yarns or threads used, such as strength, elasticity, or thermal characteristics. It falls under Textiles → Weaving → Woven fabrics, focusing on how the material qualities of warp or weft elements influence the fabric's performance.
[D03D 15/507] magnetic -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Fabrics with warp or weft yarns or threads having magnetic properties.
[D03D 15/513] heat-resistant or fireproof -- D03D 15/513 covers woven fabrics that are specifically designed to be heat-resistant or fire-proof, meaning the warp or weft elements are treated or constructed to sustain high temperatures or prevent ignition. This subclass falls under the CPC hierarchy for textiles, weaving, and woven fabrics, focusing on the material, structure, or properties that provide fire-safety performance.
[D03D 15/52] thermal insulating, e.g. heating or cooling -- D03D 15/52 covers woven fabrics that provide thermal insulation, such as those used for heating or cooling purposes. The classification includes fabrics whose insulating performance is derived from the choice of fibres, yarns, structure or other warp-weft elements.
[D03D 15/527] waterproof or water-repellent -- The subclass D03D 15/527 covers woven fabrics that have been made waterproof or water-repellent, including the materials, treatments, constructions and properties that give the fabric resistance to water penetration. It applies to any woven textile where water resistance is a defining functional characteristic.
[D03D 15/533] antistatic -- A subclass of woven fabrics that specifies the use of fibers, yarns or other warp-or-weft elements treated or selected to provide antistatic performance, reducing static electricity buildup on the textile. This falls under the CPC hierarchy TEXTILES > WEAVING > WOVEN FABRICS and is identified by code D03D 15/533.
[D03D 15/54] coloured -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Fabrics with effects due to the use of warp or weft yarns or threads of specific colour(s).
[D03D 15/547] with optical functions other than colour, e.g. comprising light-emitting fibres -- This CPC subclass D03D 15/547 covers woven fabrics that incorporate optical functions other than colour, such as light-emitting fibers integrated into the material. It falls under textiles > weaving > woven fabrics and relates to fabrics whose fibers, yarns or threads provide illumination or other optical effects.
[D03D 15/553] with metallic effect -- A D03D 15/553 woven fabric is designed to display a metallic effect, achieved by incorporating metal-coated or metal-filled fibres, yarns or threads, or by applying metallic finishes to the warp or weft. The classification covers any woven material whose appearance, sheen or reflective properties are imparted through such metallic components.
[D03D 15/56] elastic -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Fabrics with warp or weft yarns or threads comprising elastic or stretchable yarns or threads.
[D03D 15/567] Shapes or effects upon shrinkage -- D03D 15/567 covers woven fabrics where the shape or dimensional changes resulting from shrinkage are a principal feature, including methods and treatments that control or exploit shrinkage effects. It falls under the hierarchy TEXTILES / WEAVING / WOVEN FABRICS, which classifies fabrics by the material, structure or properties of their warp and weft elements.
[D03D 15/573] Tensile strength -- This subclass concerns woven fabrics and the measurement or enhancement of their tensile strength. It covers patents that test, reinforce, or select fibers, yarns, or weave structures to improve a fabric's pull-force resistance. The code D03D 15/573 is applied to inventions dealing with load-bearing performance of woven textiles.
[D03D 15/58] characterised by the coefficients of friction -- D03D 15/58 relates to woven fabrics that are defined primarily by the coefficients of friction of their constituent fibres, yarns or threads. This subclass covers fabrics whose slip, grip or tactile performance is a key distinguishing feature, often important for applications requiring specific frictional behaviour.
[D03D 15/587] adhesive -- The D03D 15/587 subclass covers adhesives applied to woven fabrics, including the composition, preparation and application methods for bonding fibres, yarns or threads within the fabric structure. It also addresses adhesives that modify the mechanical or functional properties of the textile, such as strength, flexibility or moisture resistance.
[D03D 15/593] Stiff materials, e.g. cane or slat -- The subclass D03D 15/593 relates to woven fabrics in which the warp or weft elements are made of stiff material such as cane, slats or comparable rigid filaments. It covers products where the rigidity of the yarns is a defining feature, for example in baskets, trays or reinforced panels.
[D03D 15/60] characterised by the warp or weft elements other than yarns or threads -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Woven fabric characterised by elements not covered by subgroups D03D15/20 - D03D15/593 , or by using the element as an extra component, either in the machine direction or in the cross direction, e.g. as a reinforcement or an effect.
[D03D 15/62] Cords or ropes -- D03D 15/62 covers cords or ropes that are produced as woven fabric structures, where the warp and weft elements may consist of various fibers, filaments, yarns or threads. The classification is used for inventions relating to the material composition, construction or functional properties of such woven cords and ropes.
[D03D 15/63] Straps, e.g. leather straps -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Fabrics with warp or weft components formed from strips of materials, e.g. leather straps.
[D03D 15/65] Paper -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Fabrics with warp or weft yarns or threads of paper, often paper strips.
[D03D 15/67] Metal wires -- Metal wires used as warp, weft or other yarn elements in woven fabrics, providing reinforcement, conductivity or distinctive mechanical properties. This subclass covers fabrics where the wire itself forms the primary structure or is integrated with other fibers to achieve specific functional or aesthetic effects.
[D03D 15/68] Scaffolding threads, i.e. threads removed after weaving -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Fabrics containing temporary warp or weft yarns or threads.
[D03D 15/69] Threads with beads -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Fabrics with warp and/or weft threads containing beads, pearls and the like.
[D03D 17/00] Woven fabrics having elastic or stretch properties due to manner of weaving -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Woven fabrics having elastic or stretch properties due to the manner of weaving.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Fabrics using stretchable or elastic threads -> D03D15/56
[D03D 19/00] Gauze or leno-woven fabrics -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Fabrics comprising mainly leno yarns outside the selvedges. The leno yarns are normally provided to ensure that the warp yarns do not slide along the weft yarns in loosely woven fabrics.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Selvedges with or without leno yarns -> D03D5/00 - Fabric with a leno binding yarn additional to the weft and warp yarns, e.g. for fixing yarns in reinforcement fabrics -> D03D13/006 - Leno shedding mechanisms -> D03C7/00
[D03D 21/00] Lappet- or swivel-woven fabrics -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Fabrics with yarns passing only over part of the width or length of the fabric.  **Glossary:** - Lappet: weaving is figured by the introduction of additional warp yarns which pass only over part of the length of the fabric - Swivel: weaving is figured by the introduction of additional weft yarns which pass only over part of the width of the fabric
[D03D 23/00] General weaving methods not special to the production of any particular woven fabric or the use of any particular loom -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Weaving methods of fabrics which are not specific for a certain loom.
[D03D 25/00] Woven fabrics not otherwise provided for -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Woven fabrics not otherwise provided for.
[D03D 25/005] Three-dimensional woven fabrics -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Integrally woven 3D-constructions. It also covers multi-ply fabrics in which the length or width direction is limited to a predetermined dimension in order to create a rectangular shape.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Multi-ply fabrics which extend, apart from having a certain thickness, in two directions -> D03D11/00
[D03D 27/00] Woven pile fabrics -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Woven pile fabrics.
[D03D 27/02] wherein the pile is formed by warp or weft -- This subclass covers woven pile fabrics where the raised pile is produced by using either the warp or the weft threads to form the pile. It relates to textile manufacturing methods in which pile formation is integral to the warp or weft structure, distinguishing it from other pile-forming techniques.
[D03D 27/04] Weft pile fabrics -- Weft pile fabrics are woven textiles in which the pile - raised loops or tufts - is primarily formed by the weft yarns that pass across the fabric width. This structure creates a soft, textured surface commonly used for carpets, upholstery and decorative textiles.
[D03D 27/06] Warp pile fabrics -- Warp pile fabrics are woven pile textiles in which the pile yarns run longitudinally as warps, creating a raised surface that follows the fabric's lengthwise direction. They are produced by weaving techniques that raise warp threads to form loops or cut piles, and are commonly used for items such as towels, bathrobes and certain carpet structures where a soft, absorbent surface is required.
[D03D 27/08] Terry fabrics -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Terry fabrics, e.g. towels, beach robes.
[D03D 27/10] Fabrics woven face-to-face, e.g. double velvet -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Fabrics consisting of two layers which are connected by pile yarns, the two layers being simultaneously woven on the loom.
[D03D 27/12] wherein pile tufts are inserted during weaving -- **Definition:** This place covers:  In particular fabrics woven on Axminster looms.
[D03D 27/14] with tufts around warps -- The subclass covers woven pile fabrics in which tufts are formed around the warp yarns, creating raised loops or cords such as those found in terry or looped textiles. It includes weaving methods that anchor the tufts to the warp strands to produce a textured surface.
[D03D 27/16] with tufts around wefts -- A D03D 27/16 woven pile fabric is characterised by tufts that are formed around the weft yarns rather than the warp. The tufts are created by inserting extra yarns or loops adjacent to each weft thread, producing a raised, patterned surface on the fabric. This subclass includes any material where the pile is generated by such weft-centred tufting techniques.
[D03D 27/18] Chenille fabrics -- **Glossary:** - Chenille yarn: is manufactured by placing short lengths of yarn, called the "pile", between two "core yarns" and then twisting the yarn together. The edges of these piles then stand at right angles from the yarn's core, giving chenille both its softness and its characteristic look. Chenille will look different in one direction compared to another, as the fibres catch the light differently.
[D03D 29/00] Looms -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Looms where the weft thread is inserted by hand.
[D03D 3/00] Woven fabrics characterised by their shape -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Fabrics which are woven to assume a special shape. This shape is not achieved by cutting or by any other post-processing step.
[D03D 3/005] Tapes or ribbons not otherwise provided for  D03D1/0005 ,  D03D1/0011 ,  D03D1/06 ,  D03D1/08 ,  D03D1/0094  take precedence -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Tapes or ribbons not covered by other groups.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Safety belts -> D03D1/0005 - Tapes for labels -> D03D1/0011 - Belts -> D03D1/0094 - Curtain heading tapes -> D03D1/06 - Ladder tapes -> D03D1/08 - Slide fastener tapes -> D10B2501/063
[D03D 3/02] Tubular fabrics -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Sleeve-like structures integrally woven in a tubular shape.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group:  **Glossary:** - Tubular fabric: A tubular woven fabric is either a (1) flat-woven multi-layer fabric integrally woven as a tube, i.e. with a continuous weft connecting the layers at both edges of the fabric, and only at the edges, or (2) an integrally circular woven fabric. Woven fabrics where the tubular shape is achieved after weaving, e.g. a flat woven fabric rolled up into a tubular shape, are not considered as tubular fabrics.
[D03D 3/04] Endless fabrics -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Endless fabrics, for example belts without a seam.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Papermaking fabrics -> D21F1/0036 , D21F1/0054 , D21F1/105 , D21F1/12 , D21F1/16 , D21F7/10
[D03D 3/06] Fabrics of varying width -- The CPC entry D03D 3/06 includes woven fabrics whose width changes along their length, such as tapes, ribbons, tapered or expanding fabrics. It falls under the hierarchy textiles → weaving → woven fabrics → woven fabrics characterised by their shape, and addresses products where the shape of the fabric is a defining feature.
[D03D 3/08] Arched, corrugated, or like fabrics -- This subclass covers woven fabrics that are formed into arches, corrugations or similar three-dimensional profiles. The fabrics may be produced by special weaving techniques, post-weaving treatment or finishing processes that give the material a curved, folded or ribbed surface for reinforcement, cushioning or decorative purposes.
[D03D 31/00] Lappet, swivel or other looms for forming embroidery-like decoration on fabrics -- **Glossary:** - Lappet: weaving is figured by the introduction of additional warp yarns which pass only over part of the length of the fabric - Swivel: weaving is figured by the introduction of additional weft yarns which pass only over part of the width of the fabric
[D03D 33/00] Multiple looms, i.e. two or more looms assembled together, whether or not they have mechanisms in common -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Multiple looms, i.e. two or more looms assembled together, whether or not they have mechanisms in common.
[D03D 35/00] Smallware looms, i.e. looms for weaving ribbons or other narrow fabrics -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Smallware looms, i.e. looms for weaving ribbons or other narrow fabrics.
[D03D 35/005] Shuttles -- In smallware looms used for weaving ribbons or other narrow fabrics, a shuttle is a handheld or mechanised carrier that holds the weft yarn and travels back-and-forth through the shed to interlace the yarns and form the woven structure. It is a core component that enables continuous insertion of the weft in narrow-fabric production.
[D03D 37/00] Circular looms -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Looms where the weft inserting means follows a circular path.
[D03D 39/00] Pile-fabric looms -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Looms for weaving pile fabrics.
[D03D 39/02] Axminster looms, i.e. wherein pile tufts are inserted during weaving -- D03D 39/02 relates to Axminster looms, a class of pile-fabric looms in which individual pile tufts are inserted directly into the weave during weaving to produce woven pile fabrics such as carpets and rugs.
[D03D 39/04] Spool Axminster looms -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Axminster looms where the yarn comes from a tube, actuated mechanically, without gripper.
[D03D 39/06] Tuft yarn tube or spool frames -- The D03D 39/06 subclass deals with frames or devices that support yarn tubes or spools in tuft-fabric looms. These structures feed the tuft yarn to the weaving or knitting mechanism that creates pile-type woven fabrics. They fall under the broader categories of textiles, weaving, woven fabrics and pile-fabric looms.
[D03D 39/08] Gripper Axminster looms -- Gripper Axminster looms are a variant of pile-fabric looms that employ a gripper mechanism to control the weft yarns during the weaving of Axminster carpets and similar textiles. The classification D03D 39/08 covers these looms and related devices that enable precise placement of yarns to produce patterned, high-density pile fabrics.
[D03D 39/083] Selecting mechanisms -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The tuft yarn selection in Axminster looms.
[D03D 39/086] Gripper details -- D03D 39/086 covers the design and construction of gripper elements used in pile-fabric looms, such as pins, claws or suction devices that secure yarn or fabric during weaving. The code pertains to detailed features that affect grip force, adjustability or integration with the loom's beater and guide system.
[D03D 39/10] Wire-tapestry looms, e.g. for weaving velvet or Brussels or Wilton carpets, the pile being formed over weftwise wires -- This subclass covers looms that employ wires as the weft to form the pile of woven fabrics such as velvet, Brussels or Wilton carpets. The machines create a wire-tapestry structure where the pile is raised over the weftwise wires, enabling specialized carpet and velvet production.
[D03D 39/12] Mechanisms for operating the pile wires -- The CPC subclass D03D 39/12 covers mechanisms that operate the pile wires in pile-fabric looms used for weaving woven textiles with a raised surface. It includes devices that drive, control or adjust the wires that lift the pile yarns, such as cam, lever, pneumatic or electronic systems that create the pile effect.
[D03D 39/14] Construction of the pile wires, e.g. pile wires which cut -- This CPC subclass deals with the design and formation of the individual wires that constitute the pile in woven fabrics, focusing on wires that are cut to shape. It pertains to the construction of pile wires used in pile-fabric looms for textile production.
[D03D 39/16] Double-plush looms, i.e. for weaving two pile fabrics face-to-face -- The D03D 39/16 subclass covers double-plush looms, machines designed to weave two pile fabrics simultaneously so that their plush sides face each other. These looms enable the production of double-sided pile textiles used in items such as reversible blankets, upholstery, and specialty apparel, allowing efficient manufacturing of identical or complementary pile surfaces in a single operation.
[D03D 39/18] Separating the two plush layers, e.g. by cutting
[D03D 39/20] Looms forming pile over warpwise wires -- D03D 39/20 designates looms that form pile by laying yarns over warpwise wires, producing woven fabrics with a raised surface. It falls under the CPC hierarchy of Textiles → Weaving → Woven fabrics → Pile-fabric looms.
[D03D 39/22] Terry looms -- D03D 39/22 covers looms specially constructed for producing terry fabrics, a type of pile fabric with looped yarns on one surface used in towels and similar products. The subclass falls under pile-fabric looms within woven fabrics and textiles, defining mechanical features and configurations that form the characteristic loops of terry material.
[D03D 39/223] Cloth control -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Cloth shifting type terry looms.
[D03D 39/226] Sley control -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Sley movement adjusting type terry looms.
[D03D 39/24] Devices for cutting the pile on the loom -- The CPC subclass D03D 39/24 pertains to devices mounted on pile-fabric looms that trim or cut the raised pile fibers during weaving to achieve a uniform surface or specific texture. These mechanisms are employed in the production of woven pile fabrics within the textile industry.
[D03D 41/00] Looms not otherwise provided for, e.g. for weaving chenille yarn -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Looms not otherwise provided for, e.g. for weaving chenille yarns.
[D03D 41/001] Hair-cloth looms -- The CPC subclass D03D 41/001 covers looms that are not specified elsewhere, particularly those designed for producing hair-cloth and similar fabrics such as chenille yarn. These machines fall under the textile hierarchy of weaving woven fabrics and are intended for specialty or decorative weaving operations.
[D03D 41/002] Looms for weaving reed, straw or wood -- D03D 41/002 covers looms specially constructed to weave rigid natural materials such as reed, straw or wood. These machines include adaptations for feeding, tensioning and interlacing stiff fibers, differentiating them from conventional textile looms used for flexible yarns. The subclass falls within the CPC hierarchy of textiles, weaving and woven fabrics.
[D03D 41/004] Looms for three-dimensional fabrics -- This subclass relates to looms specially configured to produce three-dimensional woven structures, where the fabric is formed with depth or curvature during the weaving process. It includes machines that create tubular, sculpted or volumetric fabrics by controlling warp and weft placement in multiple planes, distinguishing them from conventional flat-weave looms.
[D03D 41/005] Linear-shed multiphase looms -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Looms with a rotor having multiple weft insertion channels in each of which a weft is inserted into the shed simultaneously.  Documents relating to the Sulzer rotor loom.
[D03D 41/007] Vertical looms -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Looms with a vertical arrangement of the warp yarns at the weft insertion position.
[D03D 41/008] Looms for weaving flat yarns -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Looms especially adapted to weave fabrics with flat yarns in the warp or weft.
[D03D 43/00] Looms with change-boxes -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Shuttle looms which have shuttle boxes for storing multiple shuttles, in particular with different weft yarns, from which the shuttles are selected in accordance with a specific pattern.
[D03D 43/02] with drop boxes -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Change boxes with compartments in a non-rotary arrangement, in which the shuttles are dropped.
[D03D 43/04] Operating mechanisms -- D03D 43/04 concerns the operating mechanisms of looms that incorporate change-boxes, devices that allow weft yarn to be inserted, removed or altered while the loom is running. It includes drive, control and actuation systems that coordinate the motion of the change-box with the fabric-forming cycle.
[D03D 43/06] with rotary boxes -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Change boxes with compartments in a rotary arrangement, in which the shuttles are dropped.
[D03D 43/08] Operating mechanisms -- The D03D 43/08 class defines the operating mechanisms used on looms equipped with change-boxes, including devices that control shaft motion, shed formation, and treadle or foot-pedal actuation. It covers any mechanical arrangements that regulate the timing, speed or direction of the loom's functional components during weaving.
[D03D 43/10] Weft catchers, i.e. means for preventing entanglement of wefts -- The CPC entry D03D 43/10 covers weft catchers, devices installed on looms with change-boxes to prevent weft yarns from becoming tangled during weaving. These mechanisms secure the weft as it passes the shed, ensuring continuous, defect-free fabric production.
[D03D 45/00] Looms with automatic weft replenishment -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Automatic replenishment of weft yarn stored in a shuttle.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Automatic replenishment in smallware looms -> D03D35/00 - Automatic replenishment in circular looms -> D03D37/00
[D03D 45/02] Controlling replenishment -- D03D 45/02 covers mechanisms and control systems that manage the automatic supply of weft yarn to a loom during weaving. It includes sensors, actuators and software that monitor yarn tension, detect depletion and trigger replenishment to maintain continuous fabric production.
[D03D 45/04] Pirn feelers or other detectors for initiating replenishment -- D03D 45/04 covers devices on looms with automatic weft replenishment that sense the condition of the pirn or other weft supply and trigger the replenishment cycle. It includes mechanical feelers, optical or electronic detectors, and any related mechanisms that initiate weft replacement when the supply runs low.
[D03D 45/06] mechanical -- The D03D 45/06 class pertains to the mechanical features of looms that automatically replenish the weft during weaving of woven fabrics. It includes the design, operation and components of automated weft-feeding mechanisms used in textile production.
[D03D 45/08] of the side-slip type -- This subclass covers looms that automatically replenish the weft using a side-slip system, where the weft yarn is drawn from a side reservoir and slipped into the shed as the insertions occur. The side-slip type is used to maintain continuous weaving without manual weft loading.
[D03D 45/10] electrical -- The D03D 45/10 subclass covers electrical features of looms that automatically replenish the weft, including electric drives, control circuitry, sensors and power supply arrangements that manage the weft-feeding mechanism and overall loom operation.
[D03D 45/12] optical -- The D03D 45/12 subclass pertains to looms equipped with optical devices that monitor yarn or fabric during automatic weft replenishment. It includes systems that use light beams, cameras or other visual sensors to detect thread breakage, position, or pattern and to control loom actions accordingly.
[D03D 45/14] Storing the need for replenishment or the colour required until the spent shuttle returns to the replenishing end of the loom
[D03D 45/16] selecting thereby weft of correct colour -- The D03D 45/16 subclass covers looms equipped with automatic weft-replenishment systems that incorporate means to identify and select the weft yarn of the proper colour for each shed. It relates to woven-fabric production where colour sequencing is controlled by sensors, colour-coded yarn carriers, or software that ensures the correct coloured weft is inserted during weaving. This facilitates precise colour patterns without manual yarn handling.
[D03D 45/18] Shuttle feelers or other devices for preventing replenishment if shuttle is incorrectly positioned in shuttle box -- D03D 45/18 deals with devices such as shuttle feelers that detect a mis-positioned shuttle in looms with automatic weft-replenishment and stop the weft from being fed. These mechanisms ensure correct shuttle placement before the replenishment cycle proceeds, improving loom reliability and fabric quality.
[D03D 45/20] Changing bobbins, cops or other loom components carried by the shuttle -- D03D 45/20 covers mechanisms for replacing bobbins, cops or other shuttle-carried components during loom operation, enabling automatic change without stopping the machine. It relates to looms with automatic weft replenishment, facilitating continuous weaving of woven fabrics.
[D03D 45/22] effected with shuttle in normal shuttle box -- The classification covers looms that automatically supply weft but still use a conventional shuttle moving back and forth within a normal shuttle box to insert the weft yarn, rather than alternative insertion mechanisms such as jet or rapier systems. This method combines automatic replenishment with the classic shuttle-based weaving process.
[D03D 45/24] effected otherwise than in normal box -- A D03D 45/24 loom automatically feeds weft but does so by a method other than the conventional box or bobbin system, employing alternative carriers, conveyors or dispensing devices to supply yarn during weaving. This subcategory covers non-standard weft replenishment mechanisms in automatically fed looms.
[D03D 45/26] Magazines -- D03D 45/26 designates patents concerning magazines for looms with automatic weft replenishment, which store and supply weft yarn to the shuttle or other insertion mechanism during weaving of woven fabrics. These magazines may be detachable, have tension control, or include mechanisms for sequential yarn feeding, improving efficiency and reducing manual handling.
[D03D 45/28] for one type of thread, e.g. same colour -- The D03D 45/28 subclass covers looms with automatic weft replenishment that are designed to operate with a single type of thread, such as a uniform colour, throughout the weaving process. It includes mechanisms that feed and replace one yarn continuously without handling multiple yarns or colour changes.
[D03D 45/30] rotary -- The D03D 45/30 subclass pertains to rotary devices employed in weaving machines that automatically feed the weft thread. It falls under textiles, weaving, woven fabrics, looms with automatic weft replenishment and addresses the mechanical arrangement of rotating elements that control weft insertion.
[D03D 45/32] for several types of thread, e.g. multicolour -- D03D 45/32 denotes looms equipped with automatic weft-replenishment mechanisms that can handle multiple thread types, such as multicolour wefts, during the weaving of woven fabrics. It falls under the textiles-weaving hierarchy, specifically for woven fabrics produced on looms capable of varying the weft material without manual intervention.
[D03D 45/34] Shuttle changing -- The D03D 45/34 subclass covers devices or methods for changing the shuttle in looms that automatically replenish the weft. It includes shuttle-changing mechanisms that replace or exchange the shuttle during operation to keep weaving continuous. This area addresses improvements to shuttle handling in automatic weaving machines.
[D03D 45/36] fresh shuttle being substituted for spent shuttle in the same cell of shuttle box -- The classification D03D 45/36 pertains to weaving machines, specifically looms with automatic weft replenishment, where a fresh shuttle is exchanged for a spent shuttle within the same cell of the shuttle box, enabling continuous fabric production without manual intervention.
[D03D 45/38] Shuttle box with an extra cell which is used only during replenishment -- The classification D03D 45/38 refers to a shuttle box that includes an extra cell used only during automatic weft replenishment in looms. It falls under the textile weaving hierarchy for woven fabrics and looms with automatic weft replenishment and describes a mechanism that aids shuttle handling during the replenishment phase.
[D03D 45/40] the spent shuttle being received in the normal cell and the fresh shuttle being picked from the extra cell -- The D03D 45/40 subclass addresses the shuttle-handling system in looms that automatically replenish the weft. It covers arrangements whereby the spent shuttle is returned to the normal cell while a fresh shuttle is drawn from an extra cell, ensuring continuous weaving without manual shuttle exchange. This mechanism is part of woven-fabric production in automated weaving equipment.
[D03D 45/42] the spent shuttle being received in the extra cell and the fresh shuttle being picked from the normal cell -- It denotes a loom mechanism that automatically transfers the spent weft shuttle into an extra cell while simultaneously picking a fresh shuttle from the normal cell, allowing continuous weaving without manual shuttle changes. This arrangement is used in automatic weft-replenishment systems for woven fabrics.
[D03D 45/44] Magazines -- The CPC subclass D03D 45/44 covers magazines, i.e., containers or feeding devices that hold and supply weft yarn to looms equipped with automatic weft replenishment systems for the production of woven fabrics. This entry falls under the textiles-weaving-woven-fabrics hierarchy, specifically addressing the apparatus that continuously delivers weft during weaving operations.
[D03D 45/46] for one type of thread, e.g. single colour -- D03D 45/46 classifies looms equipped with an automatic weft-replenishment system that feeds only a single type or colour of thread during weaving. The subclass covers mechanisms that continuously supply the weft without manual intervention, suitable for producing uniform-coloured woven fabrics.
[D03D 45/48] for several types of threads, e.g. multicolour -- This CPC subclass covers looms that automatically replenish the weft using a mixture of different coloured or material threads, allowing simultaneous handling of multiple thread types. It relates to woven fabric production where multicolour or varied thread feeding is controlled during weaving.
[D03D 45/50] Cutting, holding, manipulating, or disposing of, weft ends -- D03D 45/50 covers techniques and apparatus for managing weft ends in looms with automatic weft-replenishment, including cutting, holding, repositioning, or discarding excess or broken yarn. The classification addresses how the weft is controlled to maintain continuous fabric production.
[D03D 45/52] Cutting weft of depleted shuttle near shuttle and holding the piece attached to the fabric
[D03D 45/54] Cutting fresh weft and holding the piece attached to the fabric -- The CPC subclass D03D 45/54 covers loom mechanisms that automatically cut a newly supplied weft thread and secure the resulting piece to the fabric while it is being woven. It addresses continuous weft replenishment and piece-holding functions that improve fabric integrity and reduce manual intervention.
[D03D 45/56] Cutting depleted and fresh wefts at selvedge -- It refers to a method where the used (depleted) weft threads and new fresh weft threads are cut at the fabric edge (selvedge) during weaving on looms with automatic weft replenishment, facilitating a seamless transition and reducing waste. The cut sections are then removed or repositioned to maintain continuous fabric formation.
[D03D 45/58] Removing tip bunch or fresh weft end from fresh bobbin -- A device or method for automatically removing the tip bunch or freshly wound weft end from a new bobbin during the weft-replenishment cycle of a loom. It streamlines the transition to the next weft insertion, improving efficiency in woven-fabric production.
[D03D 45/60] substantially by air stream or suction -- This CPC subclass covers looms that supply the weft to the shed mainly by an air stream or suction instead of mechanical pick insertion, applying to automatic-weft-replenishment machines for woven fabrics.
[D03D 45/62] Disposing of waste pieces of cut weft end -- The CPC subclass D03D 45/62 relates to mechanisms on looms with automatic weft replenishment that collect, remove or discharge waste fragments of cut weft end, keeping the weaving process efficient by disposing of off-cut material.
[D03D 47/00] Looms in which bulk supply of weft does not pass through shed, e.g. shuttleless looms, gripper shuttle looms, dummy shuttle looms -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Looms in which the weft yarn is stored outside the shed (often at the side) and taken up by weft insertion means for introduction into the shed, e.g. rapier looms, needle looms and fluid jet looms.
[D03D 47/02] wherein loops of continuous weft thread are inserted, i.e. double picks -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Looms wherein loops of continuous weft thread are inserted, e.g. smallware looms where loops of weft yarn are normally inserted with a needle.
[D03D 47/04] by a reciprocating needle having a permanently-threaded eye -- This subclass deals with weaving apparatus where the weft is introduced by a needle that moves back and forth and carries the thread permanently in its eye, typical of shuttleless looms such as gripper or dummy shuttle looms. It provides a means to insert the weft without a shuttle.
[D03D 47/06] by a pivoted needle having a permanently-threaded eye -- This CPC subclass D03D 47/06 covers weaving on shuttle-less looms, such as gripper or dummy shuttle looms, where the weft is inserted by a pivoted needle that carries a permanently threaded eye, eliminating the need for the weft to pass through the shed. It specifies the needle-based weft feeding mechanism used in these bulk-supply weaving processes.
[D03D 47/08] the path of the needle being modified by cams, linkages, or other means -- The entry D03D 47/08 covers mechanisms that alter the needle's trajectory in shuttleless weaving machines, such as shuttleless, gripper shuttle or dummy shuttle looms. It includes the use of cams, linkages or other devices to guide the needle's motion during fabric forming.
[D03D 47/10] by a forked needle pushing loop of weft through shed -- It concerns a method of inserting weft in weaving using a forked needle that pushes the weft loop through the shed, used in shuttleless looms - where the bulk supply of weft does not pass through the shed.
[D03D 47/12] wherein single picks of weft thread are inserted, i.e. with shedding between each pick -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Looms wherein single picks of weft thread are inserted, e.g. with a rapier.
[D03D 47/125] Weft holding devices  if the weft is reinserted in the next shed  D03D47/48 ; in jet looms  D03D47/308 -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Means for holding the weft at the side of the shed.
[D03D 47/14] by a gripper needle taking the end of the weft through the shed -- This subclass covers weaving methods in which a gripper-type needle grasps the end of the weft and pulls it through the shed, used on shuttleless looms where the bulk supply of weft does not pass directly through the shed, such as gripper shuttle or dummy shuttle looms.
[D03D 47/16] by a gripper needle entering the shed empty and drawing the weft as it retracts -- A method for inserting weft in shuttle-less looms where the weft does not travel through the shed, using a gripper needle that first penetrates the empty shed and then draws the weft back as the needle retracts. This technique is applied to looms such as gripper-shuttle or dummy-shuttle types.
[D03D 47/18] two weft inserters meeting at or near the middle of the shed and transferring the weft from one to the other -- This CPC entry D03D 47/18 covers looms in which two weft inserters meet at or near the middle of the shed and transfer the weft from one inserter to the other, a feature of shuttleless weaving machines that do not carry a bulk supply of weft through the shed. It falls under the hierarchy textiles-weaving-woven fabrics.
[D03D 47/20] Constructional features of the thread-engaging device on the inserters -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Details of rapier grippers.
[D03D 47/22] adapted for working a loop of weft part-way inserted in the shed and then straightened-out -- This subclass covers loom mechanisms that handle a partially inserted weft loop, pulling it through the shed and straightening it during weaving on shuttleless looms such as gripper, dummy shuttle or other looms where the bulk supply of weft does not pass through the shed.
[D03D 47/23] Thread grippers -- The CPC subclass D03D 47/23 covers thread grippers, devices that hold and guide weft yarn in looms where the bulk weft supply does not pass through the shed, such as shuttleless, gripper-shuttle and dummy-shuttle looms. These mechanisms secure the yarn during insertion to ensure accurate placement and tension.
[D03D 47/233] Carrying grippers -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Carrying, giver or bringing grippers for transporting the weft to the middle of the shed.
[D03D 47/236] Drawing grippers -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Drawing, taker, pulling or receiving grippers for transporting the weft from the middle of the shed to the end of the shed.
[D03D 47/24] by gripper or dummy shuttle -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Projectile looms, where the shuttle does not carry a bobbin.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Travelling-wave-shed looms -> D03D47/26 - Inserting mechanisms for shuttles -> D03D49/24
[D03D 47/25] inserted from only one side of loom -- D03D 47/25 covers looms where the weft yarn is supplied from a single side and does not travel through the shed, such as shuttleless, gripper shuttle and dummy shuttle machines. The classification applies to weaving processes that insert the weft only from one side of the loom, typical of modern high-speed weaving equipment.
[D03D 47/26] Travelling-wave-shed looms -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Looms having multiple phase shifted sheds and a rotary reed.
[D03D 47/261] Preparation of weft -- The entry covers methods and apparatus for preparing the weft yarn before it is inserted into the shed of a shuttleless loom, such as gripper-shuttle or dummy-shuttle machines, where the bulk supply of weft does not pass through the shed. It includes techniques for winding, tensioning, and arranging the weft to enable continuous feeding during weaving.
[D03D 47/262] Shedding, weft insertion or beat-up mechanisms -- The CPC subclass D03D 47/262 relates to shedding, weft insertion and beat-up mechanisms used in looms where the bulk weft supply does not pass through the shed, such as shuttleless, gripper shuttle or dummy shuttle looms. It addresses the devices that form the shed, place the weft and compact the fabric during weaving.
[D03D 47/263] Weft insertion and beat-up mechanisms -- D03D 47/263 covers mechanisms that insert the weft and beat it up in looms where a bulk weft supply does not pass through the shed, such as shuttleless, gripper shuttle and dummy shuttle looms. It includes devices for positioning, guiding and compacting the weft yarn as it is laid into the weave and for the subsequent beat-up action that secures each pick. This subclass falls under woven fabrics in the textile hierarchy.
[D03D 47/265] Beat-up mechanisms  D03D47/263  takes precedence -- D03D 47/265 covers beat-up mechanisms used on looms that feed the weft in bulk without passing it through the shed, such as shuttleless, gripper shuttle and dummy shuttle looms. It is a sub-class of the woven-fabric section of the CPC and is subordinate to D03D 47/263.
[D03D 47/266] Weft insertion mechanisms  D03D47/263  takes precedence
[D03D 47/267] Shedding mechanisms -- The CPC subclass D03D 47/267 covers shedding mechanisms used in looms that supply the weft in bulk without a shuttle passing through the shed, such as shuttleless, gripper-shuttle and dummy-shuttle looms. It details devices that create, control or vary the warp opening during weaving of woven fabrics. This subclass falls under textiles → weaving → woven fabrics → looms with non-shuttle weft supply.
[D03D 47/268] Mechanisms for compensating warp tension -- Mechanisms for compensating warp tension are devices fitted to shuttle-less, gripper shuttle or dummy shuttle looms that maintain consistent warp tension while the bulk weft supply bypasses the shed. They adjust tension in real-time to prevent breakage and ensure uniform fabric formation during weaving. This subclass falls under woven fabrics in the textile weaving hierarchy.
[D03D 47/27] Drive or guide mechanisms for weft inserting -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Drive or guide means for weft thread insertion.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Guide mechanisms for pneumatic looms (normally means for creating an air channel for weft insertion) -> D03D47/278 - Carrying the weft thread with a fluid through the shed -> D03D47/28  **Glossary:** - Rapier: means for carrying the weft thread through the shed with a rod (sometimes also called lance or needle) or a flexible band - Drive mechanism: the part outside the shed driving the means for carrying the weft thread through the shed - Guide mechanism: parts inside and outside the shed to guide the means for carrying the weft threads through the shed
[D03D 47/271] Rapiers -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Rapiers, i.e. means to insert the weft thread with a flexible band or rod.
[D03D 47/272] Rapier bands -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Details of the rapier band.
[D03D 47/273] Rapier rods -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Details of the rapier rod.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Needles for smallware looms -> D03D47/02
[D03D 47/275] Drive mechanisms -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The part outside the shed driving the means for carrying the weft thread through the shed.
[D03D 47/276] Details or arrangement of sprocket wheels -- This CPC subclass covers technical features concerning the design and positioning of sprocket wheels that drive or synchronize mechanisms in shuttleless weaving looms, where the weft is supplied in bulk rather than passing through the shed. It addresses aspects such as tooth profile, spacing, mounting and interaction with belts or chains used in gripper or dummy shuttle looms.
[D03D 47/277] Guide mechanisms  for shuttles  D03D49/24 -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Guides for all insertion means except shuttle. For example: grippers, bands, lances or dummy shuttles.
[D03D 47/278] for pneumatic looms -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Means for creating an air channel for weft insertion.
