[D03D 47/28] wherein the weft itself is projected into the shed -- The CPC entry D03D 47/28 falls under textiles-weaving-woven fabrics for looms that do not feed a bulk weft through the shed, such as shuttleless, gripper-shuttle or dummy-shuttle looms. It specifically covers loom designs where the weft yarn is actively projected into the shed, using devices that push or launch the thread rather than carrying it on a shuttle.
[D03D 47/30] by gas jet -- This subclass covers weaving processes in which the weft yarn is inserted into the shed by a jet of gas instead of a shuttle, as used in shuttleless looms such as gripper-shuttle or dummy-shuttle machines. It includes pneumatic systems that propel the yarn across the warp to form woven fabrics.
[D03D 47/3006] Construction of the nozzles -- The CPC subclass D03D 47/3006 covers the design and construction of the nozzles that feed weft yarn into shuttleless weaving machines, such as gripper, dummy-shuttle or other looms where the weft does not pass through the shed. It includes details of nozzle shape, mounting, drive mechanisms and any features that control yarn placement during the weaving process.
[D03D 47/3013] Main nozzles -- The D03D 47/3013 subclass covers the main nozzles used in shuttleless weaving machines such as gripper shuttle and dummy shuttle looms, where the bulk weft supply is delivered without passing through the shed. These nozzles control the timing and flow of yarn into the loom.
[D03D 47/302] Auxiliary nozzles -- Auxiliary nozzles are devices mounted on shuttleless or dummy-shuttle looms that direct a controlled stream of air, fluid, or gas onto the weft-insertion zone to aid yarn placement, reduce friction, or clean the shed during operation. This feature falls under the woven-fabric category of textile weaving technologies.
[D03D 47/3026] Air supply systems -- Air supply systems for shuttleless weaving machines deliver a controlled flow of air to lift or separate weft yarns, replace traditional shuttle mechanisms, and improve insertion speed and fabric quality. These systems are integrated into looms such as gripper, dummy shuttle, and other shuttleless types, providing precise pneumatic control of yarn handling during the weaving process.
[D03D 47/3033] Controlling the air supply -- D03D 47/3033 relates to devices or methods for regulating the airflow that conveys weft yarn in shuttleless weaving machines, such as gripper-shuttle or dummy-shuttle looms, to ensure proper yarn insertion and fabric quality.
[D03D 47/304] Controlling of the air supply to the auxiliary nozzles -- D03D 47/304 covers mechanisms for regulating the airflow to the auxiliary nozzles of shuttleless weaving looms, where a continuous bulk weft is introduced without passing through the shed. It addresses devices that adjust pressure, volume or timing of the air stream to improve yarn placement and loom efficiency.
[D03D 47/3046] Weft yarn selection -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The weft yarn selection on air jet looms.
[D03D 47/3053] Arrangements or lay out of air supply systems -- The subclass deals with the design and configuration of pneumatic or other air-delivery mechanisms in shuttleless weaving machines, specifying how ducts, blow pipes, nozzles and related components are positioned to supply air, remove debris or assist fabric formation. It focuses on the spatial layout and integration of these air-supply systems within looms that use bulk weft delivery without a traditional shuttle.
[D03D 47/306] Construction or details of parts, e.g. valves, ducts
[D03D 47/3066] Control or handling of the weft at or after arrival -- The subclass concerns devices that control or handle the weft once it reaches the loom, especially in shuttleless looms where the bulk weft does not pass through the shed. It covers mechanisms for guiding, tensioning, inserting, metering or otherwise managing the weft after arrival, ensuring correct placement and fabric quality.
[D03D 47/3073] Detection means therefor -- D03D 47/3073 covers detection devices for shuttleless looms that feed a bulk supply of weft without it passing through the shed, such as gripper, dummy shuttle or other non-shuttle looms. The classification includes mechanisms that monitor warp or weft status, thread breakage, tension or positioning to ensure proper operation of these weaving machines.
[D03D 47/308] Stretching or holding the weft -- This CPC subclass D03D 47/308 covers mechanisms that stretch or hold the weft during weaving on shuttleless looms, such as gripper-shuttle or dummy-shuttle machines, allowing the weft to be positioned accurately without passing through the shed.
[D03D 47/3086] Weft removal  extraction of defective weft after stopping at definite point in weaving cycle  D03D51/085 -- This subclass covers mechanisms or steps for stopping a shuttleless weaving operation and extracting a faulty weft yarn that has been inserted incorrectly, allowing removal at a defined point in the weaving cycle. It applies to looms such as gripper shuttle or dummy shuttle types.
[D03D 47/3093] Displaying data
[D03D 47/32] by liquid jet -- The D03D 47/32 subclass covers shuttleless looms that insert the weft by means of a liquid jet, such as water-jet or other fluid streams, rather than by a traditional shuttle. This method is used on looms where the bulk supply of weft does not pass through the shed, enabling high-speed, precise placement of yarns in woven fabric production.
[D03D 47/34] Handling the weft between bulk storage and weft-inserting means -- **Definition:** This place covers:  All aspects concerning the handling of the weft yarns from the weft yarn supply means (i.e. normally bobbins) up to (but not including) the weft insertion.
[D03D 47/342] knot detection -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Systems for detecting wefts with faults such as knots.
[D03D 47/345] Rotating bobbins -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Bulk storage bobbins which are rotatable supported and/or driven for lateral unwinding without inducing twist to the yarn.
[D03D 47/347] Yarn brakes  D03D47/364  takes precedence -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Braking devices for weft yarns to be inserted.
[D03D 47/36] Measuring and cutting the weft -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Devices and methods of measuring and cutting the weft.  Devices for measuring and temporary storing the weft.
[D03D 47/361] Drum-type weft feeding devices -- The D03D 47/361 class covers drum-type weft feeding devices that supply the weft to shuttleless looms, such as gripper and dummy shuttle looms, where the bulk weft does not pass through the shed. These mechanisms use a rotating drum to control and deliver the weft yarn to the insertion point during weaving.
[D03D 47/362] with yarn retaining devices, e.g. stopping pins  D03D47/367  takes precedence -- D03D 47/362 covers shuttleless and similar looms in which a bulk supply of weft is fed without passing through the shed, focusing on yarn-retaining mechanisms such as stopping pins that hold the weft in place. This subclass falls under TEXTILES → WEAVING → WOVEN FABRICS and is superseded by the more specific D03D 47/367. It addresses devices that secure yarn during insertion on modern looms.
[D03D 47/363] Construction or control of the yarn retaining devices -- D03D 47/363 covers the design and control of yarn-retaining devices on shuttleless looms where the weft is supplied in bulk, such as gripper, dummy-shuttle or other non-shuttle looms. It addresses mechanisms that hold, tension and guide the weft yarn during weaving of woven fabrics.
[D03D 47/364] Yarn braking means acting on the drum -- The code refers to devices that apply a braking force to yarn wound on a drum to regulate its feed in shuttle-less looms such as gripper or dummy shuttle machines. The braking mechanism controls tension and feed rate of the weft supply, improving fabric quality and preventing yarn breakage.
[D03D 47/365] Brushes -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Braking bodies being constituted by a brush.
[D03D 47/366] Conical -- **Definition:** This place covers:  (Frusto-) conical braking bodies.
[D03D 47/367] Monitoring yarn quantity on the drum -- The D03D 47/367 entry covers devices that monitor the amount of yarn wound on a drum in looms where the weft supply does not pass through the shed, such as shuttleless, gripper-shuttle or dummy-shuttle looms. It pertains to systems that detect yarn quantity to ensure consistent feeding and prevent breakage during weaving.
[D03D 47/368] Air chamber storage devices -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Feeding devices in which the weft is temporarily stored in an air chamber, e.g. as loops or coils.
[D03D 47/369] Communication systems -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Communication systems between pre-winders and the loom.
[D03D 47/38] Weft pattern mechanisms -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Pneumatic weft pattern mechanisms -> D03D47/3046
[D03D 47/39] wherein cane, straw, slats, material for hair-cloth or similar material is handled -- D03D 47/39 covers woven-fabric looms in which the bulk supply of weft does not pass through the shed, including shuttleless, gripper-shuttle and dummy-shuttle looms, and specifically addresses the handling of materials such as cane, straw, slats or hair-cloth used in the weave.
[D03D 47/40] Forming selvedges -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Means, other than a separate shedding mechanism, necessary for the formation of selvedges, i.e. the side edges of woven fabrics.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Elvedges -> D03D5/00 - Selvedge jacquards -> D03C3/18 - Selvedge shedding mechanisms not forming part of main shedding mechanism -> D03C11/00
[D03D 47/42] by knitting or interlacing loops of weft -- This subclass covers looms that produce woven fabrics by forming loops of the weft and interlacing them, rather than passing a bulk weft through the shed. It applies to shuttleless, gripper-shuttle and dummy-shuttle machines that employ knitting-like actions to create the fabric structure.
[D03D 47/44] with additional selvedge thread -- D03D 47/44 covers shuttleless weaving machines, such as gripper-shuttle or dummy-shuttle looms, in which the weft is supplied in bulk and does not pass through the shed, and which incorporate an extra selvedge thread to reinforce the fabric edges. This subclass refines the broader categories of woven fabrics and looms without a shuttle-passing weft.
[D03D 47/46] by selvedge shuttle or other device passing selvedge thread through loop of weft
[D03D 47/48] by inserting cut end of weft in next shed, e.g. by tucking, by blowing -- This CPC subclass covers methods for advancing the weft in shuttleless, gripper shuttle or dummy shuttle looms where a bulk supply of weft does not pass through the shed. It includes techniques that insert the cut end of the weft into the next shed, such as tucking or blowing the yarn.
[D03D 47/50] by adhesion -- D03D 47/50 covers processes for producing woven fabrics on shuttleless looms where the weft is placed by adhesion rather than being passed through the shed. The method involves bonding the yarn or fabric strip to the warp using adhesives, enabling continuous feeding in looms such as gripper shuttle or dummy shuttle types. This classification falls under textiles, weaving, woven fabrics and specific loom types.
[D03D 49/00] Details or constructional features not specially adapted for looms of a particular type -- The CPC subgroup D03D 49/00 pertains to woven fabric characteristics that are independent of the specific loom used, covering general details and constructional features such as surface treatments, reinforcement methods, finishing processes, or other structural elements that do not rely on a particular weaving apparatus.
[D03D 49/02] Construction of loom framework -- This group is in the process of being reorganised to its subgroups
[D03D 49/022] Protective hoods or curtains -- D03D 49/022 designates protective hoods or curtains formed from woven fabrics whose details or constructional features are not tailored to a specific loom type. The subclass includes general protective coverings such as safety hoods, privacy curtains, or barrier screens used in industrial, medical, or other settings.
[D03D 49/025] Ground supports -- Ground supports are elements incorporated into or placed behind woven fabrics to provide stiffness, shape retention or cushioning, such as backing layers, stiffeners or padding. They are generic structural features not tied to a particular loom design.
[D03D 49/027] Arrangements or means for noise reduction -- The D03D 49/027 entry covers design elements in woven fabrics that reduce acoustic noise, such as damping layers, sound-absorbing inserts, or structural modifications that limit vibration and sound transmission during handling or use, without being tied to a specific loom type.
[D03D 49/04] Control of the tension in warp or cloth -- This CPC subclass covers methods, devices and systems for controlling the tension of warp threads or finished cloth during weaving, applicable to any loom type. It includes tension-regulating mechanisms, sensors, feedback loops and adjustments aimed at maintaining uniform tension throughout the process.
[D03D 49/06] Warp let-off mechanisms -- Warp let-off mechanisms control the rate at which warp yarn is fed from the beam to the loom, maintaining consistent tension and preventing breakage during weaving. They include brakes, tension devices and automated systems used across different loom types.
[D03D 49/08] Warp beam brakes -- D03D 49/08 covers devices that control the rotation of the warp beam during weaving, specifically brake mechanisms that regulate tension of the warp threads. These are general warp beam brakes not limited to any particular loom type, used to prevent thread breakage and ensure uniform fabric construction.
[D03D 49/10] Driving the warp beam to let the warp off -- The D03D 49/10 entry addresses mechanisms for driving the warp beam to release warp during weaving, covering general features of warp-off control that are not specific to any particular loom type. It falls under the hierarchy of textiles, weaving, and woven fabrics, focusing on the operation of the warp supply system.
[D03D 49/12] Controlling warp tension by means other than let-off mechanisms -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Controlling warp tension by means other than let-off mechanisms, e.g. by cloth take-up.
[D03D 49/14] Compensating for tension differences during shedding -- D03D 49/14 covers devices or methods that adjust warp tension during shedding to compensate for differences across the yarn band, ensuring uniform lift of warp threads and preventing distortion in woven fabrics. The solutions may include tension springs, variable-speed rollers or control systems that operate independently of a specific loom type.
[D03D 49/16] Warp supplied by creel -- The CPC class D03D 49/16 relates to woven fabric production where the warp threads are delivered to the loom from a creel, a rack that holds multiple bobbins or spools. It covers arrangements, mechanisms and control of warp feeding, tensioning and positioning from the creel, irrespective of the particular loom design. The subclass falls under details of woven fabric construction not specific to any loom type.
[D03D 49/18] Devices for indicating warp tension -- Devices for indicating warp tension are tools used in textile weaving to monitor and display the pulling force on warp threads, helping operators maintain consistent tension and avoid thread breakage while producing woven fabrics. They provide visual or mechanical feedback applicable to various loom types.
[D03D 49/20] Take-up motions -- The CPC subclass D03D 49/20 pertains to take-up motions, which are the mechanisms that advance woven fabric during production. These motions are described as constructional features that are not specially adapted for any particular loom type, and they fall under the hierarchy of textiles → weaving → woven fabrics.
[D03D 49/22] Back rests -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Bars which extend across the width of the warps or of the woven fabric.
[D03D 49/24] Mechanisms for inserting shuttle in shed -- The D03D 49/24 subclass covers devices that introduce the shuttle into the shed of a weaving loom, regardless of loom type. It includes mechanical, pneumatic or automatic insertion mechanisms designed to place the shuttle in the opening formed by the lifted warp threads.
[D03D 49/26] Picking mechanisms, e.g. for propelling gripper shuttles or dummy shuttles -- The D03D 49/26 class covers picking mechanisms used in weaving machines to launch or propel gripper shuttles, dummy shuttles, or similar carriers across the shed. It includes devices that generate the thrust or timing needed for shuttle movement, regardless of loom type.
[D03D 49/28] Driving mechanisms for the picker stick -- The CPC subclass D03D 49/28 covers driving mechanisms for the picker stick in weaving machines that produce woven fabrics. It concerns devices that move the picker to lift the warp yarns, without being limited to any specific loom type, and is used in textile manufacturing.
[D03D 49/30] operated by cam -- The D03D 49/30 subclass refers to woven fabrics whose constructional features are not tied to a specific loom type and where the fabric-forming action is driven by a cam mechanism that converts rotary motion into the lifting or lowering of warp or weft elements. This includes looms that use cams to control harness movement, shuttle timing or beat-up actions.
[D03D 49/32] operated by previously-loaded spring or equivalent -- D03D 49/32 covers woven-fabric features that are driven or tensioned by a previously-loaded spring or an equivalent mechanism, rather than by a loom-specific component. It applies to any weaving process where the spring-based action provides the necessary movement or control of the fabric, irrespective of the loom type used. This classification falls under details or constructional features of woven textiles that are not specially adapted for a particular loom.
[D03D 49/34] operated by liquid or gas pressure -- D03D 49/34 covers woven-fabric forming or processing methods and devices in which the motion of warp, weft or other elements is driven by liquid or gas pressure, such as hydraulic or pneumatic looms and related mechanisms. The subclass applies regardless of the specific loom type and includes any fluid-powered means for inserting, tensioning or patterning yarns.
[D03D 49/36] Pickers
[D03D 49/38] Picking sticks -- D03D 49/38 covers picking sticks, simple rods or tweezers used in weaving to separate or open warp threads and to aid the passage of weft yarns. The feature is a constructional detail applicable to woven fabrics in general, not limited to any specific loom type. It falls under textiles → weaving → woven fabrics → details or constructional features not specially adapted for looms.
[D03D 49/40] Check straps -- Check straps are reinforcing or fastening elements sewn into woven fabrics to hold portions of the material together, such as layers of a garment or sections of a bag. They are considered constructional features of woven textiles that are not specifically adapted to any particular type of loom. This falls under CPC group D03D 49/40 for details or constructional features of woven fabrics.
[D03D 49/42] whereby the shuttle is propelled by liquid or gas pressure -- D03D 49/42 relates to woven-fabric production methods in which the loom shuttle is moved by liquid or gas pressure instead of conventional mechanical drive, covering any loom not specially adapted for a particular type.
[D03D 49/44] whereby the shuttle is propelled by electric or magnetic means -- The class D03D 49/44 pertains to woven-fabric looms in which the shuttle is moved by electrical or magnetic forces rather than mechanical means. It addresses general shuttle-propulsion concepts that are not tied to a specific loom design, and is located under the textiles-weaving-woven-fabric hierarchy.
[D03D 49/46] wherein the shuttle is pushed or pulled positively -- D03D 49/46 is a CPC subclass for woven fabrics that addresses constructional features not tied to a specific loom type, specifically where the shuttle is moved by a positive push or pull action rather than by gravity or inertia. It sits under TEXTILES → WEAVING → WOVEN FABRICS.
[D03D 49/48] positioning shuttle in readiness for picking -- This class covers features of woven fabrics related to preparing the shuttle for picking, not specific to particular loom types. It includes mechanisms and arrangements that position the shuttle before the weft insertion.
[D03D 49/50] Miscellaneous devices or arrangements concerning insertion of weft and not otherwise provided for -- D03D 49/50 covers miscellaneous devices or arrangements used to insert the weft in woven fabrics that are not specifically adapted to a particular loom type, including auxiliary tools, mechanisms or modifications that assist weft insertion but are not otherwise classified elsewhere.
[D03D 49/52] Shuttle boxes -- Shuttle boxes are components used in loom mechanisms to hold and guide the shuttle that carries the weft yarn across the warp. They may be detachable or integral and include features for easy loading, removal, or adjustment of the shuttle, and are designed for various loom types without specialization.
[D03D 49/54] Braking means -- D03D 49/54 pertains to braking means for woven fabrics, covering devices or methods that control the movement or tension of fabric during handling or processing without being tied to a specific loom type. These features may include friction brakes, clamps or other mechanisms that halt or regulate fabric feed.
[D03D 49/56] Shuttle relief mechanisms, i.e. reducing shuttle swell pressure before picking -- The D03D 49/56 subclass falls under woven fabrics and addresses constructional features that are not specific to any particular loom type. It covers shuttle relief mechanisms designed to reduce shuttle swell pressure before picking, helping to minimise disturbance of the weft insertion.
[D03D 49/58] Shuttle guards -- Shuttle guards are protective devices fitted to looms to shield the shuttle from accidental contact and to keep it on its intended path during weaving. They are employed with woven fabrics that are not specially adapted for a particular loom type, improving safety and reducing fabric defects.
[D03D 49/60] Construction or operation of slay -- The D03D 49/60 subclass covers aspects of constructing or operating the sley - the loom component that carries the heddles and inserts the weft - in woven fabrics whose details or constructional features are not tailored to a specific type of loom. It includes general design, adjustment and functional features of the sley applicable across various weaving machines.
[D03D 49/62] Reeds mounted on slay -- The code D03D 49/62 covers woven-fabric production features concerning reeds that are mounted on a slay, a frame that holds the reed in place during weaving. It addresses general arrangements of the reed-slay assembly that are not limited to any particular loom type.
[D03D 49/64] wherein the slay dwells or moves slowly while the weft is being inserted -- The subclass D03D 49/64 relates to woven-fabric constructions in which the shed stays open or moves only slowly while the weft is inserted, without requiring special loom mechanisms. It covers general weaving techniques that control shed stability to aid weft insertion.
[D03D 49/66] Shuttle races -- The class D03D 49/66 covers shuttle-race devices and related details of woven-fabric construction that are not limited to a specific loom type. It includes the mechanisms that guide, accelerate or decelerate the shuttle as it traverses the shed, as well as structural features of the raceway itself.
[D03D 49/68] Reeds or beat-up combs not mounted on the slay -- D03D 49/68 pertains to reeds or beat-up combs that are separate from the slay and used in the preparation of woven fabrics. These components are considered details of construction that are not specifically adapted for any particular loom type, falling under the broader categories of textiles, weaving and woven fabrics.
[D03D 49/70] Devices for cutting weft threads -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The cutting of weft threads including the cutting of weft threads for removing selvedges.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Cutting weft in looms with automatic weft replenishment -> D03D45/50
[D03D 5/00] Selvedges -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Selvedges, i.e. the side edges of woven fabrics.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Means to form selvedges -> D03D47/40 - Selvedge jacquards -> D03C3/18 - Leno shedding mechanisms -> D03C7/00 - Selvedge shedding mechanisms not forming part of main shedding -> D03C11/00
[D03D 51/00] Driving, starting, or stopping arrangements -- The D03D 51/00 subclass pertains to devices that drive, initiate or halt the operation of weaving or other woven-fabric machinery. It includes motor-control arrangements, starters, brakes and related control systems that start, regulate or stop the machine's motion during fabric production.
[D03D 51/002] Avoiding starting marks -- D03D 51/002 addresses technical measures that prevent visible start marks on woven fabrics when a loom is driven, started or stopped. It includes devices or methods that mask or eliminate the initial weave formation to keep the fabric surface uniform.
[D03D 51/005] Independent drive motors  independent frame drives for dobbies  D03C1/146 , for other shedding mechanisms  D03C13/02 -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Operation and synchronisation of independent motors in looms.
[D03D 51/007] Loom optimisation -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Controlling one or more looms, e.g. by speed variation, to find an optimal operating condition.
[D03D 51/02] General arrangements of driving mechanism -- The subclass D03D 51/02 covers the overall layout and organization of mechanisms that drive, start, or halt weaving machines used in the production of woven fabrics. It includes the positioning of motors, gear trains, clutches and control devices that coordinate the movement of the loom.
[D03D 51/04] Manual controls -- D03D 51/04 pertains to manual control devices for driving, starting or stopping woven-fabric production equipment. It includes hand levers, pedals, cranks or similar manually operated mechanisms that engage or disengage the machine's drive. The classification is used for inventions that enable the operator to control weaving motion without automatic or electronic systems.
[D03D 51/06] using particular methods of stopping -- D03D 51/06 covers woven-fabric production where specific techniques are used to halt the weaving process. It includes mechanisms or control methods that stop weft insertion, cease loom motion, or otherwise interrupt fabric formation at a predetermined point. This sub-group falls under driving, starting or stopping arrangements for woven textiles.
[D03D 51/08] stopping at definite point in weaving cycle, or moving to such point after stopping -- The code D03D 51/08 covers devices or methods that halt a weaving machine at a precisely defined position in the weave cycle, or that resume operation from that exact point after a pause. Such stopping and restarting arrangements are used to insert pattern changes, conduct inspections, or synchronize auxiliary operations during weaving.
[D03D 51/085] Extraction of defective weft -- The CPC subclass D03D 51/085 covers methods for removing a defective weft yarn from a woven fabric during or after weaving. It includes mechanisms that detect, extract, or replace the flawed weft without disrupting the surrounding warp structure, facilitating repair or re-weaving of the fabric.
[D03D 51/10] stopping suddenly -- The CPC subclass D03D 51/10 covers features that cause a woven-fabric machine to halt instantly, such as braking devices, emergency stops or clutch disengagements that halt the drive, starting or stopping system without gradual deceleration. It applies to textile equipment where rapid cessation of motion is required for safety, product quality or process control.
[D03D 51/12] for adjusting speed -- D03D 51/12 covers mechanisms that adjust the speed of weaving equipment, such as variable speed drives, speed regulators, and control systems that modify the motion of the loom or other woven-fabric production machines during operation.
[D03D 51/14] for reducing speed temporarily -- D03D 51/14 relates to woven-fabric machinery and covers devices or methods that temporarily lower the running speed of a loom's drive system, enabling a controlled slowdown of warp or weft feed during operation.
[D03D 51/16] for varying speed cyclically -- A D03D 51/16 device belongs to the woven-fabric section of the Cooperative Patent Classification and concerns arrangements that drive, start or stop a loom or similar textile machine while varying its speed in a cyclic manner, such as speed-modulation mechanisms that periodically accelerate or decelerate the fabric-forming process.
[D03D 51/18] Automatic stop motions -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods and their implementations to detect broken warp or weft yarns and to stop the weaving machine upon this detection.
[D03D 51/20] Warp stop motions -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods and their implementations to detect broken warp yarns and to stop the weaving machine upon this detection.
[D03D 51/22] mechanical -- The CPC subclass D03D 51/22 covers mechanical systems that control the motion of weaving equipment, specifically devices that drive, start, or stop the operation of looms producing woven fabrics. It includes mechanisms such as motor couplings, cams, levers and other actuators that regulate the movement of warp or weft threads during the weaving process.
[D03D 51/24] wherein droppers are suspended on individual warp threads or small groups of threads -- This subclass defines driving, starting or stopping arrangements in woven fabric production where droppers are suspended on single warp threads or on small groups of threads, enabling precise control of warp tension and fabric formation.
[D03D 51/26] Detector healds -- The D03D 51/26 entry covers detector healds used in loom mechanisms to sense the position or movement of yarns, enabling automatic control of driving, starting or stopping actions. Such healds incorporate sensing elements-optical, mechanical or electronic-that detect the passage or tension of individual warp threads, helping maintain proper fabric tension and weave quality during production.
[D03D 51/28] electrical -- **Definition:** This place covers:  E.g. optical warp stop motions.
[D03D 51/30] wherein droppers are suspended on individual warp threads or small groups of threads -- D03D 51/30 classifies woven-fabric weaving arrangements in which the droppers that release weft yarns are hung from single warp threads or from small bundles of warp threads. This subcategory falls under the broader groups of textiles, weaving, woven fabrics, and driving, starting, or stopping mechanisms.
[D03D 51/32] Detector healds -- The D03D 51/32 subclass covers devices that sense the presence, position or condition of healds in a loom, such as detectors that monitor warp thread movement, breakage or tension to control the weaving process. These detectors are part of the driving, starting or stopping arrangements used in woven-fabric production.
[D03D 51/34] Weft stop motions -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods and their implementations to detect broken weft yarns and to stop the weaving machine upon this detection.
[D03D 51/36] Centre weft forks -- Centre weft forks are devices positioned in the centre of a loom that control the movement of the weft yarn. They engage or disengage the weft thread at specific points, enabling precise starting, stopping or redirection of the weft during the weaving of woven fabrics. This mechanism is used to create patterned fabrics or to manage complex shuttle motions in textile production.
[D03D 51/38] Side weft forks -- The D03D 51/38 subclass covers side-weft fork mechanisms used in weaving. These devices form part of the driving, starting or stopping arrangements that guide the weft yarn into the shed from the side of a loom, allowing precise control of insertion timing and direction in woven fabrics.
[D03D 51/40] Shuttle stop motions -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods and their implementations to detect broken weft yarns which are inserted by a shuttle and to stop the weaving machine upon this detection.
[D03D 51/42] Loose reed mechanisms -- D03D 51/42 relates to mechanisms that detach the reed from the warp and later re-engage it, enabling rapid stopping or starting of the weaving process. These loose reed devices are part of the drive, starting or stopping arrangements that control reed movement in looms forming woven fabrics.
[D03D 51/44] acting on defective operation of loom mechanisms -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods and their implementations to stop the weaving machine upon detection of failure in the operation of one or more of the loom mechanisms.
[D03D 51/46] of shedding mechanisms -- D03D 51/46 relates to shedding mechanisms in woven-fabric production, covering devices that drive, start or stop the shedding process which raises and lowers warp threads during weaving.
[D03D 7/00] Woven fabrics designed to be resilient, i.e. to recover from compressive stress -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Multi-layered fabrics with elastic yarns connecting the individual layers in particular spacer fabrics.
[D03D 9/00] Open-work fabrics -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Net like fabrics, with spaced apart warp and weft yarns, where the spacing results from the weaving process.
[D03J] AUXILIARY WEAVING APPARATUS -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Auxiliary apparatus combined with or associated with looms of subclass D03D not covered by the groups of that subclass. This aspect is covered by the main group D03J1/00 .  All tools for working on and setting-up the loom. This aspect is covered by the main group D03J3/00 .  Shuttles for weft insertion. This aspect is covered by the main group D03J5/00 .
[D03J 1/00] Auxiliary apparatus combined with or associated with looms -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Apparatuses which are closely associated with the loom but which are not covered by the groups of subclass D03D . These apparatuses are mounted normally on the loom and are often part of the loom.
[D03J 1/001] Cloth or warp beam replacement -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Devices, often carriages, to replace the warp beam or the cloth beam.
[D03J 1/002] Climatic conditioning or removing lint or dust -- The code D03J 1/002 covers auxiliary equipment used with looms that provides climate control or removes lint and dust from the woven material or working area. It includes devices such as air-conditioning, ventilation, dust-extraction or lint-removal mechanisms integrated with or attached to weaving machines.
[D03J 1/003] Devices for lubricating machine parts -- D03J 1/003 covers devices that lubricate machine parts in auxiliary weaving equipment for looms. These mechanisms deliver oils or greases to moving components to reduce wear and ensure smooth operation during textile production.
[D03J 1/004] Detection and repair of broken warp yarns -- This subclass covers systems and methods for locating breakages in the warp threads during weaving and for automatically or manually mending them, including sensors, break-detection devices, and repair mechanisms integrated with loom apparatus.
[D03J 1/005] Displays or displaying data -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Displaying weft insertion data for pneumatic looms (i.e. weft projected in the shed by gas jet) -> D03D47/3093
[D03J 1/006] Controlling a group of looms -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The control of a group of looms, often according to an optimisation routine.
[D03J 1/007] Fabric inspection on the loom and associated loom control -- This CPC subclass relates to auxiliary equipment integrated with looms that inspects fabric during weaving and provides real-time control based on the inspection results. It includes sensors, cameras and control mechanisms that monitor yarn placement, density and defects while the cloth is on the loom, enabling automatic adjustments to maintain quality.
[D03J 1/008] Cooling systems -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Cooling systems present on machines of classes D02H (i.e. warpers, beaming machines etc), D03C (i.e. shedding mechanisms) and D03D (i.e. looms).
[D03J 1/02] for treating warp, e.g. cleaning, moistening -- The D03J 1/02 class covers auxiliary devices attached to looms that treat the warp thread during weaving, such as systems for cleaning, moistening, or otherwise conditioning the warp before it passes through the shed. It falls under Textiles → Weaving → Auxiliary Weaving Apparatus.
[D03J 1/04] for treating weft -- The CPC subclass D03J 1/04 falls under Textiles → Weaving → Auxiliary Weaving Apparatus and covers auxiliary devices that are combined with or associated with looms for treating the weft, such as mechanisms that guide, tension, or condition the weft yarn during the weaving process. These treatments improve fabric quality and production efficiency.
[D03J 1/06] for treating fabric -- The CPC subclass D03J 1/06 covers auxiliary devices that are integrated with or attached to weaving looms for the purpose of treating the fabric during or after the weaving process. Examples include machines that apply chemicals, heat, moisture, or mechanical actions such as padding, calendering or finishing while the material remains on the loom. This subclass is part of the broader group of auxiliary weaving apparatus.
[D03J 1/08] for slitting fabric -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Cutting-off of selvedges -> D03D47/40 , D03D49/70 - Apparatus or processes for cutting, or otherwise severing, specially adapted for the cutting, or otherwise severing, of textile materials longitudinally -> D06H7/04
[D03J 1/10] for indicating pattern to weaver -- The D03J 1/10 subclass covers auxiliary weaving devices that display or project the pattern to be woven, helping the weaver follow the design while operating a loom. Such tools include pattern markers, illuminated guides or schematic displays integrated with loom control systems.
[D03J 1/12] transferring bobbins between loom and winding station -- The CPC subclass D03J 1/12 covers devices that move bobbins from a loom to a winding station or vice versa. These mechanisms are part of auxiliary weaving apparatus and facilitate continuous thread supply and collection during weaving operations. They may include transfer arms, conveyors, or robotic handlers integrated with loom and winding units.
[D03J 1/13] for leasing warp -- The D03J 1/13 class defines auxiliary equipment linked to looms that allows warp yarns to be temporarily leased, exchanged or supplied during weaving, enabling efficient management of warp stock in textile production.
[D03J 1/14] Apparatus for threading warp stop-motion droppers, healds, or reeds -- The D03J 1/14 class defines apparatus that threads warp stop-motion droppers, healds or reeds, auxiliary devices used with looms to guide and insert warp threads during weaving.
[D03J 1/16] Apparatus for joining warp ends -- D03J 1/16 refers to devices that join the ends of warp yarns in a loom, enabling continuous weaving by splicing, knotting or otherwise connecting warp threads before they enter the shedding zone. These auxiliary apparatuses help prevent breaks and maintain fabric quality.
[D03J 1/18] for joining, e.g. tying, a complete series of fresh warp threads to the used warp threads -- The D03J 1/18 entry covers an auxiliary loom device that links a fresh series of warp threads to the already-used warp, allowing the loom to continue weaving without interruption. It provides a method or mechanism for tying or otherwise joining the new warp to the old, maintaining proper tension and alignment during the transition.
[D03J 1/20] Measuring length of fabric or number of picks woven -- This subclass covers auxiliary devices attached to looms that measure the length of fabric produced or count the number of picks woven, providing real-time monitoring of weaving progress.
[D03J 1/22] Temples -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Devices and methods for width-wide spreading of the cloth on the loom.
[D03J 1/223] acting on the full width of cloth -- This subclass covers devices or mechanisms that treat or process a fabric across its entire width while it is on a loom, such as full-width beaters, take-down rollers, or heating elements that act simultaneously over the whole cloth. It is part of the auxiliary weaving apparatus group within the textiles-weaving section of the CPC.
[D03J 1/226] provided with a thread cutter -- The CPC subclass D03J 1/226 relates to auxiliary weaving apparatus that include a thread-cutting function. It covers devices integrated with looms or associated with them for automatically severing warp or weft threads during or after the weaving process, facilitating material handling and production efficiency.
[D03J 1/24] Mirrors or other arrangements for inspecting loom parts -- Mirrors or similar inspection devices are auxiliary fittings attached to or used with looms to enable visual checking of loom components during operation. The classification covers arrangements that improve monitoring of moving parts, reducing the need to stop the machine for manual inspection.
[D03J 2700/00] Auxiliary apparatus associated with looms -- The CPC subclass D03J 2700/00 covers auxiliary apparatus used with looms, including devices that aid in tension control, cloth take-up, stop mechanisms, cleaning and other supporting functions that are not part of the primary weaving process. These items fall under the broader categories of textiles, weaving and auxiliary weaving apparatus.
[D03J 2700/02] Treatment of the weaving material on the loom, e.g. sizing, humidifying, cleaning -- The CPC subclass D03J 2700/02 covers methods applied to the weaving material while it is on the loom, such as sizing, humidifying, or cleaning, to improve its handling or performance. It belongs to the auxiliary weaving apparatus group, which includes devices that assist loom operation but focuses on the treatment processes rather than the machinery itself. This classification is used by both the EPO and USPTO for patent documents relating to on-loom material conditioning.
[D03J 2700/04] Efficiency control of looms -- D03J 2700/04 covers devices and methods that monitor and optimise loom performance, including speed regulation, power consumption tracking and fault detection. These systems are classified under auxiliary weaving apparatus, helping improve productivity and reduce waste in textile manufacturing.
[D03J 2700/06] Auxiliary devices for inspecting, counting or measuring -- The class D03J 2700/06 covers auxiliary devices mounted on looms that inspect the fabric, count threads or otherwise measure dimensions during weaving. It includes sensors, electronic counters and other measuring tools used to monitor product quality and process performance.
[D03J 2700/10] Shuttles -- The CPC entry D03J 2700/10 designates shuttles, the auxiliary devices that transport the weft yarn across the shed of a loom. These components are classified under auxiliary weaving apparatus within the broader weaving subclass of textiles.
[D03J 2700/12] for automatic bobbin changing -- A device integrated with looms that automatically removes spent bobbins and inserts fresh ones, reducing manual handling and downtime during weaving. It falls under auxiliary weaving apparatus for automatic bobbin-changing.
[D03J 2700/14] Gripper shuttles -- Gripper shuttles are auxiliary loom components that use a gripping mechanism to hold and propel the shuttle or weft carrier across the shed, ensuring precise and rapid insertion of the weft yarn. They improve weaving efficiency and reduce shuttle misplacement, and are employed in both shuttle-loom and rapier-type systems.
[D03J 2700/16] with warp or weft stop motion -- The D03J 2700/16 subclass covers auxiliary devices for looms that provide a stop or control motion for the warp or weft threads. Such mechanisms interrupt or regulate yarn feed to prevent breakage, maintain tension, or enable pattern changes during the weaving process.
[D03J 3/00] Weavers' tools, e.g. knot-tying tools -- **Definition:** This place covers:  All tools used for working on and setting-up the loom.
[D03J 3/02] Reed and heald hooks -- Reed and heald hooks are small hand tools used by weavers to insert, remove or adjust reeds and healds within a loom. They facilitate the setting of shed geometry and aid in routine maintenance of weaving equipment.
[D03J 3/04] Shuttle-threading tools -- D03J 3/04 covers shuttle-threading tools, devices used by weavers to insert the weft yarn into the loom's shuttle. These auxiliary weaving apparatus facilitate quick and precise loading of the shuttle, improving efficiency in textile production.
[D03J 5/00] Shuttles -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Shuttles, normally carrying a bobbin, for weft insertion.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Swivel shuttles -> D03D31/00 - Shuttles for smallware looms -> D03D35/005 - Shuttles for circular looms -> D03D37/00 - Selvedge shuttles -> D03D47/46
[D03J 5/02] Construction of shuttle body -- The D03J 5/02 subclass deals with the design and manufacturing of the shuttle body used in loom weaving, covering aspects such as shape, material selection, reinforcement features, and methods of forming solid or hollow constructions to improve durability and performance. It addresses any structural variations that affect the shuttle's function in transporting weft yarns.
[D03J 5/04] Shuttle tips -- Shuttle tips are the end pieces of a weaving shuttle that retain the weft yarn and guide it through the shed, influencing insertion and tension. In the CPC they are classified under D03J 5/04, a subgroup of auxiliary weaving apparatus within the textiles-weaving hierarchy.
[D03J 5/06] Dummy shuttles -- Dummy shuttles are non-functional weaving implements that occupy the shuttle slot in a loom without carrying weft yarn. They are used for testing loom operation, demonstrating weaving processes, or maintaining proper tension and spacing when no real shuttle is needed.
[D03J 5/065] for travelling-wave-shed looms -- D03J 5/065 designates shuttles specifically adapted for travelling-wave-shed looms, a subclass of auxiliary weaving apparatus used in textile manufacturing. It covers the components that carry the weft yarn through the warp during the rapid, back-and-forth motion of this loom type, facilitating efficient pattern formation. This entry falls under the CPC sections TEXTILES > WEAVING > AUXILIARY WEAVING APPARATUS.
[D03J 5/08] Supports for pirns, bobbins, or cops -- The D03J 5/08 entry covers devices that hold or support pirns, bobbins or cops within weaving shuttles, ensuring smooth unwinding of yarn during the weaving process. These supports are a type of auxiliary weaving apparatus used to maintain tension and alignment of the thread as it is fed through the loom.
[D03J 5/10] Pegs or spindles -- The D03J 5/10 subclass includes pegs, spindles and similar auxiliary devices employed in weaving to guide, support or replace the shuttle, helping to tension, position or retain the weft yarn during the insertion process. It falls under auxiliary weaving apparatus within the broader weaving group.
[D03J 5/12] removable -- The D03J 5/12 subgroup concerns shuttles that are removable from a loom, allowing them to be detached for replacement, cleaning or repair. It includes designs where the shuttle can be taken out without dismantling the main weaving mechanism, facilitating quick change-over and maintenance of the weaving apparatus.
[D03J 5/14] pivoted -- The D03J 5/14 subclass covers shuttles that incorporate a pivoted element in their design. These pivoted shuttles are auxiliary weaving apparatus used to alter the shuttle's orientation or direction during the weaving process, improving maneuverability and thread placement.
[D03J 5/16] Bobbin clamps, e.g. for automatic bobbin-changing loom shuttles -- This CPC subclass, D03J 5/16, pertains to bobbin-clamp mechanisms used in automatic bobbin-changing loom shuttles. The devices secure the bobbin within the shuttle and facilitate rapid replacement of empty bobbins with full ones, supporting continuous weaving operation.
[D03J 5/18] for coreless cops, i.e. shuttles having no peg or bobbin clamp -- The CPC subclass D03J 5/18 covers auxiliary weaving apparatus, specifically shuttles designed without a peg or bobbin clamp - known as coreless shuttles. These shuttles hold the weft yarn without a traditional core, allowing smoother insertion and reducing mechanical complexity in textile looms.
[D03J 5/20] Devices in shuttles for threading -- The D03J 5/20 subclass covers mechanisms incorporated in weaving shuttles that assist the insertion of the weft thread, such as thread guides, tension-control devices, or automatic feeding units, enabling smoother and more efficient threading during the weaving process.
[D03J 5/22] for self-threading, i.e. threading automatically when shuttle is picked -- This CPC subclass D03J 5/22 covers shuttle devices for weaving that automatically load their own thread when the shuttle is lifted, eliminating manual threading. It falls under auxiliary weaving apparatus in the textiles hierarchy.
[D03J 5/24] Tension devices -- D03J 5/24 refers to tension devices used in shuttle-type auxiliary weaving apparatus. These devices control yarn tension during weaving, helping maintain consistent fabric quality and prevent yarn breakage. They fall under the broader textile weaving category.
[D04] BRAIDING -- The CPC subclass D04 covers technologies related to braid formation, including methods and apparatus for producing braided structures and their applications in textiles, composites, and other engineered materials. It encompasses the design of braid patterns, the interlacing of multiple filaments or yarns, and the machinery used to create flat, circular, or multilayer braids for reinforcement, decorative, or functional purposes.
[D04B] KNITTING -- **Definition:** This place covers:  weft knitting machines are covered by D04B7/00 - D04B13/00 ,  details of, or auxiliary devices incorporated in such machines are covered by D04B15/00 and  articles made by such machines are covered by D04B1/00  warp knitting machines are covered by D04B23/00 - D04B25/00 , details of, or auxiliary devices incorporated in such machines are covered by D04B27/00 and articles made by such machines are covered by D04B21/00  details of, or auxiliary devices incorporated in knitting machines not limited to a specific kind of knitting machine are covered by D04B35/00  miscellaneous knitting machines and articles made by such machines are covered by D04B39/00  hand knitting equipment is covered by D04B3/00 , D04B5/00 and D04B33/00  auxiliary apparatuses or devices for use with knitting machines are covered by D04B37/00 or for hand knitting equipment are covered by D04B17/00 , D04B19/00 and D04B31/00  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Knitted products of unspecified structure or composition, e.g. wearing apparel, e.g. filters implantable into blood vessels, prostheses etc. -> A41 , A61F - Layered products (i.e. laminates) including knitted articles -> B32B5/00
[D04B 1/00] Weft knitting -- **Definition:** This place covers:  weft knitting processes or fabrics with specific details on materials and/or fabric structure  weft knitted fabrics and weft knitting processes not otherwise provided for, e.g. plain jersey or rib knitting processes and fabrics:  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Warp knitting processes and products -> D04B21/00 - Combined weft and warp knitting -> D04B39/04
[D04B 1/02] Pile fabrics or articles having similar surface features -- **Definition:** This place covers: Fabrics or articles comprising a weft knit ground fabric with knitted in fibres or yarns projecting from one or both fabric faces. Example:  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** This place does not cover: pile fabrics without pile forming material , e.g. fleece, and loft obtained by finishing treatments like napping D10B2403/0111 , D10B2403/0121 in combination with the appropriate D04B (sub)group pile fabrics subjected to napping treatment D06C11/00 piles applied to a preformed ground fabric (e.g. by tufting) D05C pile fabrics forming part of socks or stockings D04B1/26 pile fabrics included in upper parts of panties or pants D04B1/243  **Glossary:** In this place, the following terms or expressions are used with the meaning indicated: Pile fabrics are fabrics having a significant portion of fibres or yarns, different from ground fabric fibres or yarns, projecting from and oriented transverse to one or both ground fabric faces.
[D04B 1/025] incorporating loose fibres, e.g. high-pile fabrics or artificial fur -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Fabrics or articles having loose fibres incorporated during the weft knitting process. Examples:
[D04B 1/04] characterised by thread material -- **Definition:** This place covers:  pile fabrics or articles whereby the properties of the pile thread material alone, or in combination with the pile arrangement, ensure special effects, e.g. enhanced cushioning or thermal shielding.
[D04B 1/06] Non-run fabrics or articles -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Fabrics or articles rendered ravel resistant and/or ladderproof by knitting texture or composition. Example:
[D04B 1/08] characterised by thread material -- **Definition:** This place covers:  non-run fabrics or articles whereby the properties of a thread material alone, or in combination with the stitch pattern, ensure ravel resistance and render the fabric or article ladderproof.
[D04B 1/10] Patterned fabrics or articles -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Fabrics or articles wherein the non-uniform arrangement of stitches and/or different threads, alone or in combination, ensures the intended effects.
[D04B 11/00] Straight-bar knitting machines with fixed needles -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Flat straight bar weft knitting machines with concurrent stitch formation in a row of stitches.
[D04B 1/102] with stitch pattern  D04B1/06 ,  D04B1/08  take precedence -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Patterned fabrics or articles wherein primarily the knitted texture ensures the intended effects.
[D04B 11/02] with one set of needles -- D04B 11/02 classifies straight-bar knitting machines that use a single set of fixed needles, employed for producing knitted textile structures. The subclass falls under the broader knitting category within textiles and specifies machines where the needle bar is not divided into multiple interchangeable groups.
[D04B 11/025] with stitch-length-regulating mechanism -- This CPC subclass (D04B 11/025) refers to straight-bar knitting machines equipped with a stitch-length-regulating mechanism that enables precise control of yarn feed and fabric dimensions. It lies within the hierarchy textiles → braiding → knitting and addresses machines that adjust stitch length during operation.
[D04B 1/104] Openwork fabric, e.g. pelerine fabrics -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.
[D04B 11/04] with two sets of needles -- The CPC code D04B 11/04 identifies straight-bar knitting machines that employ two sets of fixed needles, allowing simultaneous knitting on both needle beds to produce interlaced or ribbed fabrics. This subclass falls under textiles, braiding, knitting, and specifically straight-bar machines with fixed needles.
[D04B 1/106] at a selvedge, e.g. hems or turned welts -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.
[D04B 11/06] with provision for narrowing or widening to produce fully-fashioned goods -- The CPC subclass D04B 11/06 falls under textiles, braiding and knitting and pertains to straight-bar knitting machines with fixed needles that incorporate a mechanism for narrowing or widening the fabric, enabling the production of fully-fashioned goods directly on the machine. This capability allows shape-forming such as sleeves or cuffs without subsequent cutting or seaming.
[D04B 1/108] Gussets, e.g. pouches or heel or toe portions -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group:
[D04B 11/08] with provision for incorporating pile threads -- D04B 11/08 covers straight-bar knitting machines with fixed needles that include a built-in system for adding pile threads during knitting, allowing the production of pile fabrics such as terry or boucle directly on the machine.
[D04B 11/10] with provision for incorporating internal threads in laid-in fabrics -- D04B 11/10 relates to straight-bar knitting machines with fixed needles that are designed to incorporate internal threads into laid-in fabrics, allowing the creation of reinforced or multifunctional textile structures.
[D04B 11/12] for producing fabrics from, or incorporating, elastic threads -- This CPC subclass D04B 11/12 relates to methods for producing knitted fabrics on straight-bar machines with fixed needles that use or incorporate elastic yarns, enabling the resulting textile to stretch and regain its shape.
[D04B 11/14] with provision for changing the fabric construction, e.g. from plain to rib-loop fabric -- Straight-bar knitting machines with fixed needles that include a mechanism allowing the operator to switch the stitch formation, enabling the machine to produce different fabric constructions such as plain, rib or loop structures without changing the hardware. This capability provides flexibility in textile production while using a single machine setup.
[D04B 11/16] with special provision for commencing goods, e.g. with non-run edges -- The subclass D04B 11/16 covers straight-bar knitting machines with fixed needles that include a special provision for starting the knitting process when the work edge is not pre-rolled, such as mechanisms for handling non-run edges. It relates to textile equipment that can commence fabric formation without the need for a continuous edge feed.
[D04B 11/18] for producing patterned fabrics -- D04B 11/18 designates straight-bar knitting machines that have fixed needles and are employed to produce patterned fabrics. The subclass is situated within the textiles hierarchy under braiding and knitting and pertains to equipment and methods for forming repeatable designs during the knitting process.
[D04B 1/12] characterised by thread material -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Fabrics or articles whereby the properties of a thread material in combination with the stitch pattern ensure intended effects. Example:
[D04B 11/20] with colour patterns -- D04B 11/20 classifies straight-bar knitting machines with fixed needles that are designed to produce fabrics featuring colour patterns, such as multi-yarn or dyed-yarn knitting where different colours are combined in a predetermined design during the knitting process. This subclass covers the machinery and associated methods for creating such patterned knit textiles.
[D04B 11/22] with stitch patterns -- D04B 11/22 relates to straight-bar knitting machines that have fixed needles and are capable of forming stitch patterns. It covers the mechanisms and processes for producing patterned knitted fabrics on such equipment, typically used in textile production.
[D04B 1/123] with laid-in unlooped yarn, e.g. fleece fabrics  with elastic weft yarn  D04B1/18 -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative examples of subject matter covered by this group.
[D04B 11/24] with provision for transfer of knitted goods from one machine to another -- **Definition:** This place covers:  straight bar knitting machines with the provision for transfer of knitted goods from one machine to another whereby the other machine may form part of the knitting machine but performs a special function, e.g. rib knitting, not performed on the knitting head.
[D04B 1/126] with colour pattern, e.g. intarsia fabrics -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative examples of subject matter classified in this group.  **Glossary:** - Intarsia: is a knitting technique used to create patterns with multiple colours. There is only one active colour on any given stitch.
[D04B 11/26] specially adapted for producing goods of particular configuration -- D04B 11/26 covers straight-bar knitting machines whose fixed needles are specially adapted to produce items of a particular shape or configuration, such as shaped panels, gussets or 3-D forms. The subclass addresses machine designs and knitting actions that enable the creation of goods with predefined geometries rather than flat fabric.
[D04B 11/28] stockings, or portions thereof -- The D04B 11/28 subgroup pertains to the production of stockings or parts of stockings using straight-bar knitting machines that have fixed needles, a method suited to seamless hosiery manufacturing within the broader textile knitting hierarchy.
[D04B 11/30] non-run stockings -- D04B 11/30 pertains to non-run stockings made on straight-bar knitting machines that have fixed needles, a specific sub-category of knitting within the CPC's textile and braiding hierarchy. The entry covers hosiery designed to retain its shape and avoid sagging during wear.
[D04B 11/32] welts, e.g. double or turned welts -- The CPC subclass D04B 11/32 covers the creation of welts-edge or decorative folds such as double or turned welts-on fabrics produced with straight-bar knitting machines that use fixed needles. It classifies methods and apparatuses for forming these specific knit structures.
[D04B 11/34] heel or toe portions -- D04B 11/34 relates to the heel or toe portions of articles made on straight-bar knitting machines with fixed needles. It addresses the shaping, construction and attachment of these end sections, covering features such as reinforced areas, stitching patterns and finishing techniques used in items like socks, stockings and similar knitted textiles.
[D04B 11/36] other wearing apparel -- D04B 11/36 covers patents for other wearing apparel made using straight-bar knitting machines that have fixed needles. It falls under the textiles hierarchy of braiding and knitting, specifically for items not covered by more specific garment categories.
[D04B 1/14] Other fabrics or articles characterised primarily by the use of particular thread materials -- **Definition:** This place covers:  fabrics comprising threads providing special properties, e.g. metal yarns, not provided for in groups D04B1/16 - D04B1/20 .
[D04B 1/16] synthetic threads -- **Definition:** This place covers:  fabrics or articles wherein the properties of a thread material in combination with the thread arrangement ensure the intended effects, e.g. cover, hand or resilience.  **Glossary:** - Synthetic threads: are yarns made from or predominantly comprising artificial organic fibres or filaments.
[D04B 1/18] elastic threads -- **Glossary:** - Elastic threads: are threads which fully recover to the initial length from extension by 100 per cent or more.
[D04B 1/20] crimped threads -- **Glossary:** - Crimped threads: are threads having elasticity imparted primarily by the fibre or filament configuration, e.g. by curls or twists.
[D04B 1/22] specially adapted for knitting goods of particular configuration -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Articles requiring adaptation of the standard knitting process and/or the standard knitting machine setup. Examples:
[D04B 1/225] Elongated tubular articles of small diameter, e.g. coverings or reinforcements for cables or hoses -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Warp knitted elongated tubular articles of small diameter -> D04B21/205 - Elongated tubular articles of small diameter made by knitting processes other than weft knitting -> D04B39/00
[D04B 1/24] wearing apparel -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative examples of subject matter classified in this group.
[D04B 1/243] upper parts of panties; pants -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.
[D04B 1/246] Upper torso garments, e.g. sweaters, shirts, leotards -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative examples of subject matter classified in this group.
