[D06G] MECHANICAL OR PRESSURE CLEANING OF CARPETS, RUGS, SACKS, HIDES, OR OTHER SKIN OR TEXTILE ARTICLES OR FABRICS -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods or devices for mechanical or pressure cleaning of textile articles or fabrics.  Methods or devices for turning inside-out tubular or other hollow textile articles.
[D06G 1/00] Beating, brushing, or otherwise mechanically cleaning or pressure cleaning carpets, rugs, sacks, hides, or other skin or textile articles or fabrics -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Suction cleaners (i.e. domestic) -> A47L7/00 , A47L9/00 - Cleaning devices for sheets or webs in connection with printing -> A47L11/00 - Cleaning devices for sheets or webs in connection with printing -> B41F23/002
[D06G 1/005] inside a rotary receptacle -- The CPC subclass D06G 1/005 covers methods for cleaning carpets, rugs, sacks, hides or other textile items by beating, brushing or pressure cleaning within a rotating container. The process uses a rotary drum or similar receptacle to apply mechanical action to the textile during cleaning. This is a subcategory of mechanical or pressure cleaning of textiles.
[D06G 3/00] Turning inside-out flexible tubular or other hollow articles -- **Glossary:** - Flexible tubular or other hollow articles: textile or fabric type articles
[D06G 3/02] by mechanical means -- The D06G 3/02 subclass covers methods that turn flexible tubular or other hollow textile articles inside-out using mechanical means. It falls under mechanical or pressure cleaning of carpets, rugs, sacks, hides, or other skin and fabric items, focusing on the physical manipulation of the article rather than chemical treatment. This technique is applied to improve cleaning efficiency for hollow or tubular textile products.
[D06G 3/04] pneumatically -- D06G 3/04 is a CPC subclass dealing with mechanically cleaning textiles by using pneumatic pressure to turn inside-out flexible tubular or other hollow articles such as carpets, rugs, sacks, hides or similar fabric items. The entry covers methods that invert these hollow textile products with air-driven force during the cleaning process.
[D06G 5/00] Mechanical, vacuum, or pressure cleaning in combination with the turning inside-out of flexible tubular or other hollow articles -- **Glossary:** - Flexible tubular or other hollow articles: textile or fabric type articles
[D06H] MARKING, INSPECTING, SEAMING OR SEVERING TEXTILE MATERIALS -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods or devices for marking textile materials.  Methods or devices for inspecting textile materials.  Methods or devices for seaming textile materials.  Methods or device for cutting or severing textile materials.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Inspecting textile on the loom -> D03J1/007 , D03J1/20 - Slitting fabrics on the loom -> D03J1/08
[D06H 1/00] Marking textile materials -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Decorating textiles -> D06Q1/00
[D06H 1/003] by passing a needle through the layers, e.g. with a marking fluid flowing through the needle -- D06H 1/003 refers to a method of marking textile materials where a needle is driven through the fabric layers and a marking fluid is delivered through the needle to create a visible mark or pattern on the material. This technique is used for identification, decoration, or quality control in textile processing.
[D06H 1/006] Marking frames for large sized textiles, e.g. for drapery pleat or length marking -- The entry D06H 1/006 covers marking frames designed for large-sized textiles, such as drapery, that hold the material in place while providing visual guides for pleat positions or length measurements, aiding precise cutting or sewing operations.
[D06H 1/02] Marking by printing or analogous processes -- D06H 1/02 covers the marking of textile materials by printing or analogous processes, including the application of dyes, inks, pigments or other marking substances to fabrics, yarns or fibers for identification, branding or decorative purposes.
[D06H 1/04] by attaching threads, tags, or the like -- This subclass covers methods of marking textile items by affixing threads, tags, or similar elements to the fabric, enabling identification, branding or traceability. It includes techniques for sewing, stapling or otherwise attaching such markers onto textiles.
[D06H 1/043] by attaching threads -- The CPC subclass D06H 1/043 covers techniques for marking textile materials by affixing threads to the fabric. It includes methods that attach yarn or other filamentary elements to create visible or functional markings, such as logos, identification codes, or decorative patterns, using stitching, embroidery, or similar thread-attachment processes. This subclass falls under the broader category of textile treatment involving marking, inspecting, seam­ing or severing.
[D06H 1/046] by attaching tags -- This CPC subclass relates to the marking of textile materials through the attachment of tags. It includes techniques for affixing identification, care, or branding labels to fabrics, garments, or yarns, typically by sewing, stapling, or adhesive means. The classification is used for patents concerning tag-based marking processes applied to textiles.
[D06H 2201/00] Inspecting textile materials -- This subclass covers methods and apparatus for examining the condition, quality, composition, or dimensions of textile fibers, yarns, fabrics, or finished garments. Techniques may include visual, mechanical, optical, or electronic inspection, as well as systems for detecting defects, measuring properties, or verifying markings. It falls under the broader category of marking, inspecting, seaming or severing textile materials.
[D06H 2201/10] by means of television equipment -- The subclass D06H 2201/10 relates to methods and apparatus for inspecting textile materials using television equipment, such as cameras, video monitors or other visual-display systems, to detect defects, gauge quality or monitor processing in real time.
[D06H 3/00] Inspecting textile materials -- D06H 3/00 covers methods and apparatus for inspecting textile materials to detect defects, assess quality, or measure physical and chemical properties during or after production. It includes visual, optical, electronic and sensor-based techniques for checking fabric integrity, colour, weave, thickness, and related characteristics.
[D06H 3/02] visually -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Inspecting textile materials by the eyes of an operator.
[D06H 3/04] wherein the material is supported on a table -- The D06H 3/04 subgroup deals with inspecting textile materials while they are placed on a flat table, allowing visual or instrumental examination of the fabric's surface, dimensions, or defects. It covers methods and devices that require the material to be supported on a table during the inspection process.
[D06H 3/06] wherein the material is supported on a drum -- The subclass D06H 3/06 covers inspection of textile material when the fabric is mounted on a drum, typically a rotating one, to enable continuous monitoring of properties such as defects, dimensions, colour or tension. It pertains to methods or devices that support the material on the drum during the detection process, fitting within the broader group for marking, inspecting, and seam-or-severing textile materials.
[D06H 3/08] by photo-electric or television means -- D06H 3/08 covers techniques for inspecting textile materials using photo-electric or television devices, such as light sensors, cameras or video analysis systems that detect flaws, measure dimensions or assess fabric quality during production or after processing.
[D06H 3/10] by non-optical apparatus -- The CPC subclass D06H 3/10 pertains to methods and devices for inspecting textile materials that do not rely on optical means. It includes mechanical, tactile, acoustic, electrical or other non-visual techniques for detecting defects, measuring properties or checking quality during processing or after manufacture.
[D06H 3/12] Detecting or automatically correcting errors in the position of weft threads in woven fabrics -- This subclass covers techniques for identifying mispositions of weft threads in woven fabrics and automatically correcting those errors, often by using optical or electronic inspection systems that detect thread displacement and adjust the weave in real time.
[D06H 3/125] Detecting errors in the position of weft threads -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Correcting errors in the position of weft threads -> D06H3/12
[D06H 3/14] Detecting and removing metal particles, e.g. broken needles, or card clothing, from fabrics -- The CPC subclass D06H 3/14 covers methods and apparatus for detecting metal particles such as broken needles or bits of card clothing in fabrics and for removing them during textile processing. It is used to ensure product quality and prevent damage to downstream equipment or end-use garments.
[D06H 3/16] Inspecting hosiery or other tubular fabric -- The D06H 3/16 subclass covers methods and devices for examining hosiery or other tubular fabrics, focusing on detecting defects, measuring dimensions, and assessing material quality. It includes visual, optical or sensor-based inspection techniques applied to the interior and exterior of tubular textile products. The subclass is part of the broader category dealing with marking, inspecting, sewing or severing textile materials.
[D06H 3/165] Devices for supplying, removing or stacking the work -- CPC D06H 3/165 covers apparatus used in textile inspection that moves the material through the inspection station, including feeders, ejectors and stacking mechanisms that supply, remove or accumulate the workpiece. Such devices enable continuous handling of fabrics for quality checking, trimming or marking operations.
[D06H 5/00] Seaming textile materials -- The CPC subclass D06H 5/00 covers methods and apparatuses for joining textile materials, including stitching, bonding, welding, or other techniques used to create seams in fabrics, garments, or similar textile products. It falls under the broader categories of textile treatment, marking, inspecting, and severing, and addresses the mechanical or chemical processes that securely fasten textile pieces together.
[D06H 5/001] Devices or apparatus for connecting back and forward ends of fabrics, e.g. for continuous processing -- The D06H 5/001 class covers devices or apparatus that join the back and forward ends of fabric to enable continuous processing, such as splicers, seamers or other joining machines used in textile production lines. These tools are employed to create a seamless feed of material for looms, knitting machines or other fabric-handling equipment.
[D06H 5/002] for tubular fabrics -- D06H 5/002 covers methods and devices for joining or sealing the edges of tubular textile products, such as hoses, sacks, or tube-shaped fabrics, by stitching, welding, adhesive bonding or other seaming techniques. It is classified under the broader groups dealing with treatment of textiles and the seaming of textile materials, specifically addressing how tubular fabrics are assembled or repaired.
[D06H 5/003] Devices or apparatus for joining the longitudinal edges of fabrics -- D06H 5/003 covers devices or apparatus designed to join the longitudinal edges of fabrics, such as sewing machines, edge-tape applicators, or ultrasonic welders used in textile manufacturing. The classification belongs to the treatment of textiles, specifically marking, inspecting, seaming, or severing textile materials.
[D06H 5/005] for making a tubular fabric -- This CPC subclass covers methods and apparatus for forming a tubular fabric by seaming textile strands, typically used in knitting or weaving processes to produce tubes for garments, medical tubing, or industrial hoses. It falls under textile treatment, specifically seaming.
[D06H 5/006] by folding the fabric longitudinally -- The D06H 5/006 subclass covers methods of seaming textile materials in which the fabric is first folded longitudinally, aligning the edges before they are joined. This technique creates a continuous seam by bonding or stitching the folded edges, facilitating efficient assembly and reinforcing the joint along the length of the material.
[D06H 5/007] by helically winding the fabric -- D06H 5/007 describes a seaming method in which textile material is joined by helically winding the fabric to form a continuous stitch, providing a seam without using straight or conventional stitching techniques.
[D06H 5/008] Making sacks from tubular fabrics -- D06H 5/008 covers processes for producing sacks by shaping and sealing tubular fabrics without conventional seams, including cutting, closing, and finishing steps that convert a continuous tube into a bag. The methods are used for making flexible containers such as bags for bulk goods and packaging.
[D06H 7/00] Apparatus or processes for cutting, or otherwise severing, specially adapted for the cutting, or otherwise severing, of textile materials -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Among others, details of cutting devices as such, even if disclosed in combination with another fabric process.
[D06H 7/005] characterized by means or processes to avoid or reduce fraying of the cut edge -- This CPC subclass covers devices or methods used during the cutting or severing of textile materials that incorporate features to prevent or minimise fraying of the cut edge. It includes any means such as severing tools, cutting actions, or post-cut treatments designed to keep the fiber ends intact.
[D06H 7/02] transversely
[D06H 7/025] in line with an embossed or a raised pattern on the fabric; Cutting pile fabric along a loopless or napless zone, e.g. the plain woven portion of towel cloth -- The subclass D06H 7/025 covers methods or machines that cut pile fabrics by following an embossed or raised pattern on the material, such as severing the yarns along a loop-less or nap-less zone like the plain-woven section of a towel.
[D06H 7/04] longitudinally
[D06H 7/045] in line with an embossed or a raised pattern on the fabric; Cutting pile fabric along a loopless or napless zone  D06H7/06 ,  D06H7/16  take precedence -- This subclass covers machines or methods that cut pile fabrics by following an embossed or raised pattern, aligning the cut with loopless or napless zones of the material. It falls under marking, inspecting, seam-ing or severing textile materials, and is subordinate to the broader groups D06H 7/06 and D06H 7/16.
[D06H 7/06] Removing selvedge edges -- D06H 7/06 covers devices or methods used to trim the selvedge-the finished edge of a fabric-by cutting or otherwise separating it from the main textile material. It falls under the broader category of textile treatment involving marking, inspecting, and severing processes.
[D06H 7/08] for cutting tubular fabric longitudinally -- The CPC subclass D06H 7/08 covers apparatus or processes specifically designed to cut tubular fabric along its length. It falls under the broader categories of textiles, their treatment, and methods for marking, inspecting, sewing or severing textile materials. This subclass is used for inventions that longitudinally cut tubular fabrics.
[D06H 7/10] obliquely -- D06H 7/10 covers apparatus or processes that cut or sever textile materials by an oblique or angled action rather than a straight cut. It includes devices specially adapted to make slanted incisions for sewing, trimming, or shaping fabrics.
[D06H 7/12] cutting a tubular fabric helically -- D06H 7/12 covers methods and devices for cutting tubular fabric in a helical manner, enabling continuous or sectional removal of material from round or sleeve-shaped textiles. This subclass falls under textile treatment processes focused on severing fabric, typically applied in garment, hose or technical textile production.
[D06H 7/14] Cutting fabrics by cutting the weft or warp threads while making special provision to avoid cutting warp or weft threads, respectively, adjacent to the cut -- The CPC subclass D06H 7/14 covers methods for cutting textile fabrics that separate either the weft or the warp threads while taking special measures to prevent cutting the adjacent warp or weft threads. It applies to apparatus or processes designed to sever the selected yarn set cleanly, preserving the integrity of the surrounding weave.
[D06H 7/16] specially adapted for cutting lace or embroidery -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Apparatus specially adapted for or processes for cutting lace or embroidery completely.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Apparatus or processes for shearing textile surfaces -> D06C13/00
[D06H 7/18] Cutting rolls of material without unwinding -- The subclass covers devices and methods that cut a continuous roll of textile or similar material while it stays wound, eliminating the need to unwind the web. It includes cutting rolls directly with integrated knives, shears or other severing tools designed for precision severing of the material in situ.
[D06H 7/20] Tearing -- D06H 7/20 covers apparatus or processes specifically designed for tearing textile materials, a subclass of cutting and severing operations used to separate fabric. It includes devices that apply a controlled tear rather than a clean cut, typically for creating ragged edges, opening seams or preparing fabrics for further processing.
[D06H 7/22] Severing by heat or by chemical agents -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Working by laser beam e.g. welding, cutting, boring of fabrics -> B23K26/402 , B23K2103/38
[D06H 7/221] by heat -- D06H 7/221 covers apparatus and processes that cut or otherwise sever textile materials by applying heat. The classification includes methods such as laser cutting, hot-knife or thermal blade techniques, and any heat-based means that melt, burn, or otherwise separate fibers, particularly for synthetic or heat-sensitive fabrics. The focus is on the use of thermal energy to achieve precise severing without mechanical blades.
[D06H 7/223] using ultrasonic vibration -- D06H 7/223 concerns the use of ultrasonic vibration in devices or methods that cut or otherwise sever textile materials. It falls under the CPC group for apparatus or processes for cutting, especially adapted for textile treatment, and is part of the hierarchy TEXTILES - TREATMENT OF TEXTILES OR THE LIKE - MARKING, INSPECTING, SEAMING OR SEVERING TEXTILE MATERIALS.
[D06H 7/225] using meltable yarns or interwoven metal wires heated by an electric current -- D06H 7/225 pertains to apparatus or processes for cutting or otherwise severing textile materials that use meltable yarns or interwoven metal wires heated by an electric current to achieve the cut, providing a specialized method for precise textile separation.
[D06H 7/226] by chemical agents -- D06H 7/226 covers methods of cutting or otherwise severing textile materials that employ chemical agents, such as solvents, acids or enzymes, to weaken or dissolve the fibers before or during the cutting process. This subclass falls under apparatus or processes specially adapted for cutting textiles.
[D06H 7/228] using dissolvable separator yarns -- The D06H 7/228 subclass concerns methods and apparatus that use dissolvable separator yarns to facilitate cutting or otherwise severing textile materials, with the yarn being removed after the operation.
[D06H 7/24] Devices specially adapted for cutting-out samples -- D06H 7/24 covers devices specifically designed to cut out sample pieces from textile materials. These tools are adapted for precise severing of fabrics to obtain representative sections for testing, inspection or further processing. They may include knives, cutters, or automated equipment that ensures consistent sample size and shape.
[D06J] PLEATING, KILTING OR GOFFERING TEXTILE FABRICS OR WEARING APPAREL -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods for pleating, kilting or goffering textile fabrics or wearing apparel including of mechanical or physical processing with or without using heat, steam or treatment composition and also the combination thereof.  Apparatus for pleating, kilting or goffering textile fabrics or wearing apparel such as fabric folding mechanisms, pleat forming devices, pleating machines, including kilting machines, pressing patterns, ironing devices specially adapted for forming goffers or pleats, e.g. fluting irons, pleating irons or goffers.  Illustrative examples of the subject matter classified in this place:  1. Pleating  Pleating is a method of an arrangement of fold by doubling the material upon itself and then pressing it into place. The Figure illustrates pleats (13) were formed via rollers (6) and (10).  2. Kilting  Kilting is a method of a perpendicular arrangement of flat, single plaits, each plait being folded so as to cover half the breadth of the preceding one (generally to make three thicknesses of stuff), as on kilt. A system of pleating a kilt in which one full set of the tartan pattern is taken up in each pleat, resulting in a reproduction of the pattern across the back of the kilt. Box pleats or knife pleats are used for pleating a kilt.  3. Goffering  Goffering is the process of creating frills in textile fabrics or wearing apparel so that they are evenly and finely gathered, e.g. make wavy by means of heated goffering iron, or other forms of goffers by pressing or stamping with patterned tools as shown.  4. Pressing or stamping device  5. Goffering scissors  6. Herringbone goffer pattern  7. Forms of pleats or the like, e.g. accordion pleats, kilt pleats, box pleats or goffers  In Figure 7: (a) plain folds or pleats, (b) double folds or pleats, (c) box pleats, (d) double box pleats, (e) rolled pleats and (f) military rolled pleats.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Pleating or kilting textile fabrics or wearing apparel, by sewing -> D05B  **Glossary:** - goffer (also gauffer): an iron or a heated tool used for pressing ridges or narrow pleats; to make wavy, flute or crimp with a heated iron. An ornamental frill made by pressing pleats; a series of pleats, crimps or flutes. - kilt pleat: a perpendicular arrangement of flat, single plaits, each plait being folded so as to cover half the breadth of the preceding one - kilt: a garment with pleats - kilting: providing a garment with kilt pleats - pleat: a fold in cloth made by doubling the material upon itself and then pressing it into place. Pleats can be pressed (i.e. ironed), non-pressed for round folds or heat-set for sharp creases. - textile fabrics: woven, knitted and non-woven textile fabrics
[D06J 1/00] Pleating, kilting or goffering textile fabrics or wearing apparel -- The D06J 1/00 entry covers processes and devices for forming permanent folds in textiles, including pleating, kilting and goffering of fabrics and garments. It addresses techniques that create structured, decorative, or functional folds by mechanical, thermal or chemical means, and the equipment used to produce such treated textile products.
[D06J 1/02] continuously and transversely to the direction of feed -- This subclass covers methods of pleating, kilting or goferring textile fabrics or garments where the material is processed continuously and the forming action is applied transversely to the feed direction, creating folds across the width as the fabric moves. It includes apparatus and techniques that produce regular or irregular transverse pleats, kilts or goffers during continuous fabrication.
[D06J 1/04] by co-operating ribbed or grooved rollers or belts -- The subclass D06J 1/04 covers pleating, kilting or goffering of textile fabrics or garments by using cooperating ribbed or grooved rollers or belts that shape the material into the desired folds or texture.
[D06J 1/06] by reciprocating blades -- The D06J 1/06 subclass covers processes for pleating, kilting or goffering textile fabrics or apparel in which the material is shaped by reciprocating blades that move back-and-forth across the cloth to create regular folds or gathers, typically in a machine designed for high-speed, repeatable folding operations.
[D06J 1/08] which introduce the folds between rollers or belts -- This CPC subgroup covers processes and machines that create pleats, kilts or goffes in textiles by feeding the material between rollers or belts that form the folds, rather than using needles, heat or other means. The technique is used for fabrics, garments or other sheet materials to produce regular or decorative folds.
[D06J 1/10] continuously and longitudinally to the direction of feed -- This subclass covers methods of pleating, kilting or goffering fabrics in which the operation is performed continuously along the feed direction of the material, i.e., longitudinally. The technique creates rows of folds as the fabric moves through the machine without stopping.
[D06J 1/12] Forms of pleats or the like -- The D06J 1/12 subclass defines the specific shapes, configurations and methods used to form pleats, kilts or goffered folds in textile fabrics and garments, addressing the geometry, spacing and pattern of the pleated structures.
[D06L] DRY-CLEANING, WASHING OR BLEACHING FIBRES, FILAMENTS, THREADS, YARNS, FABRICS, FEATHERS OR MADE-UP FIBROUS GOODS -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Dry-cleaning or industrial washing of fibres, filaments, threads, yarns, fabrics, feathers, or made-up fibrous goods using organic, inorganic or aqueous baths. Processes include desizing or chemical preparation for subsequent chemical treatments like dyeing or the application of finishing agents. Furthermore, it includes the regeneration of used chemical baths, working under pressure in closed vessels, processes combined with mechanical means, and multi-step processes.  Bleaching fibres, filaments, threads, yarns, fabrics, feathers, or made-up fibrous goods, leather, or furs using, for example, reducing agents, enzymes, irradiation, ozonisation or optical bleaching and compounds which develop oxygen or which contain halogen.  **Glossary:** - Fibre: Fibre is a relatively short, elongated member or natural or artificial material. - Filament: Filament is an endless or quasi-endless, elongated member of natural or man-made material; - Thread: Thread is an assembly of yarns or filaments usually produced by twisting. - Yarn: Yarn is a unitary assembly of fibres or filaments, usually produced by spinning. - Fabric: Fabric is a planar textile structure produced by interlacing yarns, threads, fibers or filaments by a weaving, knitting or non-woven process. - Bleaching: Process of improving the whiteness of textile materials, with or without the removal of natural colouring matter and/or extraneous substances, by a bleaching agent - Brightening: Process of making textile materials brighter by using a brightener or optical brightening agent - Optical bleaching or brightening: Processes that give textile materials the effect of great whiteness and brightness by the use of optical brightening agents - Optical brightener, optical whitener, brightening agents, fluorescent brightener, fluorescent whitening agent: A substance that is added to an uncoloured or a coloured textile material to increase the apparent reflectance in the visible region by conversion of ultraviolet radiation into visible light and so to increase the apparent brightness or whiteness Optical brighteners, optical brightening agents (OBAs), fluorescent brightening agents (FBAs) or fluorescent whitening agents (FWAs) are chemical compounds that absorb light in the ultraviolet and violet region (usually 340-370 nm) of the electromagnetic spectrum, and re-emit light in the blue region (typically 420-470 nm). Fluorescent activity is a short term or rapid emission response, unlike phosphorescence, which is a delayed emission. These additives are often used to enhance the appearance of colour of fabric and paper, causing a "whitening" effect, making materials look less yellow by increasing the overall amount of blue light reflected. FLUORESCENT BRIGHTENING AGENTS are colourless fluorescent dyes. They are also called OPTICAL BRIGHTENERS. In accordance with their definition as "colourless" fluorescent dyes, Their colouristic performance is that of fluorescent dyes, without absorbing light in the visible range of the spectrum. They absorb ultraviolet rays present in incident sunlight. This absorbed ultraviolet light is re-emitted by the FLUORESCENT BRIGHTENING AGENT, which is of longer wavelength than the incident ultraviolet light and is known as the fluorescence light. This generally falls in the blue region of the visible spectra. The fluorescence light is then superimposed on the reflected light thereby restoring the balance of different colours and hence producing brilliant white effect. - Differential optical brightening: Process to optically brighten fibres which are of the same basic type, e.g. nylon, but with different optical brightening properties.
[D06L 1/00] Dry-cleaning or washing fibres, filaments, threads, yarns, fabrics, feathers or made-up fibrous goods -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The dry cleaning or the industrial washing like desizing of textile materials as with organic or inorganic solvents, aqueous baths and compositions therefor.  They are thus prepared for subsequent treatment.  Domestic dry cleaning processes and compositions.  The regeneration of used chemical baths.
[D06L 1/01] using only solid or pasty agents -- The subclass D06L 1/01 covers cleaning of fibres, filaments, threads, yarns, fabrics, feathers or made-up fibrous articles by means of solid or pasty cleaning agents such as powders, pastes or gels, rather than liquid solutions. It includes methods where the dry-cleaning or washing process relies exclusively on these non-fluid compositions to remove soils, stains or contaminants.
[D06L 1/02] using organic solvents -- D06L 1/02 covers the use of organic solvents for dry-cleaning or washing fibres, filaments, threads, yarns, fabrics, feathers or other made-up fibrous goods. It is part of the CPC group for textile treatment methods that employ solvent-based processes.
[D06L 1/04] combined with specific additives -- D06L 1/04 covers processes for dry-cleaning, washing or bleaching of fibres, filaments, threads, yarns, fabrics, feathers or other made-up fibrous goods where the cleaning or bleaching step is performed together with particular additives such as detergents, bleaches, conditioners or other agents that enhance the treatment.
[D06L 1/06] De-sizing -- De-sizing is the removal of sizing agents applied to fibres, filaments, threads, yarns or fabrics prior to subsequent processing such as dyeing or finishing. The operation typically uses aqueous, chemical or enzymatic treatments to clean the material and restore its absorbent properties, facilitating later treatment steps.
[D06L 1/08] Multi-step processes -- This CPC subclass covers methods where dry-cleaning, washing or bleaching of textile materials is performed in more than one distinct stage, such as sequential cleaning, rinsing, drying or other treatment steps. It applies to processes that combine several operations to achieve the final cleaning effect.
[D06L 1/10] Regeneration of used chemical baths -- The CPC subclass D06L 1/10 covers methods for regenerating spent chemical baths used in dry-cleaning, washing, or bleaching of fibres, filaments, threads, yarns, fabrics, feathers or other made-up fibrous goods. It focuses on restoring or recycling these solutions so they can be reused, minimizing waste and operational costs.
[D06L 1/12] using aqueous solvents -- The sub-class deals with dry-cleaning or washing processes that employ water-based or other aqueous cleaning agents for fibres, yarns, fabrics and similar fibrous goods. It includes procedures where the cleaning medium is primarily water or water mixed with other liquids. The focus is on the use of aqueous solvents rather than organic solvents.
[D06L 1/14] De-sizing -- De-sizing is the removal of size chemicals such as starch, resin or polymers that were applied to fibres, yarns or fabrics to protect them during weaving or handling. The process typically uses washing, chemical or enzymatic treatment to prepare the material for subsequent dyeing, printing or finishing. In the CPC it is filed under code D06L 1/14 within the dry-cleaning, washing or bleaching subclass of textile treatment.
[D06L 1/16] Multi-step processes -- D06L 1/16 covers multi-step cleaning or treatment procedures applied to fibres, filaments, threads, yarns, fabrics, feathers or made-up fibrous goods, where two or more operations such as washing, rinsing, bleaching, drying or other treatments are combined in a single process.
[D06L 1/18] Working under pressure in closed vessels -- This subclass covers textile cleaning methods that use sealed vessels subjected to elevated pressure, such as pressure-cycle washers or autoclave-type equipment, to wash, dry-clean or bleach fibres, threads, fabrics or other fibrous goods while the container remains closed.
[D06L 1/20] combined with mechanical means -- D06L 1/20 covers textile cleaning processes that combine mechanical action with chemical or physical agents, such as agitation, tumbling, pressing or vibrating, to dry-clean, wash or bleach fibres, filaments, threads, yarns, fabrics, feathers or made-up fibrous goods. The subclass specifies that the mechanical means are integral to the cleaning step, improving removal of soils or stains.
[D06L 1/22] Processes involving successive treatments with aqueous and organic agents -- The subclass D06L 1/22 covers textile cleaning methods that combine water-based and solvent-based steps in sequence, such as washing a fabric with an aqueous detergent followed by a treatment with an organic cleaning agent, or vice-versa. It includes any process where successive aqueous and organic exposures are employed to remove soils, stains, or impurities from fibres, yarns, fabrics or made-up textile articles.
[D06L 4/00] Bleaching fibres, filaments, threads, yarns, fabrics, feathers or made-up fibrous goods -- **Definition:** This place covers:  All chemical bleaching processes and compositions using oxidizing, reducing or optical bleaching agents as well as enzymatic compositions; processes which are providing whiter textile materials than non-treated textile materials.  In the present case, bleaching means fading of natural colour and not removal of dyes.
[D06L 4/10] using agents which develop oxygen -- This subclass covers bleaching processes for textiles and related fibrous goods that employ oxygen-generating agents such as peroxides or oxidizing enzymes to develop oxygen in situ for a bleaching effect. It includes methods and compositions where the active agent releases oxygen during treatment.
[D06L 4/12] combined with specific additives -- The D06L 4/12 subclass covers bleaching of fibres, filaments, threads, yarns, fabrics, feathers or other made-up fibrous goods when the bleaching step is carried out together with specific chemical additives such as catalysts, wetting agents or stabilisers, which improve the efficiency, uniformity or protection of the material during washing or dry-cleaning.
[D06L 4/13] using inorganic agents -- This subclass covers bleaching of fibres, filaments, threads, yarns, fabrics, feathers or made-up fibrous goods by means of inorganic agents such as hydrogen peroxide, sodium hypochlorite, calcium hypochlorite or other mineral bleaching compounds, optionally in combination with catalysts or auxiliaries.
[D06L 4/15] using organic agents -- D06L 4/15 covers bleaching processes for fibres, filaments, threads, yarns, fabrics, feathers or other made-up fibrous goods that employ organic bleaching agents such as hydrogen peroxide, peracids, organic peroxides or enzymatic substances. The subclass includes methods, formulations and conditions where these organic chemicals replace inorganic bleach to achieve whitening or colour removal.
[D06L 4/17] in an inert solvent -- The subclass D06L 4/17 covers bleaching of fibres, filaments, threads, yarns, fabrics, feathers or other made-up fibrous goods where the bleaching agent is applied in an inert solvent. It pertains to processes that avoid reactive solvents, using chemically stable media such as nitrogen-filled or hydrocarbon liquids to achieve whitening or de-coloration.
[D06L 4/18] in a gaseous environment -- D06L 4/18 covers bleaching of fibers, filaments, threads, yarns, fabrics, feathers or assembled textile goods by exposing them to a gaseous bleaching medium. The subclass includes methods that employ gases such as chlorine, ozone or other oxidising vapours to achieve whitening or removal of colour. It pertains to treatments performed in a gaseous environment rather than liquid baths.
[D06L 4/20] using agents which contain halogen -- The D06L 4/20 subclass covers bleaching of fibers, filaments, threads, yarns, fabrics, feathers or made-up fibrous goods using halogen-containing agents such as chlorine, bromine or their compounds; it includes the processes, conditions and specific chemicals (e.g., sodium hypochlorite, bleaching powder) employed to achieve whitening or brightening.
[D06L 4/21] combined with specific additives -- D06L 4/21 covers bleaching of fibres, filaments, threads, yarns, fabrics, feathers or other made-up fibrous goods when the bleaching agent is used together with specific additives, such as stabilisers, buffering compounds or agents that control colour development, to enhance efficiency, protect material integrity or achieve particular finish characteristics.
[D06L 4/22] using inorganic agents -- D06L 4/22 covers the bleaching of fibres, filaments, threads, yarns, fabrics, feathers or made-up fibrous goods by means of inorganic bleaching agents such as hydrogen peroxide, sodium hypochlorite or chlorine compounds, applied in dry-cleaning, washing or other treatment processes. This subclass falls under the broader category of textile treatment aimed at improving whiteness or removing colourants.
[D06L 4/23] using hypohalogenites -- The CPC subclass D06L 4/23 concerns methods for bleaching fibres, filaments, threads, yarns, fabrics, feathers or other made-up fibrous goods by using hypohalogenite agents such as chlorine- or bromine-based bleach solutions. It is part of the broader category dealing with dry-cleaning, washing or bleaching of textiles.
[D06L 4/24] using chlorites or chlorine dioxide -- D06L 4/24 covers bleaching of fibres, filaments, threads, yarns, fabrics, feathers or other made-up fibrous goods using chlorite or chlorine-dioxide agents. The method employs these oxidising compounds to remove colour or stains as part of dry-cleaning, washing or other textile treatment processes.
[D06L 4/26] combined with specific additives -- The CPC subclass D06L 4/26 - combined with specific additives - relates to bleaching processes applied to fibres, filaments, threads, yarns, fabrics, feathers or made-up fibrous goods where particular chemical additives are used simultaneously to improve bleaching efficiency, enhance whiteness or protect the material during treatment.
[D06L 4/27] using organic agents -- The subclass D06L 4/27 covers bleaching processes for fibres, filaments, threads, yarns, fabrics, feathers or made-up fibrous goods in which the bleaching medium is an organic agent such as peroxide, peracid or organic solvent. It falls under the broader categories of textile treatment, specifically dry-cleaning, washing or bleaching.
[D06L 4/28] in an inert solvent -- D06L 4/28 covers bleaching of fibres, filaments, threads, yarns, fabrics, feathers or made-up fibrous goods when performed in an inert solvent. The process uses a non-reactive liquid medium to achieve bleaching without chemical interaction, fitting within the CPC hierarchy for textile treatment and cleaning.
[D06L 4/29] in a gaseous environment -- The CPC subclass D06L 4/29 covers bleaching processes for fibres, filaments, threads, yarns, fabrics, feathers or assembled fibrous goods that are carried out in a gaseous environment, such as using chlorine, ozone, nitrogen oxides or other reactive gases, rather than liquid bleaching agents. It is a subcategory of dry-cleaning, washing or bleaching of textiles, focusing on gas-phase chemical treatments to achieve whitening or discoloration.
[D06L 4/30] using reducing agents -- The D06L 4/30 subgroup concerns bleaching of fibres, filaments, threads, yarns, fabrics, feathers or other made-up fibrous goods by means of reducing agents. It covers processes in which a chemical reducer is applied to eliminate colour, stains or impurities, providing an alternative to oxidative bleaching methods.
[D06L 4/40] using enzymes -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The bleaching of textile materials, leather or furs with compositions containing enzymes.
[D06L 4/50] by irradiation or ozonisation -- The D06L 4/50 subclass covers bleaching of fibres, filaments, threads, yarns, fabrics, feathers or made-up fibrous goods by means of irradiation or ozonisation. It falls under the CPC group for treatment of textiles, specifically dry-cleaning, washing or bleaching processes.
[D06L 4/60] Optical bleaching or brightening -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The optical bleaching or brightening of textile materials, i.e. the treatment of textile materials with compounds providing the effect of great whiteness, generally fluorescent colourless dyes which are reflecting more blue light.
[D06L 4/607] in organic solvents -- The D06L 4/607 subclass refers to bleaching processes for fibres, filaments, threads, yarns, fabrics, feathers or other made-up fibrous goods that are carried out in organic solvents rather than aqueous media. It falls under the broader category of dry-cleaning, washing or bleaching of textiles within the Cooperative Patent Classification system.
[D06L 4/614] in aqueous solvents -- This CPC subclass covers bleaching of fibres, filaments, threads, yarns, fabrics, feathers or made-up fibrous goods using water-based solvents. It falls under the broader category for dry-cleaning, washing or bleaching of textiles, and specifically addresses aqueous-solvent bleaching methods.
[D06L 4/621] with anionic brighteners -- D06L 4/621 relates to bleaching of fibres, filaments, threads, yarns, fabrics, feathers or made-up fibrous goods in which anionic brighteners are added to improve whiteness and color stability during the cleaning or bleaching process.
[D06L 4/629] with cationic brighteners -- The code D06L 4/629 covers bleaching processes for fibres, filaments, threads, yarns, fabrics, feathers or made-up fibrous goods in which cationic brighteners are employed to enhance whiteness and colour lift. It falls under the textile treatment hierarchy for dry-cleaning, washing or bleaching.
[D06L 4/636] with disperse brighteners -- The D06L 4/636 code designates bleaching operations for fibres, filaments, threads, yarns, fabrics, feathers or made-up fibrous goods that incorporate disperse brighteners. It falls under the broader category of textile treatment processes aimed at cleaning or whitening, specifically addressing the use of these organic fluorescent agents to enhance brightness during the bleaching stage.
[D06L 4/643] wherein the brightener is introduced in a gaseous environment or in solid phase, e.g. by transfer, by use of powders or by use of super-critical fluids -- This subclass concerns bleaching of fibres, filaments, threads, yarns, fabrics, feathers or other made-up fibrous goods by introducing a brightener in a gaseous environment or in solid form, for example through transfer, powder application or super-critical fluids.
[D06L 4/65] with mixtures of optical brighteners -- D06L 4/65 relates to bleaching of fibres, filaments, threads, yarns, fabrics, feathers or other made-up fibrous goods in which the bleaching composition includes mixtures of optical brighteners, compounds that improve the visual whiteness of the material by fluorescence.
[D06L 4/657] combined with other treatments, e.g. finishing, bleaching, softening, dyeing or pigment printing -- The CPC subclass D06L 4/657 covers processes where bleaching of textile materials is performed together with other post-treatment steps such as finishing, softening, dyeing or pigment printing. It applies to methods treating fibers, filaments, threads, yarns, fabrics, feathers or made-up goods where bleaching is integrated with these additional operations.
[D06L 4/664] Preparations of optical brighteners -- The classification D06L 4/664 within the Cooperative Patent Classification covers preparations of optical brighteners used in bleaching of textiles and related fibrous materials. It pertains to chemical formulations that impart fluorescence to improve whiteness during cleaning processes.
[D06L 4/671] Optical brightening assistants, e.g. enhancers or boosters -- The D06L 4/671 subclass covers agents that enhance the effect of optical brighteners during the bleaching of fibres, threads, yarns, fabrics, feathers or other made-up fibrous goods, such as enhancers or boosters that improve the whitening performance of the treatment.
[D06L 4/679] Fixing treatments in optical brightening, e.g. heating, steaming or acid shock -- The D06L 4/679 subclass covers methods that permanently set optical brighteners on textiles after bleaching, such as applying heat, steam or an acid shock to fix the brightening agents onto fibres, filaments, yarns or fabrics. This ensures durability of the brightening effect during subsequent use and laundering.
[D06L 4/686] Fugitive optical brightening -- The D06L 4/686 subclass concerns temporary, or fugitive, optical brightening agents applied during bleaching of fibres, filaments, threads, yarns, fabrics, feathers or other made-up fibrous goods. It covers compounds that impart a short-term increase in whiteness or brightness which diminishes after washing, exposure to light or subsequent processing steps. This subclass falls under the broader categories of textiles, treatment of textiles, and dry-cleaning, washing or bleaching.
[D06L 4/693] Blueing with mixtures of dyes -- A method for bleaching fibres, filaments, threads, yarns, fabrics, feathers or other made-up fibrous goods that incorporates blueing agents together with other dyes to counteract yellowing and improve whiteness. The technique applies a mixture of dyes during the bleaching stage to obtain a uniform, bright appearance. This process falls under the CPC subgroup D06L 4/693 for blueing with dye mixtures.
[D06L 4/70] Multi-step processes -- D06L 4/70 within CPC pertains to multi-step bleaching processes applied to fibres, filaments, threads, yarns, fabrics, feathers or other made-up fibrous goods. It covers sequences where two or more distinct bleaching or conditioning stages are performed in a single operation, such as pretreatment followed by bleaching or combined chemical and physical steps. The code is used to classify inventions that integrate several bleaching actions in a unified method.
[D06L 4/75] combined with cleaning or washing -- The subclass covers bleaching operations that are performed in conjunction with cleaning or washing of fibres, filaments, threads, yarns, fabrics, feathers or other made-up fibrous goods, where the bleaching step is integrated into the washing cycle or uses combined reagents. Such combined processes aim to improve efficiency, reduce water usage, and achieve uniform colour removal in a single treatment.
[D06M] TREATMENT, NOT PROVIDED FOR ELSEWHERE IN CLASS -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The chemical and physical treatment of fibrous materials in any form like fibres, nanofibers, microfibers, threads, yarns, knits, fabrics, non-wovens, feathers made from organic natural, synthetic macromolecular compounds or carbon to modify their properties or impart specific functions.
[D06M 10/00] Physical treatment of fibres, threads, yarns, fabrics or fibrous goods made from such materials, e.g. by ultrasonic waves, corona discharge, irradiation, electric currents or magnetic fields -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The physical treatment of fibrous materials or the physical treatment of fibrous materials combined with chemical compounds, simultaneously or successively, like treatment with lasers, UV, plasma, microwaves, X-rays or gamma-rays.
[D06M 10/001] Treatment with visible light, infrared or ultraviolet, X-rays -- D06M 10/001 covers the physical treatment of textile materials using electromagnetic radiation such as visible light, infrared, ultraviolet or X-rays. The processes alter the properties of fibres, yarns or fabrics by exposure to these wavelengths, enabling actions such as bleaching, curing, surface modification, sterilisation or other desired changes.
[D06M 10/003] Treatment with radio-waves or microwaves -- A method for processing textiles, fibers, yarns or fabrics by exposing them to radio-waves or microwaves, typically to modify physical or chemical properties such as strength, conductivity, moisture resistance or surface energy. This treatment falls under physical actions not covered elsewhere, including ultrasonic, corona, irradiation or magnetic field techniques, and is classified in the CPC as D06M 10/003.
[D06M 10/005] Laser beam treatment -- The D06M 10/005 class covers laser beam treatment of textiles and similar fibrous materials. It includes using laser radiation to modify surface properties, pattern, cut or cure fibres, yarns, fabrics or other fibrous goods. This physical treatment is classified under treatment of textiles not provided for elsewhere.
[D06M 10/006] Ultra-high-frequency heating -- D06M 10/006 concerns the use of ultra-high-frequency electromagnetic energy, generally 300 MHz to several gigahertz, to heat textile fibers, yarns, fabrics or other fibrous products. The heating can be applied for drying, curing, bonding or other modifications and is performed by equipment that generates a high-frequency field within the material.
[D06M 10/008] Treatment with radioactive elements or with neutrons, alpha, beta or gamma rays -- D06M 10/008 covers methods for treating textiles or fibrous goods by exposure to radioactive elements, neutrons, or ionizing radiation such as alpha, beta or gamma rays. The radiation is used to alter physical or chemical properties of fibers, yarns or fabrics, for example to improve strength, color stability or resistance to wear, and is classified under physical treatments not provided for elsewhere.
[D06M 10/02] Sonic or ultrasonic waves -- The CPC subclass D06M 10/02 falls under treatment of textiles not provided for elsewhere, covering physical processes that apply sonic or ultrasonic energy to fibres, threads, yarns, fabrics or other fibrous materials. It includes methods that use sound waves, ultrasonic vibrations or related acoustic techniques to alter surface properties, improve texture, enhance bonding, or impart functional characteristics.
[D06M 10/025] Corona discharge or low temperature plasma -- This subcategory covers processes that treat textiles using corona discharge or low-temperature plasma to modify surface properties, improve adhesion, or impart new functionality. Methods may involve exposing fibers, yarns or fabrics to ionized gas or electric fields to alter wettability, dye uptake, or antimicrobial activity.
[D06M 10/04] Physical treatment combined with treatment with chemical compounds or elements -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The physical treatment combined with treatment with chemical compounds, both treatments can be carried out simultaneously or consecutively and in any order.  The chemical/physical vapour deposition on textile materials.
[D06M 10/06] Inorganic compounds or elements -- The CPC subclass D06M 10/06 covers the use of inorganic compounds or elements in physical treatments of textiles, such as applying ultrasonic waves, corona discharge, irradiation, electric currents or magnetic fields, where the specific chemical agents are not otherwise classified. This includes substances like metal salts, oxides, or gases employed to modify fibre, yarn or fabric properties during non-chemical processing steps.
[D06M 10/08] Organic compounds -- The D06M 10/08 subclass covers the use of organic compounds in the physical treatment of textile materials, such as fibres, yarns, fabrics or other fibrous goods. It includes substances like dyes, polymeric finishes, surfactants or other organic agents applied by ultrasonic waves, corona discharge, irradiation, electric currents or magnetic fields to modify the properties of the textile.
[D06M 10/10] Macromolecular compounds -- D06M 10/10 designates the use of macromolecular compounds in physical textile treatments not covered elsewhere, such as applying polymeric substances during ultrasonic, corona, irradiation, electrical or magnetic processing of fibers, yarns, fabrics or other fibrous goods to modify their properties or performance.
[D06M 11/00] Treating fibres, threads, yarns, fabrics or fibrous goods made from such materials, with inorganic substances or complexes thereof -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The chemical treatment of fibrous materials with inorganic compounds.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Physical treatment of fibres, threads, yarns, fabrics, or fibrous goods made from such materials; Physical treatment combined with treatment with chemical compounds or elements -> D06M10/00
[D06M 11/01] with hydrogen, water or heavy water -- The CPC subclass D06M 11/01 covers the treatment of textiles with hydrogen, water or heavy water, i.e., processes in which these fluids are applied to fibres, yarns, fabrics or similar fibrous materials, typically for cleaning, bleaching or modifying properties.
[D06M 11/05] with water, e.g. steam -- D06M 11/05 covers processes for treating fibers, yarns, fabrics or other fibrous goods with water, such as steam, where the treatment involves inorganic substances or their complexes. It falls under treatment of textiles not provided elsewhere, focusing on aqueous methods that modify material properties.
[D06M 11/07] with halogens -- The CPC subclass D06M 11/07 covers methods for treating fibers, yarns, fabrics or other textile materials with inorganic substances that contain halogen atoms, applying such halogen-bearing compounds or their complexes to achieve desired chemical or physical modifications.
[D06M 11/09] with free halogens or interhalogen compounds -- This subclass concerns textile treatment methods that employ inorganic reagents containing free halogen atoms or interhalogen compounds, such as chlorine, bromine, iodine or mixtures thereof, to modify fibres, yarns, or fabrics. The processes aim to impart specific chemical or physical properties by reacting the halogen-based agents with the textile material.
[D06M 11/11] with halogen acids or salts thereof -- The D06M 11/11 classification covers processes for treating fibers, yarns, fabrics or other textile goods with inorganic halogen acids-such as hydrochloric, hydrobromic or hydrofluoric acid-or their corresponding salts. It falls under the broader category of textile treatments that are not specified elsewhere, focusing on inorganic chemical applications.
[D06M 11/13] Ammonium halides or halides of elements of Groups 1 or 11 of the Periodic Table -- The D06M 11/13 subclass covers processes in which textiles, yarns or fabrics are treated with ammonium halides or halides of Group 1 (alkali metals) or Group 11 (copper, silver, gold) elements. These inorganic compounds are applied to impart properties such as flame retardancy, antimicrobial action or coloration, and the classification includes any treatment not covered elsewhere in the textile-treatment classes.
[D06M 11/155] Halides of elements of Groups 2 or 12 of the Periodic Table -- The code D06M 11/155 refers to textile treatments that employ halide compounds of Group 2 (alkaline-earth) or Group 12 (zinc-group) elements. These inorganic halides are applied to fibres, yarns or fabrics for purposes such as bleaching, dye-fixing, antimicrobial or other functional finishes. The subclass falls under the broader category of treatments not listed elsewhere.
[D06M 11/17] Halides of elements of Groups 3 or 13 of the Periodic Table
[D06M 11/20] Halides of elements of Groups 4 or 14 of the Periodic Table, e.g. zirconyl chloride
[D06M 11/22] Halides of elements of Groups 5 or 15 of the Periodic Table -- This sub-class covers the use of inorganic halide compounds of elements from periodic-table groups 5 or 15 as treatment agents for fibres, threads, yarns, fabrics or other fibrous goods, typically to impart functional or protective properties.
[D06M 11/24] Halides of elements of Groups 6 or 16 of the Periodic Table, e.g. chromyl chloride -- The D06M 11/24 subclass covers the use of halide compounds of elements from groups 6 or 16 of the periodic table-examples include chromyl chloride-as inorganic agents for treating fibres, threads, yarns, fabrics or other fibrous goods when such treatment is not defined in any other subclass.
[D06M 11/26] Halides of elements of Groups 7 of the Periodic Table -- D06M 11/26 covers the application of halide compounds of Group 7 elements, such as manganese, technetium or rhenium halides, in inorganic treatments of fibres, threads, yarns, fabrics or other fibrous textile goods.
[D06M 11/28] Halides of elements of Groups 8, 9, 10 or 18 of the Periodic Table
[D06M 11/30] with oxides of halogens, oxyacids of halogens or their salts, e.g. with perchlorates -- This subclass concerns textile treatment that uses inorganic halogen compounds, specifically oxides of halogens, halogen oxyacids or their salts such as perchlorates, applied to fibers, yarns, fabrics or other fibrous goods to alter their properties.
[D06M 11/32] with oxygen, ozone, ozonides, oxides, hydroxides or percompounds -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - With water or heavy water -> D06M11/05 - With oxides or oxyacids of halogens -> D06M11/30
[D06M 11/34] with oxygen, ozone or ozonides -- The D06M 11/34 subclass covers processes for treating fibres, threads, yarns, fabrics or similar fibrous goods with inorganic oxygen-containing reagents, specifically oxygen, ozone or ozonides. It is part of the broader textile-treatment group for treatments not covered elsewhere, focusing on inorganic chemical methods.
[D06M 11/36] with oxides, hydroxides or mixed oxides -- This subclass covers processes for treating fibers, threads, yarns, fabrics or other textile goods with inorganic oxides, hydroxides or mixed oxides, addressing treatment methods not covered elsewhere in the textile treatment hierarchy.
[D06M 11/38] Oxides or hydroxides of elements of Groups 1 or 11 of the Periodic Table -- D06M 11/38 concerns the use of oxides or hydroxides of Group 1 (alkali metals) or Group 11 (copper, silver, gold) elements to treat fibers, yarns, fabrics or other textile goods. The treatment applies these inorganic compounds to modify properties such as durability, dye affinity, fire resistance or conductivity.
[D06M 11/385] Saponification of cellulose-acetate -- This subclass covers the chemical conversion of cellulose-acetate fibers, yarns or fabrics by saponification, typically using alkali to hydrolyze acetate groups to cellulose and acetate salts. The process is used to modify surface properties or improve dyeability of the treated textiles.
[D06M 11/40] combined with, or in absence of, mechanical tension, e.g. slack mercerising -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The mercerising of textile materials in any form.
[D06M 11/42] Oxides or hydroxides of copper, silver or gold -- The D06M 11/42 subclass covers textile treatments that employ inorganic oxides or hydroxides of copper, silver or gold, applied to fibres, yarns, fabrics or other fibrous goods to impart properties such as antimicrobial activity, coloration, conductivity or flame-retardancy. It is a narrow category within the broader group for treatments of textiles not provided for elsewhere.
[D06M 11/44] Oxides or hydroxides of elements of Groups 2 or 12 of the Periodic Table -- The subclass covers methods of treating textiles with inorganic oxides or hydroxides derived from Group 2 (alkaline-earth) or Group 12 (zinc, cadmium, mercury) elements. Such treatments are used to modify the fabric's properties, for example to provide flame retardancy, antimicrobial action, or electrical conductivity.
[D06M 11/45] Oxides or hydroxides of elements of Groups 3 or 13 of the Periodic Table -- The D06M 11/45 subclass covers textile treatment processes that employ oxides or hydroxides of elements from Groups 3 or 13 of the periodic table, applied to fibers, yarns, fabrics or similar fibrous goods. It falls under the broader category of "treatment, not provided for elsewhere in class" within textile processing.
[D06M 11/46] Oxides or hydroxides of elements of Groups 4 or 14 of the Periodic Table -- The D06M 11/46 class covers textile treatments that employ inorganic oxides or hydroxides of elements from Group 4 (such as titanium, zirconium, hafnium) or Group 14 (such as silicon, germanium, tin) of the periodic table. These compounds are applied to fibres, yarns, fabrics or similar fibrous products to modify properties such as durability, stain resistance or other functional characteristics, and are not classified elsewhere in the textile treatment hierarchy.
[D06M 11/47] Oxides or hydroxides of elements of Groups 5 or 15 of the Periodic Table

This falls under the broader categories of textiles, their treatment, and miscellaneous inorganic treatment processes.

[D06M 11/49] Oxides or hydroxides of elements of Groups 8, 9,10 or 18 of the Periodic Table -- This subclass covers the use of oxides or hydroxides of transition metals and noble gases - specifically elements from groups 8, 9, 10 or 18 - in the inorganic treatment of textiles, fibers, yarns or fabrics where the treatment is not otherwise classified. Such compounds are applied to modify properties like durability, conductivity, or flame resistance.
[D06M 11/50] with hydrogen peroxide or peroxides of metals -- The D06M 11/50 subclass covers the inorganic treatment of fibres, yarns, fabrics or other textile goods by means of hydrogen peroxide or metal peroxides, such as peroxides of copper, iron or titanium. It is used for bleaching, brightening or modifying the physical-chemical properties of textiles where no other specific treatment is defined.
[D06M 11/51] with sulfur, selenium, tellurium, polonium or compounds thereof -- The CPC subclass D06M 11/51 covers processes for treating textiles with sulfur, selenium, tellurium, polonium or their compounds. It falls under treatment of textiles not elsewhere specified, focusing on applying inorganic substances or complexes of these elements to fibers, yarns, fabrics or other fibrous goods.
[D06M 11/52] with selenium, tellurium, polonium or their compounds -- D06M 11/52 covers textile or fabric treatments in which inorganic selenium, tellurium, polonium or their compounds are applied to fibres, yarns or finished goods, typically to impart specific functional properties such as antimicrobial activity, UV resistance or coloration. This subclass falls under the broader group for miscellaneous textile treatments not covered elsewhere.
[D06M 11/53] with hydrogen sulfide or its salts -- This subclass covers processes for treating fibers, threads, yarns, fabrics or similar textile materials with hydrogen sulfide or its salts, typically to impart sulfur-containing functional groups, improve dye affinity, or modify surface properties. It falls under treatment of textiles not elsewhere specified, focusing on inorganic chemical treatments.
[D06M 11/54] with sulfur dioxide -- The subclass D06M 11/54 covers textile treatments in which fibers, yarns, fabrics or similar fibrous goods are contacted with sulfur dioxide, an inorganic gas used for bleaching, desizing or chemically modifying the material. The process applies sulfur dioxide to alter or improve the physical or chemical properties of the textile product.
[D06M 11/55] with sulfur trioxide -- A method for treating fibers, threads, yarns, fabrics or other textile goods by applying sulfur trioxide, an inorganic reagent, to alter the material's properties such as dye affinity, durability or flame resistance. This falls under the subclass for textile treatments not covered elsewhere, specifically involving inorganic substances or complexes.
[D06M 11/56] Sulfates or thiosulfates other than of elements of Groups 3 or 13 of the Periodic Table -- This CPC subclass covers processes for treating fibres, threads, yarns or fabrics with inorganic sulfate or thiosulfate salts, provided the anion is derived from elements other than those in group-3 or group-13 of the periodic table. The treatment may aim at improving properties such as dye uptake, fire resistance or dimensional stability. It falls under the broader category of textile treatments not otherwise specified.
[D06M 11/57] Sulfates or thiosulfates of elements of Groups 3 or 13 of the Periodic Table, e.g. alums
[D06M 11/58] with nitrogen or compounds thereof, e.g. with nitrides -- D06M 11/58 refers to textile treatments that employ nitrogen-based inorganic compounds, including nitrides, to modify or improve fibers, yarns, or fabrics. The process typically involves applying these inorganic nitrogen species to impart specific properties such as durability, fire resistance, or conductivity.
[D06M 11/59] with ammonia -- This subclass covers processes where fibers, yarns, fabrics or similar textile materials are treated with ammonia, an inorganic compound, to modify properties such as dye uptake, shrink-resistance or flame retardancy. It falls under the broader category of textile treatments not covered elsewhere in the classification.
[D06M 11/60] Ammonia as a gas or in solution -- This subclass covers the use of ammonia, either as a gas or dissolved in a solution, for chemically treating fibers, yarns, fabrics or other textile materials, such as for swelling, cleaning, pH adjustment, or preparing the material for further finishing. It is placed under inorganic treatments not provided for elsewhere in the textile treatment hierarchy.
[D06M 11/61] Liquid ammonia -- The D06M 11/61 subclass covers the use of liquid ammonia in the treatment of textiles, fibers, threads, yarns or fabrics. It relates to processes where ammonia in its liquid state is applied, often to alter physical or chemical properties, such as swelling, cleaning, or preparing the material for further finishing steps.
[D06M 11/62] Complexes of metal oxides or complexes of metal salts with ammonia or with organic amines

The methods are applied when no other more specific textile-treatment categories are appropriate.
[D06M 11/64] with nitrogen oxides -- This subclass covers methods for treating fibers, yarns, fabrics or other textile articles with nitrogen oxides or their complexes, typically to impart functional properties such as fire resistance, coloration, or other chemical modifications. It falls under the broader category of textile treatments not provided for elsewhere, using inorganic substances.
[D06M 11/65] Salts of oxyacids of nitrogen -- The D06M 11/65 class covers the use of nitrogen-oxyacid salts, such as nitrate, nitrite or nitro-complexes, in the inorganic treatment of fibres, yarns, fabrics or other textile materials. It pertains to processes that apply these salts to modify, protect or improve textile properties where the treatment is not covered by more specific categories.

This treatment falls under "treatment, not provided for elsewhere in class" for textiles, addressing chemical modification or finishing with sulfamic-based compounds.
[D06M 11/67] with cyanogen or compounds thereof, e.g. with cyanhydric acid, cyanic acid, isocyanic acid, thiocyanic acid, isothiocyanic acid or their salts, or with cyanamides -- This subclass covers textile treatment processes that apply cyanogen or related compounds - such as cyanhydric acid, cyanuric acid, isocyanic acid, thiocyanic acid, isothiocyanic acid, their salts or cyanamides - to fibers, yarns, fabrics or other fibrous goods. The chemicals are used to modify surface properties, improve dye uptake, confer flame resistance or impart other functional characteristics. [D06M 11/73] with carbon or compounds thereof -- D06M 11/73 covers processes for treating fibers, yarns, fabrics or other fibrous goods with carbon or carbon-containing compounds, typically as inorganic substances or complexes, to impart desired properties. [D06M 11/74] with carbon or graphite -- D06M 11/74 pertains to methods of treating fibers, yarns, fabrics or other fibrous goods with carbon or graphite, applying inorganic carbon-based substances or complexes to impart properties such as conductivity, heat resistance or enhanced durability. [D06M 11/75] with phosgene -- The D06M 11/75 subclass covers textile treatments in which fibres, yarns, or fabrics are reacted with phosgene, an inorganic carbonyl halide, to form carbamoyl or carbonate linkages. This process modifies the material's surface properties, such as flame resistance or dye affinity, and is classified under "treatment, not provided for elsewhere in class."
[D06M 11/76] with carbon oxides or carbonates -- This subclass covers the treatment of fibers, yarns, fabrics or other textile goods with inorganic carbon oxides or carbonates, such as carbon dioxide, carbonic acid, or carbonate salts, applied to alter properties, finish surfaces, or achieve bleaching. It is used for processes that modify textiles through reactions with these carbon-based inorganic compounds.
[D06M 11/77] with silicon or compounds thereof -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The treatment of textiles with silicon or compounds thereof.  This group also covers the treatment of fibrous materials with silicium carbide (SiC).
[D06M 11/78] with silicon -- D06M 11/78 covers the application of silicon-containing inorganic substances or complexes to fibers, yarns, fabrics or other fibrous products for treatment not elsewhere specified. The process imparts properties such as water repellency, softness, or durability through silicon-based finishes. It is a subclass under textile treatment in the Cooperative Patent Classification.

[D06M 11/80] with boron or compounds thereof, e.g. borides -- The CPC subclass D06M 11/80 covers textile treatment processes that use boron or boron-containing compounds, such as borides, applied to fibres, yarns, fabrics or other fibrous goods. It falls under the broader category of treatment of textiles with inorganic substances not provided for elsewhere.
[D06M 11/81] with boron -- D06M 11/81 concerns textile treatments that employ boron-containing inorganic compounds such as boric acid, borates or boron complexes. [D06M 11/83] with metals -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The textile treatment with metal compounds or metal generating compounds, i.e. compounds or compositions which are not necessarily in metallic form at the beginning of the treating process, but are transformed to a metallic form during the process such that a metal is deposited on the fibrous material.
[D06M 11/84] combined with mechanical treatment -- D06M 11/84 covers processes that treat fibres, yarns or fabrics with inorganic substances while simultaneously applying mechanical actions such as mixing, kneading, pressing or other deformation steps, combining chemical-inorganic treatment with physical treatment. This subclass lies under treatment of textiles not provided for elsewhere, focusing on combined inorganic and mechanical processing.
[D06M 13/00] Treating fibres, threads, yarns, fabrics or fibrous goods made from such materials, with non-macromolecular organic compounds -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The treatment of textile materials with non-macromolecular or low molecular weight organic compounds.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Treatment with optical brighteners -> D06L - Treatment with coloured compounds or organic dyes -> D06P
[D06M 13/005] Compositions containing perfumes; Compositions containing deodorants -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The treatment with compositions which are avoiding the development of bad smells or compositions which are covering or masking bad smells odour or composition which are containing perfumes or deodorant molecules.

[D06M 13/144] Alcohols -- D06M 13/144 covers processes for treating textile fibers, yarns, or fabrics using low-molecular-weight organic compounds classified as alcohols, where the alcohol functions as a solvent, wetting agent, or chemical modifier. The treatment may improve properties such as dye uptake, softness, water repellency, or flame resistance, and is applied to a wide range of natural or synthetic textiles not covered by more specific categories.
